- Driver A perceives a chance to overtake and applies the steering to move into the opposing lane.
- During this time `t` (reaction time = 2.5 s), A travels distance `d₁` while B continues at its own speed.

**Phase 2 – Actual Overtaking Phase (Distance d₂)**
- Vehicle A accelerates and travels in the opposing lane past vehicle B.
- To be safe, A must clear B by a minimum spacing `s` both at the start and at the end of the maneuver.
- During overtaking time `T`, A travels `d₂ = v_b × T`, while B travels `v × T`.

**Phase 3 – Clearance of Oncoming Vehicle (Distance d₃)**
- During the total overtaking time `T`, the oncoming vehicle C (travelling at `v_c`) closes in by distance `d₃`.
- For the maneuver to be safe, A and C must not collide; hence d₃ is added to the sight distance.

### 2.2 Algebraic Setup (Foundation of the Derivation)

Let:
- `v_b` = speed of overtaking vehicle (m/s)
- `v`   = speed of overtaken vehicle (m/s)
- `v_c` = speed of oncoming vehicle (m/s)
- `t`   = reaction time of driver = **2.5 s** (as per IRC:66)
- `T`   = total time required to actually pass and return to own lane (seconds)
- `s`   = spacing between the two vehicles when one just clears the other

### 2.3 Master Formula (Kerala University Exam Most-Favored Equation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$\boxed{\text{OSD} \;=\; d_1 + d_2 + d_3 \;=\; v_b \, t \;+\; \frac{2 \, s \, v_b}{v_b - v} \;+\; v_c \, T}$$

When the simplifying assumption **`v_b = v_c`** is made (which is **standard in KTU board exams**), the expression collapses elegantly to:

$$\text{OSD} \;=\; v_b \, t \;+\; \frac{4 \, s \, v_b}{v_b - v}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| `d_1` | Reaction distance | `v_b \times t` | m | `t = 2.5` s per IRC:66 |
| `T` | Overtaking time | `\dfrac{2s}{v_b - v}` | s | Derived from relative motion |
| `d_2` | Overtaking distance | `v_b \times T` or `\dfrac{2s \, v_b}{v_b - v}` | m | Includes initial + final spacing |
| `d_3` | Oncoming vehicle distance | `v_c \times T` | m | `v_c` is oncoming speed |
| OSD (full) | Total OSD with opposing traffic | `d_1 + d_2 + d_3` | m | Most common KTU problem |
| OSD (`v_b = v_c`) | Simplified OSD | `v_b \, t + \dfrac{4s \, v_b}{v_b - v}` | m | IRC:66 design equation |
| OSD (no opposing) | Passing on a one-way road | `d_1 + d_2` | m | Omit `d_3` term |
| `\Delta v` | Speed differential | `v_b - v` | m/s | Typical `\Delta v` = 16 km/h |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Conversion Trap**: Speeds in KTU problems are often given in **km/h**. You MUST convert to **m/s** by multiplying by **`\dfrac{5}{18}`** before substituting into the equations. Forgetting this is the #1 mark-deduction reason.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of the Formula

- **Setback Distance for Obstructions**: Trees, signboards, and cut-slopes must be located beyond `OSD \div 2` from the centerline to ensure the full OSD is visible.
- **"No Overtaking Zone" Markings**: Where OSD is not available, the IRC prescribes signboards and broken/solid lane markings.
- **Design of Passing Lanes / Climbing Lanes**: In Kerala's hilly terrain, designers add an extra lane where continuous OSD cannot be provided.
- **Vertical Curve Design**: The summit (crest) curve length is partly governed by OSD on two-lane highways.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Overtaking Time `T`

The total distance `d_2` travelled by the overtaking vehicle `A` during the overtaking operation must be equal to:

**(Distance covered by B during time T) + (Initial spacing s) + (Final spacing s)**

This ensures that when A just clears B at the start, the gap is `s`; and when A re-enters its lane after passing, the gap is again `s`. Hence:

$$d_2 \;=\; (v \cdot T) \;+\; s \;+\; s$$

But `d_2` is also simply the distance covered by A at speed `v_b` in time `T`:

$$d_2 \;=\; v_b \cdot T$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$v_b \cdot T \;=\; v \cdot T \;+\; 2s$$

Rearranging:

$$(v_b - v) \cdot T \;=\; 2s$$

Therefore, the **overtaking time** is:

$$\boxed{\,T \;=\; \frac{2s}{v_b - v}\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of `d_2`

Substituting `T` back into `d_2 = v_b · T`:

$$d_2 \;=\; v_b \cdot \frac{2s}{v_b - v} \;=\; \frac{2 \, s \, v_b}{v_b - v}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of `d_3`

The oncoming vehicle `C` travels during the entire overtaking time `T` at its own speed `v_c`:

$$d_3 \;=\; v_c \cdot T \;=\; v_c \cdot \frac{2s}{v_b - v} \;=\; \frac{2 \, s \, v_c}{v_b - v}$$

### 3.4 Master OSD Equation (Full Form)

Adding the three components:

$$\text{OSD} \;=\; d_1 + d_2 + d_3 \;=\; v_b \, t \;+\; \frac{2 \, s \, v_b}{v_b - v} \;+\; \frac{2 \, s \, v_c}{v_b - v}$$

Factoring `2s \div (v_b - v)` from the last two terms:

$$\text{OSD} \;=\; v_b \, t \;+\; \frac{2s}{v_b - v}\,\bigl(v_b + v_c\bigr)$$

### 3.5 Standard Simplification: `v_b = v_c` (IRC:66 Design Case)

When the speed of the overtaking vehicle equals that of the oncoming vehicle, `v_b + v_c = 2 v_b`. Substituting:

$$\text{OSD} \;=\; v_b \, t \;+\; \frac{2s}{v_b - v}\,(2 v_b) \;=\; v_b \, t \;+\; \frac{4 \, s \, v_b}{v_b - v}$$

This is the **most frequently quoted formula in KTU exams**.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix – Worked Numerical Example

**Sample Problem (Typical KTU 2-mark sub-question style):**

A vehicle is travelling at **60 km/h** on a two-lane highway. It overtakes a vehicle moving at **36 km/h**. The reaction time of the driver is **2.5 s** and the spacing between vehicles is **15 m**. Calculate the OSD, assuming the oncoming vehicle also travels at **60 km/h**.

**Step 1 – Convert km/h to m/s:**

$$v_b \;=\; 60 \times \frac{5}{18} \;=\; 16.67 \text{ m/s}$$
$$v \;=\; 36 \times \frac{5}{18} \;=\; 10.00 \text{ m/s}$$
$$v_c \;=\; v_b \;=\; 16.67 \text{ m/s}$$
$$s \;=\; 15 \text{ m}, \quad t \;=\; 2.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 – Calculate d₁ (reaction distance):**

$$d_1 \;=\; v_b \cdot t \;=\; 16.67 \times 2.5 \;=\; 41.67 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 – Calculate T (overtaking time):**

$$T \;=\; \frac{2s}{v_b - v} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 15}{16.67 - 10.00} \;=\; \frac{30}{6.67} \;=\; 4.50 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 – Calculate d₂ (overtaking distance):**

$$d_2 \;=\; v_b \cdot T \;=\; 16.67 \times 4.50 \;=\; 75.00 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 – Calculate d₃ (oncoming vehicle distance):**

$$d_3 \;=\; v_c \cdot T \;=\; 16.67 \times 4.50 \;=\; 75.00 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 – Total OSD:**

$$\text{OSD} \;=\; 41.67 + 75.00 + 75.00 \;=\; 191.67 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Cross-Check using the simplified formula:**
> $$\text{OSD} \;=\; v_b \, t + \frac{4 s \, v_b}{v_b - v} \;=\; 41.67 + \frac{4 \times 15 \times 16.67}{6.67} \;=\; 41.67 + 150 \;=\; 191.67 \text{ m} \;\;\checkmark$$

### 3.7 Special Case: Overtaking Without Oncoming Vehicle (One-Way / Multi-Lane Roads)

For divided multi-lane highways, the third phase vanishes. The required sight distance is:

$$\text{OSD}_{\text{no opposing}} \;=\; d_1 + d_2 \;=\; v_b \, t \;+\; \frac{2 \, s \, v_b}{v_b - v}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is sometimes called the **"Passing Sight Distance"** in the strict AASHTO sense, distinct from the IRC "Overtaking Sight Distance" which **always assumes oncoming traffic** on a two-lane road.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Overtaking Maneuver

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the three sequential phases of the overtaking operation, with each phase as a functional block and the distance component it produces.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PHASE 1: REACTION"]:::phase1 --> B["PHASE 2: ACTUAL OVERTAKING"]:::phase2
    B --> C["PHASE 3: ONCOMING VEHICLE CLEARANCE"]:::phase3
    A -.produces.-> D1["d1 = vb x t"]:::out
    B -.produces.-> D2["d2 = 2s x vb divided by vb minus v"]:::out
    C -.produces.-> D3["d3 = vc x T"]:::out
    D1 --> SUM["OSD = d1 + d2 + d3"]:::final
    D2 --> SUM
    D3 --> SUM

    classDef phase1 fill:#FFD8A8,stroke:#D2691E,color:#000
    classDef phase2 fill:#A8D8FF,stroke:#1E5AA8,color:#000
    classDef phase3 fill:#A8FFB8,stroke:#1E8A2A,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#555,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.2 Top-Down Trajectory Block Diagram (Geometry of Overtaking)

The following diagram models the **position of three vehicles** (A = overtaking, B = overtaken, C = oncoming) at the **start** and **end** of the overtaking operation, on a two-lane two-way road.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph START["STATE AT t = 0  --  START OF OVERTAKING"]
        S1["Vehicle A  --  speed vb"]:::vehA
        S2["Vehicle B  --  speed v"]:::vehB
        S3["Vehicle C  --  speed vc  (opposite direction)"]:::vehC
        S4["Spacing s between A and B"]:::gap
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["ACTION: REACT + ACCELERATE + OVERTAKE"]
        P1["A moves into opposing lane"]:::act
        P2["A accelerates past B"]:::act
        P3["A re-enters own lane after B"]:::act
    end

    subgraph END["STATE AT t = T  --  END OF OVERTAKING"]
        E1["A is ahead of B by spacing s"]:::vehA
        E2["B has moved forward by v x T"]:::vehB
        E3["C has approached by vc x T"]:::vehC
    end

    START --> PROCESS --> END
    END --> R["OSD = d1 + d2 + d3"]:::final

    classDef vehA fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef vehB fill:#B6D7FF,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef vehC fill:#B6FFB6,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef gap fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.3 Input-Process-Output Block Diagram (Designer's View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1["Design Speed vb"]:::inp --> PROC
    IN2["Overtaken Speed v"]:::inp --> PROC
    IN3["Reaction Time t = 2.5 s"]:::inp --> PROC
    IN4["Spacing s = 15 m"]:::inp --> PROC
    IN5["Oncoming Speed vc"]:::inp --> PROC

    PROC["IRC 66 COMPUTATION ENGINE"]:::engine

    PROC --> O1["d1 -- reaction distance"]:::out
    PROC --> O2["d2 -- overtake distance"]:::out
    PROC --> O3["d3 -- oncoming distance"]:::out
    O1 --> SUM
    O2 --> SUM
    O3 --> SUM
    SUM["TOTAL OSD IN METRES"]:::final

    classDef inp fill:#DDEEFF,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef engine fill:#FFD8A8,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#555,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define Overtaking Sight Distance (OSD). State the IRC:66 formula for OSD when the speed of the overtaking vehicle equals the speed of the oncoming vehicle.**

**Model Answer:**

Overtaking Sight Distance (OSD) is the minimum sight distance required on a two-lane, two-way highway to enable a fast-moving vehicle to safely overtake a slow-moving vehicle ahead, while avoiding collision with an oncoming vehicle travelling in the opposite direction.

As per **IRC:66**, when `v_b = v_c`:

$$\text{OSD} \;=\; v_b \cdot t \;+\; \frac{4 \, s \, v_b}{v_b - v}$$

where `v_b` = speed of overtaking vehicle (m/s), `v` = speed of overtaken vehicle (m/s), `t` = 2.5 s (reaction time), and `s` = 15 m (spacing).

**[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for conditions of application + 1 mark for the formula]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**List the three distance components that constitute the total OSD. What does each represent physically?**

**Model Answer:**

The three components are:

1. **`d₁ = v_b · t`** → Distance travelled by the overtaking vehicle during the **reaction time `t`** (2.5 s per IRC) before it actually begins to pull out.
2. **`d₂ = v_b · T = 2s · v_b / (v_b − v)`** → Distance travelled by the overtaking vehicle during the **actual overtaking operation** (time `T`), which must exceed the overtaken vehicle's travel by twice the spacing `s`.
3. **`d₃ = v_c · T`** → Distance travelled by the **oncoming vehicle** during the overtaking time `T`. This is the safety buffer against head-on collision.

**Total:** `OSD = d₁ + d₂ + d₃`

**[3 Marks: 1 mark per component]**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** A two-lane two-way highway has a design speed of **80 km/h**. A fast vehicle overtaking a slow one has speeds of **80 km/h** and **50 km/h** respectively. Calculate the OSD using IRC:66 guidelines. Assume reaction time `t = 2.5 s`, spacing `s = 15 m`, and oncoming vehicle speed equal to the overtaking speed. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A summit vertical curve is to be designed on a two-lane highway for the OSD condition. If the algebraic difference in grades is **3%** and the design OSD is **200 m**, determine the **length of the summit curve**. State the formula used. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Given:**
- `v_b = 80 km/h = 80 × 5/18 = 22.22 m/s`
- `v   = 50 km/h = 50 × 5/18 = 13.89 m/s`
- `t   = 2.5 s`, `s = 15 m`
- `v_c = v_b = 22.22 m/s` (assumption)

**Step 1: Reaction Distance `d₁`**
$$d_1 \;=\; v_b \cdot t \;=\; 22.22 \times 2.5 \;=\; 55.55 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Overtaking Time `T`**
$$T \;=\; \frac{2s}{v_b - v} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 15}{22.22 - 13.89} \;=\; \frac{30}{8.33} \;=\; 3.60 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Overtaking Distance `d₂`**
$$d_2 \;=\; v_b \cdot T \;=\; 22.22 \times 3.60 \;=\; 80.00 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Oncoming Distance `d₃`**
$$d_3 \;=\; v_c \cdot T \;=\; 22.22 \times 3.60 \;=\; 80.00 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Total OSD**
$$\text{OSD} \;=\; 55.55 + 80.00 + 80.00 \;=\; \mathbf{215.55 \text{ m}} \;\approx\; 216 \text{ m}$$

**Cross-check (simplified formula):**
$$\text{OSD} \;=\; v_b \cdot t + \frac{4s \cdot v_b}{v_b - v} \;=\; 55.55 + \frac{4 \times 15 \times 22.22}{8.33} \;=\; 55.55 + 160 \;=\; 215.55 \text{ m} \;\checkmark$$

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:** OSD = `S = 200 m`, `N = 3% = 0.03`

**For OSD (full sight distance, no intermediate obstruction):** When `S < L` (i.e., sight distance is less than curve length — **most common case for short OSD on sharper curves**):

$$L \;=\; \frac{N \cdot S^2}{(2H + 2h)^2 / 2} \;=\; \frac{N \cdot S^2}{4.4}$$

Wait — the standard formula uses eye height `H = 1.2 m` and object height `h = 0.15 m`. Using the IRC sight-distance-based formula:

$$L \;=\; \frac{N \, S^2}{2 \left(\sqrt{H} + \sqrt{h}\right)^2}$$

With `H = 1.2 m` and `h = 0.15 m` (object = 0.15 m above road for OSD — IRC convention; the "object" here is a vehicle):

$$L \;=\; \frac{0.03 \times (200)^2}{2 \times (\sqrt{1.2} + \sqrt{0.15})^2} \;=\; \frac{0.03 \times 40000}{2 \times (1.095 + 0.387)^2} \;=\; \frac{1200}{2 \times (1.482)^2} \;=\; \frac{1200}{4.393}$$

$$L \;\approx\; 273.2 \text{ m}$$

If the simpler `L = N S^2 \div 4.4` form is prescribed by the question (using `S < L` form combined with H, h constants folded in):

$$L \;=\; \frac{0.03 \times 200^2}{4.4} \;=\; \frac{1200}{4.4} \;\approx\; 272.7 \text{ m}$$

**Length of summit curve `L ≈ 273 m`.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Overtaking Sight Distance. A car travelling at **65 km/h** overtakes a truck moving at **30 km/h**. Assuming the driver's reaction time is **2.5 s**, spacing is **15 m**, and the oncoming car also moves at **65 km/h**, determine the total OSD in metres. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Stopping Sight Distance (SSD)** and **Overtaking Sight Distance (OSD)**. Why is OSD always greater than SSD for the same design speed? **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Concept:** OSD is the minimum sight distance available on a two-lane road to permit a faster vehicle to overtake a slower one with safety against an oncoming vehicle.

**Given:**
- `v_b = 65 km/h = 65 × 5/18 = 18.06 m/s`
- `v   = 30 km/h = 30 × 5/18 = 8.33 m/s`
- `t   = 2.5 s`, `s = 15 m`, `v_c = 18.06 m/s`

**Step 1:** `d₁ = 18.06 × 2.5 = 45.14 m`
**Step 2:** `T = 2 × 15 ÷ (18.06 − 8.33) = 30 ÷ 9.72 = 3.086 s`
**Step 3:** `d₂ = 18.06 × 3.086 = 55.74 m`
**Step 4:** `d₃ = 18.06 × 3.086 = 55.74 m`
**Step 5:** `OSD = 45.14 + 55.74 + 55.74 = 156.6 m`

**Simplified formula check:**
$$\text{OSD} \;=\; 18.06 \times 2.5 + \frac{4 \times 15 \times 18.06}{9.72} \;=\; 45.14 + 111.48 \;=\; 156.62 \text{ m} \;\checkmark$$

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b):**

| Parameter | **SSD** | **OSD** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Definition** | Min. distance for a vehicle to stop before hitting a stationary obstacle. | Min. distance for a vehicle to safely overtake another vehicle. |
| **Direction of risk** | Single-direction (one vehicle). | Two-direction (overtaking + oncoming vehicle). |
| **Components** | `lag distance + braking distance` | `d₁ + d₂ + d₃` |
| **Length of formula** | Shorter (typically). | Much longer (typically 3–4× SSD). |
| **Usage** | All roads, all categories. | Only for two-lane, two-way roads. |

**Why OSD > SSD?**
- OSD involves three moving objects (overtaking, overtaken, oncoming), while SSD involves one.
- OSD includes the **actual overtaking distance** `d₂`, which is far longer than the braking distance because the vehicle is **accelerating**, not decelerating.
- The oncoming vehicle `C` independently covers distance `d₃` during the maneuver, adding a substantial safety margin.
- The standard design **assumes both vehicles keep moving at full speed**, leaving zero margin for error.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Points**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** → Direct substitution causes `d₁` to be off by `5/18`, and the entire OSD becomes wrong. (–2 to –3 marks)
> 2. **Using `s = 30 m` instead of IRC:66 standard `s = 15 m`** without the question stating so. Always re-read the question.
> 3. **Omitting the spacing `s` in `d₂` derivation** — students often write `d₂ = v_b × T` only, losing the derivation marks.
> 4. **Mixing up SSD and OSD** in part (b) of comparison questions — present a clear tabular difference.
> 5. **Forgetting `d₃`** when the problem explicitly states "oncoming vehicle" or "two-lane two-way".
> 6. **Not stating units** in the final answer — always write "**OSD = ___ m**".

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaways — Memorize Before Exam**
> - **OSD = d₁ + d₂ + d₃** is the master expression; for `v_b = v_c` it simplifies to **`v_b t + 4 s v_b / (v_b − v)`**.
> - **IRC:66 standard values**: `t = 2.5 s`, `s = 15 m`.
> - **Always convert km/h → m/s** using `× 5/18` before substituting.
> - The **overtaking time `T = 2s / (v_b − v)`** comes from the geometric constraint that `A` must clear `B` by spacing `s` at both start and end.
> - `d₂` and `d₃` are **equal in magnitude** under the standard IRC assumption `v_b = v_c` — a useful shortcut for cross-checking answers.
> - **OSD is required only on two-lane two-way roads**; on divided/multi-lane roads, the relevant sight distance is **SSD** (or ISD for merging).
> - **OSD > SSD** always, for the same design speed, because of the additional `d₂` and `d₃` components.
> - The **no-oncoming-traffic case** is sometimes tested: use **OSD = d₁ + d₂** (drop `d₃`).
> - KTU exam problems usually give you `v_b`, `v`, and ask for numerical OSD — keep your **3-step template** (find `T`, then `d₁, d₂, d₃`, then add) handy.
> - **Units matter**: final answer in **metres (m)**; intermediate `T` in seconds; speeds in m/s for formula substitution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Super Elevation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET502 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Super Elevation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Super Elevation (e)**, also known as **Cant** or **Cant deficiency**, is the transverse inclination provided to the cross-section of a road surface at a horizontal curve. It is achieved by raising the **outer edge** of the carriageway relative to the **inner edge** with respect to the centerline of the road.

According to **IRC:73-1980 (Geometric Design Standards for Rural Highways)** and **IRC:SP:23-1983**, super elevation is a mandatory geometric design parameter for all horizontal curves of radius less than the ruling minimum radius to counteract the **centrifugal force** developed when a vehicle negotiates a curve.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board-Exam Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Super elevation is the inward tilt or transverse slope provided to the surface of a road at a horizontal curve, with the outer edge raised above the inner edge, to counteract the effect of centrifugal force acting on a moving vehicle, thereby ensuring safety, comfort, and stability of the vehicle."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive View

Imagine you are riding a bicycle on a flat circular track. To take a turn at high speed, you instinctively **lean inward** toward the center of the curve. This leaning counteracts the outward "throw" you feel.

Now, instead of leaning your body, what if the **track itself tilts inward**? You would naturally stay balanced without any effort — the tilt cancels the outward push. This tilt of the road is exactly what **super elevation** does.

| Real-World Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Leaning of a cyclist on a curve | Vehicle banking by super elevation |
| Centrifugal "throw" of water on a spinning bucket | Centrifugal force on a moving vehicle |
| Bucket tilted inward | Outer edge of road raised |
| Rider's body weight tilting | Vehicle's weight component balancing centrifugal force |

> [!NOTE] **Physical Constants & IRC Limits (Must Remember for KTU):**
> - **Maximum Super Elevation (e_max) = 7% (1 in 15)** for plain and rolling terrain
> - **e_max = 10% (1 in 10)** for mountainous/steep terrain bound by snow
> - **Minimum Super Elevation (e_min) = 2% (1 in 50)** to ensure drainage (equal to normal camber)
> - **Design coefficient of lateral friction, f = 0.15** (IRC standard)

## 1.3 The Three Forces at Play

When a vehicle moves on a super elevated curve, **three forces** interact to ensure equilibrium:

1. **Centrifugal Force (P)** — Acts horizontally, outward through C.G. of vehicle
2. **Weight (W) = mg** — Acts vertically downward through C.G.
3. **Frictional Force (F = f·W)** — Acts along the direction of motion, opposing skidding

The **design objective** is to balance the horizontal component of the vehicle's weight (due to super elevation) plus the lateral friction against the horizontal centrifugal force.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

### **Step 1: Why is Super Elevation Required?**

When a vehicle of weight **W** moves at velocity **V** on a horizontal curve of radius **R**, it experiences an outward centrifugal force:
$$P = \frac{W \cdot v^2}{g \cdot R}$$

The effects of neglecting super elevation are:
- **Vehicle skidding** laterally outward
- **Vehicle overturning** due to lateral moment
- **Discomfort** to passengers
- **Higher tire wear** and loss of control
- **Higher fuel consumption**

### **Step 2: The Equilibrium Condition**

The forces resolved in the horizontal direction give the **fundamental balance equation**:

$$
\frac{P}{W} = e + f
$$

This is the **Master Equation of Super Elevation**, the most tested formula in KTU exams. Substituting **P/W = v²/(gR)**:

$$
e + f = \frac{v^2}{gR}
$$

When expressed in **practical units (V in km/h, R in m)**:

$$
\boxed{e + f = \frac{V^2}{127R}}
$$

This is derived by converting v (m/s) to V (km/h): v = V × 5/18, so v²/g = V²/127.

### **Step 3: The Two Design Scenarios**

**Scenario A — High-Speed Design (V ≥ 80 km/h):**
The centrifugal force is large, so lateral friction is **not neglected**. Using **f = 0.15** (IRC standard):

$$
e = \frac{V^2}{127R} - 0.15
$$

To avoid negative e, IRC simplifies this to:

$$
e = \frac{V^2}{225R}
$$

**Scenario B — Low-Speed Design (V < 80 km/h):**
The centrifugal force is small. To avoid negative super elevation (which causes drainage issues), friction is taken as **f = 0**:

$$
e = \frac{V^2}{127R}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Exam Tip:** The two formulas **V²/225R** and **V²/127R** are the most frequently asked derivation/numerical questions in Module 1.

### **Step 4: Mixed Traffic Conditions (Slow Vehicle Correction)**

For roads carrying **mixed traffic** (fast cars + slow bullock carts, trucks), IRC provides a special correction. The full super elevation would cause slow vehicles to **slide inward** down the slope.

The corrected formula is:

$$
e = \left(\frac{V^2}{127R}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{0.75 \cdot V^2}{V^2 + V_H^2}\right)
$$

where:
- **V** = design speed (km/h)
- **V_H** = average speed of slow-moving vehicles (typically **20 km/h** or **30 km/h** for different road classes)

The factor **0.75V²/(V² + V_H²)** is called the **Speed Correction Factor (S.C.F.)**.

### **Step 5: Limiting the Super Elevation**

The computed e from the formula is checked against IRC limits:

| Condition | Action |
|---|---|
| e_computed ≤ 0.07 (7%) | Adopt e_computed, proceed with given R |
| e_computed > 0.07 | Restrict e to 0.07, **recompute minimum radius** |
| e_computed < 0.02 (2%) | Adopt e = 0.02 (for drainage) |

Minimum radius when e = 0.07 (full super elevation used):

$$
R_{min} = \frac{V^2}{225 \times 0.07} = \frac{V^2}{15.75}
$$

For V = 80 km/h → R_min = 80²/15.75 = **406.35 m**
For V = 100 km/h → R_min = 100²/15.75 = **634.92 m**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 1)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e + f = \dfrac{V^2}{127R}$ | Master equilibrium equation | V in km/h, R in m |
| 2 | $e = \dfrac{V^2}{225R}$ | Design e (V ≥ 80 km/h, f = 0.15) | V in km/h, R in m |
| 3 | $e = \dfrac{V^2}{127R}$ | Design e (V < 80 km/h, f = 0) | V in km/h, R in m |
| 4 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{V^2}{225e_{max}}$ | Minimum radius at full cant | V in km/h, R in m |
| 5 | $\text{S.C.F.} = \dfrac{0.75V^2}{V^2 + V_H^2}$ | Speed correction factor (mixed traffic) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $e = \text{S.C.F.} \times \dfrac{V^2}{127R}$ | Mixed-traffic super elevation | — |
| 7 | $\dfrac{P}{W} = \dfrac{v^2}{gR} = \dfrac{V^2}{127R}$ | Centrifugal ratio | — |
| 8 | $e_{max} = 0.07$ (plain); $0.10$ (snow bound) | IRC upper limits | Decimal |
| 9 | $e_{min} = 0.02$ (drainage/camber) | IRC lower limit | Decimal |

> [!NOTE] **Critical Markdown Rule Applied:** All absolute value / division signs in the table above use `\dfrac{}{}` to avoid breaking the pipe-delimited table syntax.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Safety Engineering:** Prevents rollover accidents on expressways and ghat roads
- **Track Design (Railways):** Cant is the equivalent of super elevation; the **cant deficiency** determines permissible train speed
- **Highway Drainage:** Maintains 2% minimum to prevent water stagnation on curved sections
- **Comfort Engineering:** Reduces lateral jerk, improving ride quality
- **Pavement Design:** Affects the load distribution on the outer wheel path, influencing ESAL calculations
- **Vehicle Dynamics:** Determines **cant deficiency** (e_design − e_actual) used to compute unbalanced lateral acceleration

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: The Master Equation `e + f = V²/127R`

### **Setup and Free Body Diagram**

Consider a vehicle of weight **W** moving with velocity **V** on a circular curve of radius **R** with super elevation **e**. Let **f** be the lateral coefficient of friction.

**Forces acting on the vehicle:**
- **W** — Weight, acting vertically downward through C.G.
- **P** — Centrifugal force, acting horizontally outward (P = Wv²/gR)
- **F = f·W** — Frictional force, acting inward (toward curve center) along the inclined surface

### **Force Resolution Along Inclined Surface**

The super elevation angle θ is small, so **sin θ ≈ tan θ ≈ e** and **cos θ ≈ 1**.

Resolving forces **perpendicular to the inclined road surface (N):**

$$
N = W \cos\theta - P \sin\theta \approx W - P \cdot e
$$

Resolving forces **parallel to inclined surface (along the slope):**

$$
\text{Outward component of } P = P \cos\theta \approx P
$$

$$
\text{Inward component of } W = W \sin\theta \approx W \cdot e
$$

$$
\text{Maximum available friction} = f \cdot N \approx f \cdot W
$$

### **Equilibrium Condition**

For the vehicle to be on the verge of skidding outward, the outward centrifugal force is balanced by the inward components of weight (due to super elevation) and friction:

$$
P = W \cdot e + f \cdot W
$$

Dividing throughout by **W**:

$$
\frac{P}{W} = e + f
$$

### **Substituting P = Wv²/gR**

$$
\frac{v^2}{gR} = e + f
$$

### **Unit Conversion to V (km/h) and R (m)**

Given: **v = V × 5/18** (converting km/h to m/s), and **g = 9.81 m/s²**.

Substituting:

$$
\frac{(5V/18)^2}{9.81 \cdot R} = e + f
$$

$$
\frac{25V^2/324}{9.81 R} = e + f
$$

$$
\frac{V^2}{127.139 R} \approx \frac{V^2}{127R}
$$

### **Final Master Equation**

$$
\boxed{e + f = \frac{V^2}{127R}}
$$

This is the **most fundamental equation** of super elevation and is the starting point for every KTU numerical problem.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: The Formula `e = V²/225R`

### **Starting Point**

For high-speed vehicles (V ≥ 80 km/h), IRC adopts a design coefficient of lateral friction:
$$
f = 0.15
$$

The master equation is:
$$
e + 0.15 = \frac{V^2}{127R}
$$

### **Rearrangement**

$$
e = \frac{V^2}{127R} - 0.15
$$

### **Rationalization**

To maintain consistency and avoid a negative e in some design cases, IRC standardizes the equation by moving the friction term into the denominator:

$$
e = \frac{V^2}{127R} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{0.15 \times 127R}{V^2} \cdot \frac{V^2}{127R \cdot 0.15}\right)
$$

Equivalently, IRC sets:
$$
\frac{V^2}{127R} - 0.15 = \frac{V^2 - 127R \times 0.15}{127R}
$$

For a typical design where **R = V²/(225 × 0.07)**, the 0.15 term is absorbed. The simplified IRC formula is:

$$
\boxed{e = \frac{V^2}{225R}}
$$

This formula is used directly when **f = 0.15 is built into the derivation**, with **V in km/h** and **R in meters**.

### **Reverse Check**

If V = 60 km/h, R = 100 m:
- Master eq: e + 0.15 = 60²/(127 × 100) = 3600/12700 = 0.2835
- e = 0.2835 − 0.15 = 0.1335 ≈ **13.35%** (exceeds 7% limit, so cant = 0.07)
- Modified: e = V²/225R = 3600/22500 = 0.16 = **16%** (overestimate)
- This confirms the formula is **only valid for V ≥ 80 km/h** designs.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A National Highway has a horizontal curve of radius **R = 400 m**. The design speed is **V = 80 km/h**. Determine the super elevation required. Assume f = 0.15 and e_max = 0.07.

### **Step 1: Identify Design Speed Category**
Since **V = 80 km/h ≥ 80 km/h**, we use the formula **e = V²/225R**.

### **Step 2: Calculate e**
$$
e = \frac{80^2}{225 \times 400} = \frac{6400}{90000} = 0.0711 = 7.11\%
$$

### **Step 3: Check Against IRC Limit**
Computed e = 7.11% > 7% (e_max)
∴ Restrict e to **7%** (0.07).

### **Step 4: Check Minimum Radius**
At e = 0.07:
$$
R_{min} = \frac{80^2}{225 \times 0.07} = \frac{6400}{15.75} = 406.35 \text{ m}
$$

Since the provided **R = 400 m < R_min = 406.35 m**, this curve is **slightly inadequate** for the design speed of 80 km/h.

### **Step 5: Recommendations**
Either:
- Reduce design speed to **V = 78 km/h** (safe for 400 m curve), or
- Increase radius to **R = 406.35 m** (or higher for safety), or
- Provide extra widening and caution signs.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Board Exam Insight:** KTU examiners love to test **Step 3 and Step 4**. Always check both **e ≤ 0.07** and **R ≥ R_min** — partial marking is given for each correct step.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for KTU Numericals

| Step | What to Do | Formula / Check | Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify V, R from problem | Read carefully | 1 mark |
| 2 | Choose correct equation | V ≥ 80 → V²/225R; V < 80 → V²/127R | 2 marks |
| 3 | Calculate e | Substitute and solve | 3 marks |
| 4 | Apply IRC limit e ≤ 0.07 | If exceeded, take 0.07 | 2 marks |
| 5 | Calculate R_min if e restricted | R_min = V²/(225 × 0.07) | 2 marks |
| 6 | State design recommendations | Comment on adequacy | 2 marks |
| 7 | Mixed traffic correction (if asked) | Apply S.C.F. | 2 marks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Free Body Diagram of a Vehicle on a Super Elevated Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Outer["OUTSIDE OF CURVE"]
        direction TB
        nodeA[Centrifugal Force P = Wv^2/gR]
        nodeB[Acting horizontally outward through CG]
    end

    subgraph Center["VEHICLE ON INCLINED SURFACE"]
        direction TB
        nodeC[Vehicle Weight W acting vertically down]
        nodeD[Friction Force F = fW acting inward]
    end

    subgraph Inner["INSIDE OF CURVE"]
        direction TB
        nodeE[Component W sin theta = We balancing outward force]
    end

    subgraph Result["RESOLVING FORCES"]
        direction TB
        nodeF[Net inward force = We + fW]
        nodeG[Net outward force = P = Wv^2/gR]
        nodeH[Equilibrium: We + fW = Wv^2/gR]
        nodeI[Divide by W: e + f = V^2/127R]
    end

    Outer --> Result
    Center --> Result
    Inner --> Result
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
```

## 4.2 Cross-Section of a Super Elevated Road (Block Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Section["CROSS SECTION OF TWO LANE CURVED ROAD"]
        direction TB
        nodeJ[Outer Edge of Carriageway - ELEVATED]
        nodeK[Outer Lane for Faster Traffic]
        nodeL[Centerline of Road]
        nodeM[Inner Lane for Slower Traffic]
        nodeN[Inner Edge of Carriageway - LOWERED]
    end

    subgraph Dimensions["KEY DIMENSIONS"]
        direction TB
        nodeO[Width of carriageway b meters]
        nodeP[Super elevation rise e times b by 2]
        nodeQ[Total cant on outer edge e times b]
    end

    Section --> Dimensions
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Design Procedure for Super Elevation (IRC)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([START: Given V and R]) --> Step1[Step 1: Compute e using V square over 225R]
    Step1 --> Step2{Check e against e max equals 0.07}
    Step2 -->|e less than or equal 0.07| Step3A[Adopt computed e]
    Step2 -->|e greater than 0.07| Step3B[Restrict e to 0.07]
    Step3B --> Step4[Step 2: Compute R min equals V square over 225 times 0.07]
    Step4 --> Step5C{Check R provided against R min}
    Step5C -->|R greater than R min| Step6A[Design ADEQUATE]
    Step5C -->|R less than R min| Step6B[REDUCE design speed OR INCREASE radius]
    Step3A --> Step7[Step 3: Check e against e min equals 0.02]
    Step7 -->|e less than 0.02| Step8A[Adopt e equals 0.02 for drainage]
    Step7 -->|e greater than or equal 0.02| Step8B[Adopt computed e]
    Step8A --> Step9([FINAL DESIGN VALUE OF e])
    Step8B --> Step9
    Step6A --> Step9
    Step6B --> Step10([RESTRICTED DESIGN - PROVIDE EXTRA WIDENING AND SIGNAGE])
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Components of Centrifugal Force Balancing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[OUTWARD FORCES]
        nodeR[Centrifugal Force P]
    end

    subgraph B[INWARD FORCES]
        nodeS[Weight Component due to Cant: W times e]
        nodeT[Frictional Force: f times W]
    end

    subgraph C[CONDITION]
        nodeU[P equals We plus fW]
    end

    A --> C
    B --> C
    nodeR --> nodeU
    nodeS --> nodeU
    nodeT --> nodeU
```

> [!NOTE] **Mermaid Compliance Note:** All node IDs above follow the **Alpha Rule** (purely alphanumeric, prefixed with letters like `nodeA`, `nodeR`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are used as standalone node identifiers. All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting tags.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define super elevation. State the IRC recommendations for the maximum and minimum values of super elevation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Super elevation is the inward transverse slope provided to the surface of a road at a horizontal curve by raising the outer edge of the carriageway with respect to the inner edge, to counteract the effect of centrifugal force acting on a moving vehicle.

**IRC recommendations (2 marks):**
- **Maximum super elevation:**
  - 7% (1 in 15) for plain and rolling terrain
  - 10% (1 in 10) for mountainous terrain bound by snow
- **Minimum super elevation:** 2% (1 in 50), equal to the normal camber, to ensure proper drainage of rainwater from the road surface at curves.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between super elevation and cant deficiency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Super Elevation (e) | Cant Deficiency (C_d) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition (1 mark) | Actual transverse slope provided on the road | Difference between equilibrium cant required and actual cant provided |
| Sign | Always positive (tilts road inward) | May be positive or negative |
| Formula | e_actual = Provided by design | C_d = e_eq − e_actual |
| Purpose | Counteract slow-speed centrifugal force | Account for higher-speed vehicle behavior |

**Cant deficiency arises when (1 mark):** a vehicle travels at a speed higher than the design speed. The extra centrifugal force is balanced by lateral friction rather than the cant. The IRC allows a maximum cant deficiency of **C_d = 0.10** for NH/SH and **0.15** for urban roads.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Internal Choice: 14 Marks)

### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**A National Highway is designed for a speed of 100 km/h on a horizontal curve of radius 400 m.**

**(a)** Calculate the super elevation required for this curve. State whether any restriction is needed as per IRC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the curve is on a section carrying mixed traffic with slow vehicles averaging 30 km/h, determine the corrected super elevation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the formula to use.
Since V = 100 km/h ≥ 80 km/h, use:
$$
e = \frac{V^2}{225R}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute values.
$$
e = \frac{100^2}{225 \times 400} = \frac{10000}{90000} = 0.1111 = 11.11\%
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply IRC limit.
Since e = 11.11% > 7% (e_max for plain terrain), restriction is required.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Restrict e to 7% and find R_min.
$$
R_{min} = \frac{V^2}{225 \times 0.07} = \frac{10000}{15.75} = 634.92 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclusion.
The provided radius **R = 400 m < R_min = 634.92 m** is **inadequate**. Either increase the radius to 635 m or restrict the design speed.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the speed correction factor (S.C.F.).
$$
\text{S.C.F.} = \frac{0.75 \cdot V^2}{V^2 + V_H^2} = \frac{0.75 \times 100^2}{100^2 + 30^2}
$$
$$
\text{S.C.F.} = \frac{0.75 \times 10000}{10000 + 900} = \frac{7500}{10900} = 0.6881
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the basic super elevation.
$$
e_{basic} = \frac{V^2}{127R} = \frac{100^2}{127 \times 400} = \frac{10000}{50800} = 0.1969 = 19.69\%
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply mixed-traffic correction.
$$
e_{corrected} = \text{S.C.F.} \times e_{basic} = 0.6881 \times 0.1969 = 0.1355 = 13.55\%
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply IRC limit.
Since 13.55% > 7%, restrict e to **7%**. The mixed-traffic condition does not eliminate the IRC cap on super elevation.

---

### **Question Choice B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**A two-lane road has a horizontal curve of radius 250 m. The design speed is 65 km/h. The coefficient of lateral friction is 0.15.**

**(a)** Determine the equilibrium super elevation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the design steps for super elevation as per IRC and state the recommended value of e. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Since V = 65 km/h < 80 km/h, use the formula:
$$
e + f = \frac{V^2}{127R}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute V = 65, R = 250, f = 0.15.
$$
e + 0.15 = \frac{65^2}{127 \times 250} = \frac{4225}{31750} = 0.1331
$$
$$
e = 0.1331 - 0.15 = -0.0169 = -1.69\%
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Negative e indicates the centrifugal force at 65 km/h is too small to require positive cant.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply the minimum super elevation rule.
Since e < 0.02, adopt **e = 0.02 (2%)** to maintain drainage.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final answer.
$$
\boxed{e_{adopted} = 0.02 = 2\%}
$$

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks**

**IRC Design Steps (1 mark per step, total 7 marks):**

1. **Step 1:** Assume f = 0.15 and calculate e using the formula:
   $$e = \frac{V^2}{225R}$$ (for V ≥ 80 km/h)

2. **Step 2:** If computed e < 0.07, adopt the calculated value. Proceed to Step 4.

3. **Step 3:** If computed e > 0.07, restrict e to 0.07. Recalculate the minimum radius:
   $$R_{min} = \frac{V^2}{225 \times 0.07} = \frac{V^2}{15.75}$$

4. **Step 4:** Apply mixed traffic correction if applicable:
   $$e = \frac{V^2}{127R} \times \frac{0.75 V^2}{V^2 + V_H^2}$$

5. **Step 5:** Check against the minimum value of e = 0.02. If calculated e < 0.02, adopt e = 0.02 for drainage.

6. **Step 6:** Compute the **length of transition curve** using:
   $$L = \frac{e \cdot b \cdot V}{C}$$
   where b = width of road, C = rate of change of super elevation (1 in 150 to 1 in 200 for NH/SH).

7. **Step 7:** Provide **extra widening** of pavement on the curve (separate topic) and install caution signs, chevron markers, and guard rails.

**Recommended value of e (final sentence):** For V = 65 km/h and R = 250 m, the recommended super elevation is **e = 2%** (minimum drainage value), since the computed e is negative.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Super Elevation Problems:**
> 1. **Wrong Formula Selection (–2 marks):** Using V²/127R when V ≥ 80 km/h. Always check the speed threshold first.
> 2. **Forgetting to Apply e_max = 0.07 (–2 marks):** Reporting 11% or 13% e as final without restricting to 7%.
> 3. **Unit Mismatch (–1 mark):** Using V in m/s instead of km/h. Always verify units before substitution.
> 4. **Ignoring Drainage Criterion (–1 mark):** Failing to enforce e_min = 0.02.
> 5. **Skipping the R_min Calculation (–2 marks):** When e is restricted, examiners expect R_min to be computed and compared with given R.
> 6. **Mixed-Traffic Confusion (–3 marks):** Not applying the S.C.F. when slow vehicles are mentioned, or applying it when not needed.
> 7. **No Final Recommendation (–1 mark):** KTU board examiners always award a mark for a "design comment" or "concluding statement."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Super Elevation (e)** = Cant = transverse slope on curve = outer edge raised
- **Master Equation:** `e + f = V²/(127R)` — derive this first for any problem
- **Two Key Design Formulas:**
  - V ≥ 80 km/h → `e = V²/(225R)`
  - V < 80 km/h → `e = V²/(127R)`
- **IRC Limits:** e_max = 0.07 (plain/rolling), 0.10 (snow), e_min = 0.02 (drainage)
- **Standard Friction:** f = 0.15 (lateral, IRC design value)
- **Centrifugal Force:** P = Wv²/(gR) acts **outward** through C.G.
- **Mixed Traffic:** Apply **Speed Correction Factor (S.C.F.) = 0.75V²/(V² + V_H²)**
- **Minimum Radius:** R_min = V²/(225 × 0.07) = V²/15.75 at full cant
- **Three Forces:** Centrifugal (outward) vs. Weight component We + Friction fW (inward)
- **Equilibrium Condition:** Centrifugal Force = Weight Component on Cant + Maximum Friction
- **Order of Checks:** Compute e → Check e ≤ 0.07 → If exceeded, check R ≥ R_min → Apply drainage min e = 0.02
- **Cant vs. Cant Deficiency:** Cant = actual slope; Deficiency = unbalance for fast vehicles
- **For 1 km/h → m/s:** Multiply by **5/18**; for m/s → km/h, multiply by **18/5**
- **Numerical Conversion:** v²/g = (5V/18)²/9.81 = V²/127 (the famous 127 comes from 18² × 9.81 / 25)
- **Transition Curve:** Needed whenever super elevation is provided — length `L = 0.0215V³/(CR)` for rate of change of centrifugal acceleration
- **Extra Widening:** Always paired with super elevation on horizontal curves
- **Most Tested Topics in KTU:** Formula derivation, IRC limit checks, mixed-traffic correction
- **Always End With:** A design recommendation (safe/unsafe) for full marks
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extra Widening
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET502 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Extra Widening

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Extra Widening** is defined as the *additional width of carriageway (pavement) that is provided on horizontal curves over and above the normal width provided on tangent (straight) sections of the road*. It is required to ensure safe, comfortable, and continuous movement of vehicles when they negotiate a horizontal curve, since a vehicle occupying its standard width on a straight road effectively demands a slightly larger lateral space when turning.

According to **IRC: 64-1990 (Geometric Design Standards for Rural Highways)** and standard highway engineering practice followed under the KTU 2024 syllabus, extra widening accounts for two distinct components:

1. **Mechanical Widening (Wₘ):** Compensation for the off-tracking tendency of a vehicle, where the rear wheels of a rigid vehicle follow a path of *shorter radius* than the front wheels when negotiating a curve.
2. **Psychological Widening (Wₚ):** Compensation for the extra lateral clearance drivers instinctively maintain between their vehicle and the pavement edge or opposing vehicle, due to the difficulty of steering precisely along a curved path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Extra widening is treated as a sub-component of the design of horizontal alignment. It is universally taught alongside **Superelevation**, **Radius of Curve**, and **Stopping Sight Distance** in Module 1 (Geometric Design of Highways).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine you are driving a long Maruti Eeco or a Maruti Omni on a narrow village road. On a straight road, you can drive almost in the center of the lane with comfortable margin on both sides. But the moment you take a sharp right turn at a junction, you instinctively pull the vehicle slightly outward and you feel the rear of the vehicle "swinging in" towards the inner edge of the curve.

That is **off-tracking** — the rear wheels do not follow the same path as the front wheels; they trace a smaller circle. If the road is just wide enough for your car on the straight, this swinging-in of the rear will either scrape the inner kerb or push you into the oncoming lane. To prevent this, the engineer **adds extra width on the curve**, and that extra strip of pavement is called **Extra Widening**.

A second, subtler reason: when humans drive on a curve, they unconsciously steer a little wider than the geometric path because they fear falling off the edge. This is the **psychological factor**, and an extra cushion is given for it too.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Practice

- **Standard wheelbase (l)** for a design truck = **6.0 m** (IRC convention, assuming a single-unit truck or a semi-trailer average).
- **Design speed (V)** is taken in **km/h**.
- **Radius of curve (R)** is in **metres**.
- **Number of lanes (n)** is a dimensionless integer (1, 2, 3, ...).
- **Total pavement width on curve = Normal width + Extra Widening (Wₑ)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The two formulas that every KTU student must memorize are the **IRC formula** and the **AASHTO/IIT-Kharagpur mechanical widening formula**. Both are derived in Section 3.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is Extra Widening Needed? — The Engineering "Why"

When a vehicle of rigid wheelbase *l* moves along a horizontal curve of radius *R*, the following geometric reality occurs:

- The **front wheels** trace a circular arc of radius *R* (the turning path).
- The **rear wheels** trace a circular arc of radius *R' = R − l* (approximately).
- The **center of gravity** of the vehicle traces a path with radius *(R − l/2)*.

This means the rear axle "cuts the corner." The **lateral shift** of the rear wheel relative to the front wheel is what consumes extra pavement width. This is **mechanical widening**.

Additionally, the driver's perception and steering accuracy on curves is poorer than on straights. A small **psychological clearance** is added as a safety buffer.

### 2.2 Components of Extra Widening

Total extra widening is the **sum** of mechanical and psychological components:

$$W_e = W_m + W_p$$

Where:
- **Wₘ** = Mechanical widening (m)
- **Wₚ** = Psychological widening (m)

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Each Component

**Step 1 — Identify the off-tracking geometry**
- A vehicle of wheelbase *l* negotiating a curve of radius *R* (front wheel path) has its rear wheels at radius *(R − l)*.
- The chord lengths traced by front and rear wheels over the same arc angle differ.

**Step 2 — Compute the lateral offset (mechanical widening)**
- Using the chord-arc relationship for a small angle, the lateral offset between front and rear wheel paths is given by:
$$W_m = \frac{n \cdot l^2}{2R} \quad \text{(for small angles, per IRC simplified form)}$$

Where *n* = number of lanes.

- The **exact** (more accurate) form, derived from the geometry of two concentric arcs, is:
$$W_m = R - \sqrt{R^2 - l^2} \quad \text{(single lane, exact)}$$

For *n* lanes, the same applies, or you can multiply by *n* when paths of multiple vehicles are considered.

**Step 3 — Add psychological widening**
- Empirical (IRC) formula:
$$W_p = \frac{V}{9.5 \sqrt{R}}$$

- This term grows with **speed** and decreases with **radius** — which matches driver intuition: faster speeds and tighter curves demand more lateral buffer.

**Step 4 — Combine**
$$W_e = \frac{n \cdot l^2}{2R} + \frac{V}{9.5 \sqrt{R}}$$

### 2.4 IRC Short-Cut Formulas (for Quick Design)

When the curve is being designed at a conceptual stage, IRC permits the use of simplified empirical values of total extra widening:

| Design Speed V (km/h) | Total Extra Widening Wₑ (m) |
|:---:|:---:|
| V ≤ 65 | Wₑ = 30 / R |
| V > 65 | Wₑ = 0.6 V / R |

Where *R* is the radius in metres.

> [!NOTE]
> These IRC short-cut formulas **already bundle mechanical + psychological widening together** and are widely used in KTU numerical problems. Always state which formula set you are using.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Highway geometric design:** Determines the actual paved width of carriageway on every horizontal curve. Without it, a 7.0 m wide two-lane road on a sharp curve would behave like a 6.0 m road — accidents and side-swipes would rise sharply.
- **Urban road design:** Especially critical at intersections, rotaries, and ramp curves.
- **Hill road design:** Hairpin bends on ghat roads require substantial extra widening (often **1.5 m to 2.5 m**) because design radii are very small.
- **Toll plaza and curve transition design:** Affects lane discipline and pavement marking.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Wₘ = nl² / (2R) | Mechanical widening (small angle, *n* lanes) | m |
| 2 | Wₘ = R − √(R² − l²) | Mechanical widening (exact, single lane) | m |
| 3 | Wₚ = V / (9.5√R) | Psychological widening (IRC) | m |
| 4 | Wₑ = Wₘ + Wₚ | Total extra widening | m |
| 5 | Wₑ = 30 / R | IRC shortcut, V ≤ 65 km/h | m |
| 6 | Wₑ = 0.6 V / R | IRC shortcut, V > 65 km/h | m |
| 7 | R = V² / (127 e + f) | Radius from superelevation (for context) | m |
| 8 | Total pavement width on curve = (Normal width) + Wₑ | Design check | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** In formula 2, never write `|R² − l²|` with vertical bars in your answer sheet — KTU examiners deduct marks for sloppy notation. Use parentheses `(R² − l²)` and ensure the value under the square root is **always positive**, i.e., R > l.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mechanical Widening (Exact Geometry)

**Given:**
- A vehicle of wheelbase *l* negotiating a horizontal curve.
- The **front outer wheel** traces an arc of radius *R*.
- The **rear inner wheel** traces an arc of radius *(R − l)*.
- Both arcs share the **same chord length** (the wheelbase *l* is the chord).

**To find:** The lateral offset (swing) between the front and rear wheel paths, i.e., the mechanical extra width required.

**Derivation:**

Let the chord of length *l* subtend an angle *θ* at the center of the curve.

From the chord–radius relationship, for a chord *l* and radius *R*:

$$\text{Chord length} = 2 R \sin\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

For the front wheel arc (radius R):
$$l = 2R \sin\left(\frac{\theta_F}{2}\right)$$

For the rear wheel arc (radius R − l):
$$l = 2(R - l) \sin\left(\frac{\theta_R}{2}\right)$$

Equating:
$$R \sin\left(\frac{\theta_F}{2}\right) = (R - l) \sin\left(\frac{\theta_R}{2}\right)$$

The **mechanical widening** Wₘ is the difference in *sagitta* (the perpendicular distance from the midpoint of the chord to the arc):

$$W_m = R - R\cos\left(\frac{\theta_F}{2}\right) - \left[(R-l) - (R-l)\cos\left(\frac{\theta_R}{2}\right)\right]$$

This simplifies (using small-angle and trigonometric identities) to the **exact single-lane formula**:

$$W_m = R - \sqrt{R^2 - l^2}$$

**Verification by small-angle approximation:**

For small angles, $\sqrt{R^2 - l^2} \approx R - \dfrac{l^2}{2R}$ (using the binomial expansion), hence:

$$W_m = R - \left(R - \frac{l^2}{2R}\right) = \frac{l^2}{2R}$$

For *n* lanes, multiplying by *n* (assuming *n* vehicles travel side-by-side):

$$W_m = \frac{n l^2}{2R}$$

This is the IRC-canonical **small-angle form** used in KTU problems.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Psychological Widening (Empirical, IRC)

The psychological widening is **not derived from first principles** — it is an empirical correction obtained from field observations of driver behaviour on curves. The IRC recommends:

$$W_p = \frac{V}{9.5 \sqrt{R}}$$

Where *V* is in **km/h** and *R* in **metres**, giving Wₚ in metres.

**Reasoning behind the form:**
- The numerator *V* reflects that at higher speeds, drivers demand more lateral clearance.
- The denominator *9.5√R* reflects that on flatter (larger R) curves, less psychological buffer is needed.
- The constant **9.5** is an empirical fit calibrated by IRC to Indian road conditions.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — IRC Short-Cut (Total Widening Bundled)

By substituting **standard l = 6 m** and **standard V** values into the full formula, IRC simplified the combined extra widening into two piecewise empirical formulas:

**Case A — V ≤ 65 km/h:**
$$W_e = \frac{n l^2}{2R} + \frac{V}{9.5\sqrt{R}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{IRC simplifies to } W_e = \frac{30}{R}$$

**Case B — V > 65 km/h:**
$$W_e = \frac{0.6 \, V}{R}$$

**Sample check** (Case A, n = 2 lanes, l = 6 m, V = 50 km/h, R = 220 m):
- Mechanical: Wₘ = (2 × 36) / (2 × 220) = 72 / 440 = 0.164 m
- Psychological: Wₚ = 50 / (9.5 × √220) = 50 / (9.5 × 14.83) = 50 / 140.9 = 0.355 m
- Total: Wₑ = 0.164 + 0.355 = 0.519 m
- IRC shortcut: 30 / 220 = 0.136 m

> [!NOTE]
> The shortcut formula gives a **lower bound** suitable for preliminary design only. For final KTU numericals, use the full formula **Wₑ = nl²/(2R) + V/(9.5√R)** unless the question explicitly directs otherwise.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A two-lane national highway is to be designed for a speed of **80 km/h** on a horizontal curve of radius **250 m**. The design vehicle has a wheelbase of **6 m**. Determine (i) the mechanical widening, (ii) the psychological widening, and (iii) the total extra widening. Also verify against the IRC shortcut.

**Solution:**

Given: n = 2, l = 6 m, V = 80 km/h, R = 250 m.

**(i) Mechanical Widening:**
$$W_m = \frac{n l^2}{2R} = \frac{2 \times 6^2}{2 \times 250} = \frac{72}{500} = 0.144 \; \text{m}$$

**(ii) Psychological Widening:**
$$W_p = \frac{V}{9.5 \sqrt{R}} = \frac{80}{9.5 \times \sqrt{250}} = \frac{80}{9.5 \times 15.81} = \frac{80}{150.2} = 0.533 \; \text{m}$$

**(iii) Total Extra Widening:**
$$W_e = W_m + W_p = 0.144 + 0.533 = 0.677 \; \text{m} \approx 0.68 \; \text{m}$$

**IRC Shortcut verification (V > 65):**
$$W_e = \frac{0.6 \, V}{R} = \frac{0.6 \times 80}{250} = \frac{48}{250} = 0.192 \; \text{m}$$

As expected, the shortcut is conservative; the full formula is the **correct design value**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram: Components and Flow of Extra Widening Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Horizontal Curve Design] --> B[Identify Inputs]
    B --> B1[Design Speed V in km per h]
    B --> B2[Radius of Curve R in m]
    B --> B3[Number of Lanes n]
    B --> B4[Wheelbase l in m]
    B --> C{Choose Method}
    C -->|Full IRC Method| D[Compute Mechanical Widening]
    C -->|Shortcut IRC| H[Apply Empirical Formula]
    D --> D1[Wm = n times l squared divided by 2R]
    D --> E[Compute Psychological Widening]
    E --> E1[Wp = V divided by 9.5 times sqrt R]
    D1 --> F[Total Extra Widening We]
    E1 --> F
    F --> F1[We = Wm + Wp]
    F1 --> G[Total Pavement Width = Normal Width + We]
    H --> H1{Compare V with 65 km per h}
    H1 -->|V less than equal 65| H2[We = 30 divided by R]
    H1 -->|V greater than 65| H3[We = 0.6 V divided by R]
    H2 --> G
    H3 --> G
    G --> I[End: Final Carriageway Width on Curve]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block-Diagram: Off-Tracking Geometry (Schematic Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Front Outer Wheel Path: Radius R] --> P2[Rear Inner Wheel Path: Radius R minus l]
    P2 --> P3[Center of Gravity Path: Radius R minus l by 2]
    P3 --> P4[Lateral Offset equals Mechanical Widening Wm]
    P4 --> P5[Add Psychological Buffer Wp]
    P5 --> P6[Total Extra Widening We]
    P1 -.-> P4
    P2 -.-> P4
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description of Off-Tracking (Textual Figure)

A **plan-view schematic** of a vehicle negotiating a right-hand curve can be described as follows:

- Imagine a rectangle of length *l* (wheelbase) and width *b* (vehicle width) representing the plan of the vehicle.
- The front-right wheel is the **steering pivot** and traces an arc of radius *R* (the design radius).
- The rear wheels are rigidly fixed to the vehicle chassis. Because the wheelbase *l* is rigid, the rear axle cannot follow the same arc — its pivot point is offset inward by *l*, so it sweeps a tighter arc of radius *R − l*.
- The **lateral distance** between the front-wheel path and the rear-wheel path at the **midpoint** of the curve is exactly the **mechanical widening Wₘ**.
- Adding the **psychological buffer Wₚ** outward gives the total extra widening Wₑ.

This is the geometric essence of every KTU question on the topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define extra widening of a pavement on a horizontal curve. Mention its components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Extra widening is the **additional width of carriageway** provided on horizontal curves over and above the normal width provided on straight sections, so as to ensure safe and comfortable negotiation of the curve by vehicles.

It has **two components**:
1. **Mechanical Widening (Wₘ):** Provided to compensate for the off-tracking of vehicles, i.e., the tendency of rear wheels to follow a path of shorter radius than the front wheels.
2. **Psychological Widening (Wₚ):** Provided to account for the additional lateral clearance that drivers instinctively maintain between their vehicle and the pavement edge while driving on a curve.

**Total Extra Widening:** Wₑ = Wₘ + Wₚ.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for two components named, 1 mark for correct formula.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the IRC formula for psychological widening of a pavement on a horizontal curve. What are its variables and units?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The IRC formula for psychological widening is:

$$W_p = \frac{V}{9.5 \sqrt{R}}$$

Where:
- **Wₚ** = Psychological widening in **metres**.
- **V** = Design speed of the vehicle in **km/h**.
- **R** = Radius of the horizontal curve in **metres**.

This formula is empirical in nature and reflects that higher speeds and tighter curves demand greater psychological lateral clearance.

*(Award 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for variables, 1 mark for units.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the IRC formula for mechanical widening of a pavement on a horizontal curve. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-lane highway is to be designed on a horizontal curve of radius **300 m** for a design speed of **75 km/h**. The design vehicle has a wheelbase of **6 m**. Compute the total extra widening required using the full IRC method and verify using the IRC short-cut formula. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumptions:*
1. The vehicle is treated as a rigid body with wheelbase *l*.
2. The front wheels steer along an arc of radius *R* (the design radius of the curve).
3. The rear wheels, being rigidly fixed to the chassis at distance *l* behind the front wheels, trace an arc of smaller radius *(R − l)*.
4. The angle subtended at the centre is small (so that sin θ ≈ θ and cos θ ≈ 1 − θ²/2).

*Derivation:*

The chord of length *l* subtends an angle θ at the centre of the curve. The sagitta (offset) of an arc of radius *R* subtending angle 2α at the centre, with chord *l*, is given by:

$$S = R(1 - \cos\alpha)$$

For the front wheel (radius R):
$$\cos\alpha_F = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{l}{2R}\right)^2}$$

For the rear wheel (radius R − l):
$$\cos\alpha_R = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{l}{2(R-l)}\right)^2}$$

The **mechanical widening** is the difference in sagittas:

$$W_m = R(1 - \cos\alpha_F) - (R - l)(1 - \cos\alpha_R)$$

For a **single lane**, after algebraic simplification, this yields the **exact** result:

$$W_m = R - \sqrt{R^2 - l^2}$$

Applying the **binomial expansion** for small *l/R* (small angle approximation):

$$\sqrt{R^2 - l^2} = R\sqrt{1 - \frac{l^2}{R^2}} \approx R\left(1 - \frac{l^2}{2R^2}\right) = R - \frac{l^2}{2R}$$

Therefore:

$$W_m = R - \left(R - \frac{l^2}{2R}\right) = \frac{l^2}{2R}$$

For *n* lanes (side-by-side vehicles), multiplying by *n*:

$$\boxed{W_m = \frac{n l^2}{2R}}$$

*(Mark split: 2 for assumptions, 3 for geometric derivation, 2 for the final formula.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

*Given:* n = 2, l = 6 m, V = 75 km/h, R = 300 m.

**Step 1 — Mechanical Widening:**
$$W_m = \frac{n l^2}{2R} = \frac{2 \times (6)^2}{2 \times 300} = \frac{72}{600} = 0.120 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Psychological Widening:**
$$W_p = \frac{V}{9.5 \sqrt{R}} = \frac{75}{9.5 \times \sqrt{300}} = \frac{75}{9.5 \times 17.32} = \frac{75}{164.5} = 0.456 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Total Extra Widening:**
$$W_e = W_m + W_p = 0.120 + 0.456 = 0.576 \; \text{m} \approx 0.58 \; \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — IRC Shortcut Verification (since V = 75 > 65 km/h):**
$$W_e = \frac{0.6 V}{R} = \frac{0.6 \times 75}{300} = \frac{45}{300} = 0.150 \; \text{m}$$

**Conclusion:** The full IRC method gives **Wₑ ≈ 0.58 m**, while the shortcut gives only **0.15 m**. The full method is the correct design value; the shortcut is a conservative lower bound used only at the preliminary design stage.

*(Mark split: 1 for Wₘ, 1 for Wₚ, 1 for total, 1 for shortcut selection, 1 for shortcut calculation, 2 for conclusion/comparison.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain, with neat sketches, the concept of off-tracking of a vehicle on a horizontal curve. How does it lead to mechanical widening? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-lane hill road has a horizontal curve of radius **50 m**. The design speed is **40 km/h** and the wheelbase of the design vehicle is **6 m**. Calculate the total extra widening using (i) the full IRC formula and (ii) the IRC short-cut formula. Comment on why hill roads require significantly more extra widening than plain-terrain roads. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):**

**Off-tracking** is the phenomenon in which, when a vehicle travels along a horizontal curve, the **rear wheels do not follow the same path as the front wheels**. Instead, the rear wheels trace an arc of **smaller radius** than the front wheels, effectively "cutting the corner."

**Geometric reason:** The wheelbase *l* of a vehicle is a rigid link. When the front wheels are steered along an arc of radius *R*, the rear axle — being at a fixed distance *l* behind — is constrained to a path whose instantaneous radius of curvature is *(R − l)*. Therefore, the rear wheels swing inward relative to the front wheels.

**Consequence:** The vehicle body, which connects the front and rear axles, sweeps out a swept envelope that is wider than the static width of the vehicle. The **lateral offset** of the rear wheel path from the front wheel path is the **mechanical widening Wₘ**, given by:

$$W_m = \frac{n l^2}{2R}$$

This must be added to the normal carriageway width so that the rear of the vehicle does not encroach upon the inner edge of the pavement or the adjacent lane.

**Sketch description (to be drawn in the answer sheet):**
- A plan view showing a rectangular vehicle entering a right-hand curve.
- The front wheel path is an outer arc of radius *R*.
- The rear wheel path is an inner arc of radius *R − l*.
- The lateral distance between the two arcs at the curve's mid-point is labelled *Wₘ*.

*(Mark split: 2 for definition, 2 for geometric reason, 2 for consequence/formula, 1 for sketch description.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

*Given:* n = 1 (single lane), l = 6 m, V = 40 km/h, R = 50 m.

**(i) Full IRC Formula:**

Mechanical widening:
$$W_m = \frac{n l^2}{2R} = \frac{1 \times 36}{2 \times 50} = \frac{36}{100} = 0.360 \; \text{m}$$

Psychological widening:
$$W_p = \frac{V}{9.5 \sqrt{R}} = \frac{40}{9.5 \times \sqrt{50}} = \frac{40}{9.5 \times 7.071} = \frac{40}{67.17} = 0.595 \; \text{m}$$

Total:
$$W_e = 0.360 + 0.595 = 0.955 \; \text{m} \approx 0.96 \; \text{m}$$

**(ii) IRC Shortcut (V = 40 < 65 km/h):**
$$W_e = \frac{30}{R} = \frac{30}{50} = 0.600 \; \text{m}$$

**Comment on hill roads:**
Hill (ghat) roads typically have:
- **Very small radii of curvature** (often 30–50 m, sometimes even 14 m at hairpin bends), which makes the *1/R* and *1/√R* terms in the widening formulas **explode**.
- **Lower design speeds**, but the radius effect dominates.
- **Long, heavy vehicles** (trucks, buses) negotiating tight curves.

For example, at R = 50 m, the extra widening is **~0.96 m** — more than **6 times** the value computed for R = 300 m in the earlier example. This is why hill roads must be designed with much wider pavements on curves, often with **unpaved shoulders** to absorb the extra swing.

*(Mark split: 1 for Wₘ, 1 for Wₚ, 1 for total, 1 for shortcut selection, 1 for shortcut calculation, 2 for comment.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (from past KTU answer sheets):**
> 1. **Forgetting the number of lanes (n):** Students often compute Wₘ for one lane but the road has two. **Always multiply by *n*.** A 2-mark deduction is typical.
> 2. **Wrong shortcut branch:** Mixing up the V ≤ 65 and V > 65 conditions. Memorize: *V ≤ 65 → Wₑ = 30/R*, *V > 65 → Wₑ = 0.6V/R*.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Writing R in km and V in m/s in the same formula. R must be in **metres**, V in **km/h** — always.
> 4. **Forgetting to take √R in Wₚ:** A surprisingly common error: writing Wₚ = V/(9.5 R) instead of V/(9.5√R). The square root is non-negotiable.
> 5. **Not stating the formula used:** If the question says "find the extra widening" and you just write a number, the examiner cannot award method marks. **Always write the formula first, then substitute.**
> 6. **Confusing Wₘ with Wₑ in the final answer:** Always end with the **total** extra widening, clearly labeled as Wₑ.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Extra widening = additional carriageway width on a horizontal curve, beyond the normal width.
- **Two components:** Mechanical (Wₘ) and Psychological (Wₚ).
- **Mechanical Widening (small angle, *n* lanes):** **Wₘ = nl² / (2R)**.
- **Mechanical Widening (exact, single lane):** **Wₘ = R − √(R² − l²)**.
- **Psychological Widening (IRC):** **Wₚ = V / (9.5√R)**.
- **Total:** **Wₑ = Wₘ + Wₚ**.
- **IRC Shortcut, V ≤ 65 km/h:** **Wₑ = 30 / R**.
- **IRC Shortcut, V > 65 km/h:** **Wₑ = 0.6V / R**.
- **Standard wheelbase** used in KTU problems: **l = 6 m**.
- **Off-tracking** is the physical reason for mechanical widening — rear wheels trace a smaller arc than front wheels.
- **Psychological widening** is empirical; faster speeds → more lateral buffer needed.
- **Hill roads** need *much* more extra widening than plains roads, because *R* is small and the formulas have *1/R* or *1/√R* dependence.
- **Order of magnitude check:** For typical KTU numericals (R = 100–500 m, V = 40–80 km/h), Wₑ usually lies in the range **0.3 m to 1.5 m**.
- **Design tip:** The shortcut formula is conservative; the full formula gives the actual design value. Always state which one you are using.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transition Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET502 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transition Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transition Curve (Spiral):** A curve of **varying radius** introduced between a **tangent (straight)** and a **circular curve**, or between two **branch circular curves of different radii**, whose radius is **inversely proportional to the distance** from the point of commencement of the curve. The radius varies from **infinity (at the straight)** to the **radius R of the circular curve** it meets.

It is a **non-circular** geometric curve that provides a **gradual, smooth, and continuous** change of curvature, allowing the driver to steer the vehicle effortlessly from a straight path onto a circular path.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle on a straight road at **60 km/h** and suddenly the road becomes a sharp hairpin bend. Two things happen at the same instant:

1. The **centrifugal force** slams you outward (you feel pushed sideways).
2. The **road is banked suddenly**, throwing you off balance.

Now, picture the road instead **gently bending** — first almost straight, then a little more curved, and finally fully circular. The bend "grows" into a curve. This gentle "growing bend" is the **transition curve**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of the **ramp of a spiral parking garage (helical ramp)**. The curvature of the ramp **gradually increases** as you move from the straight entry lane into the circular parking deck. The mathematical idealization of this curve is the **Euler Spiral (Clothoid)** — the most widely used transition curve in highway and railway engineering.

## 1.3 Need / Objectives of Providing a Transition Curve

The transition curve is provided to achieve **four engineering objectives simultaneously**:

| Sl. | Objective | Engineering Reason |
|-----|-----------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Gradual introduction of centrifugal force** | Prevents sudden lateral jerk on passengers and cargo |
| 2 | **Gradual application of superelevation** | The outer edge is gradually raised across the transition length |
| 3 | **Smooth steering of vehicle** | Driver can turn the steering wheel gradually, not abruptly |
| 4 | **Safety against derailment / skidding** | Avoids the lateral impact that would otherwise occur at the tangent point (TP) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Without** a transition curve, the abrupt meeting of a straight and a circle is a **point of discontinuity** in curvature. Theoretically, a vehicle would experience an **infinite jerk** at that point — clearly unacceptable in modern highway design.

## 1.4 Ideal Requirements of a Transition Curve

1. It should **originate from the straight** with **zero curvature (R = ∞)**.
2. It should **meet the circular curve tangentially** with the **same radius R**.
3. The rate of change of curvature should be **uniform** (linear increase of centripetal acceleration).
4. Its **length should be easy to compute** and **simple to set out** in the field.

## 1.5 Common Types of Transition Curves

| Type | Equation Form | Use |
|------|---------------|-----|
| **Cubic Spiral (Clothoid)** | $y = \dfrac{x^3}{6RL_s}$ | Most widely used; easy to compute and set out |
| **True Spiral (Euler Spiral)** | $A^2 = R \cdot L$ | Theoretical ideal; rare in routine practice |
| **Cubic Parabola** | $y = \dfrac{x^3}{6RL_s}$ (approx.) | Used for very long transitions |
| **Lemniscate** | $R \cdot L = \text{const.}$ | Used in railway transitions on hilly terrain |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 syllabus, the **Cubic Spiral (Clothoid)** is the **default** transition curve. All derivations below are based on it.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Description of the Spiral

Let:
- $L_s$ = Length of transition curve (m)
- $R$ = Radius of the circular curve to which the spiral connects (m)
- $s$ = Shift (offset of the circular curve from the main tangent) (m)
- $\theta_s$ = Spiral angle (deflection angle at the end of the transition) (radians / degrees)
- $T_s$ = Tangent length from T.P. to the start of the transition
- $V$ = Design speed (km/h); $v$ = design speed (m/s)

The fundamental governing relation of the **cubic spiral** is:

$$R \cdot L = R_c \cdot L_s = \text{constant}$$

where $L$ is the length from the start of the curve to any point and $R$ is the radius at that point.

## 2.2 Why the Cubic Spiral is Preferred — The 'Why' & 'How'

- **Why linear variation in curvature?** The centrifugal acceleration $a = v^2/R$ must grow **uniformly** with time to avoid jerk. Since $R$ varies as $1/L$ in a true spiral, $a$ grows linearly with $L$, which means linearly with time at constant speed.
- **How does the clothoid achieve this?** Its defining property $R \cdot L = \text{constant}$ (Euler's spiral) gives a **linear** rate of change of curvature, satisfying the comfort criterion.
- **Why not a circular arc or parabola?** A circular arc cannot start from $R = \infty$. A simple cubic parabola closely approximates the clothoid for the lengths used in practice (typically up to 1 km) and has simpler field-setting formulae.

## 2.3 Spiral Angle (θₛ) — Derivation Logic

The total change in direction of a vehicle while traversing the transition length $L_s$ is the **spiral angle**:

$$\theta_s = \int_0^{L_s} \dfrac{dL}{R}$$

Since $R = R_c \cdot L_s / L$ (from $R \cdot L = R_c \cdot L_s$):

$$\theta_s = \int_0^{L_s} \dfrac{L \, dL}{R_c L_s} = \dfrac{1}{R_c L_s} \cdot \dfrac{L_s^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_s = \dfrac{L_s}{2R_c} \text{ radians} = \dfrac{L_s \cdot 90}{\pi R_c} \text{ degrees}}$$

## 2.4 Shift (s) of the Circular Curve

When a transition curve is inserted between the tangent and the circular curve, the **circular curve is pushed inward** by a distance called the **Shift**. The geometry is:

$$\boxed{s = \dfrac{L_s^2}{24 R_c}}$$

For long transition curves:

$$s = \dfrac{L_s^2}{24 R_c} \left( 1 - \text{second-order terms} \right)$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **V = design speed in km/h, v = design speed in m/s, R = radius in m, Lₛ = transition length in m, e = superelevation (decimal), W = lane width (m), C = rate of change of centrifugal acceleration (m/s³)**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $L_s = \dfrac{V^3}{46.5 \, C \, R}$ | Length from rate of change of centrifugal acceleration | V in km/h |
| 2 | $L_s = \dfrac{v^3}{C \, R}$ | Same criterion, in SI units | v in m/s |
| 3 | $L_s = 0.0702 \, \dfrac{V^2}{R}$ | IRC empirical formula (C = 0.5 m/s³) | V in km/h, R in m |
| 4 | $L_s = \dfrac{e \, V \, W}{150}$ | From rate of change of superelevation (t = 1 sec) | V in km/h |
| 5 | $L_s = \dfrac{V \cdot t}{3.6}$ | From vehicle travel time t in seconds | V in km/h |
| 6 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{L_s}{2R}$ | Spiral angle | radians |
| 7 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{180 \, L_s}{2\pi R} = \dfrac{90 \, L_s}{\pi R}$ | Spiral angle | degrees |
| 8 | $s = \dfrac{L_s^2}{24R}$ | Shift of the circular curve | metres |
| 9 | $y = \dfrac{x^3}{6 R L_s}$ | Tangent offset (cubic spiral) | metres |
| 10 | $\delta = \dfrac{x^2}{2 R L_s}$ | Deflection angle at distance x from T.P. | radians |
| 11 | $T_s = (R + s) \tan \dfrac{\Delta}{2} + \dfrac{L_s}{2}$ | Tangent distance for full spiral + circular curve | metres |
| 12 | $C = \dfrac{v^3}{R \cdot L_s} = \dfrac{80}{75 + V}$ | IRC standard for C (V in km/h) | m/s³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IRC Standard value of C:**
>
> $$C = \dfrac{80}{75 + V} \text{ m/s}^3$$
>
> For typical design speeds: V = 40 → C ≈ 0.69; V = 65 → C ≈ 0.57; V = 100 → C ≈ 0.46. The commonly used **C = 0.5 m/s³** is an approximate average.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Highways & Expressways:** Mandatory on all curves with R < 3500 m (or as per IRC:73).
- **Railways:** Critical on high-speed tracks (above 100 km/h) to prevent **derailment**.
- **Runways & Taxiways:** Transition spirals are designed into airport curves for jetliners.
- **Mountain Roads:** Sharp hairpins are linked via short transitions to prevent rollover.
- **Bridges & Interchanges:** Spiral curves are used in ramp design (e.g., cloverleaf loops).

> [!NOTE]
> The transition curve is the **single most important safety feature** in geometric design — every IR award-winning highway project relies on correctly designed spirals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Length of Transition Curve (Comfort Criterion)

**Given:** A vehicle traveling at speed $v$ (m/s) enters the transition of length $L_s$ at time $t = 0$ and leaves at $t = L_s / v$.

**Step 1 — Write the centrifugal acceleration at any instant.**
