> **The following table is the single most important reference for solving numerical problems in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).** All percentages and limits quoted are as per **IS 2386 (Parts I–VIII)** and **MoRTH Table 500-10** unless otherwise stated.

| # | Test / Property | Governing Formula | Acceptable Limit (Surface Course) | IS Code |
|---|----------------|-------------------|-----------------------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV)** | $\text{ACV} = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | **≤ 30 %** (WBM), **≤ 26 %** (BC) | IS 2386 Part IV |
| 2 | **Aggregate Impact Value (AIV)** | $\text{AIV} = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | **≤ 30 %** (Wearing), **≤ 35 %** (Base) | IS 2386 Part IV |
| 3 | **Los Angeles Abrasion Value (LAAV)** | $\text{LAAV} = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | **≤ 30 %** (BC), **≤ 35 %** (Base) | IS 2386 Part IV |
| 4 | **Aggregate Abrasion Value (AAV)** | $\text{AAV} = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ (Deval) | **≤ 30 %** | IS 2386 Part IV |
| 5 | **Flakiness Index (FI)** | $\text{FI} = \dfrac{\text{Pass Flaky}}{\text{Total Retained}} \times 100$ | **≤ 25 %** | IS 2386 Part I |
| 6 | **Elongation Index (EI)** | $\text{EI} = \dfrac{\text{Pass Elongated}}{\text{Total Retained}} \times 100$ | **≤ 15 %** (Combined FI+EI ≤ 30 %) | IS 2386 Part I |
| 7 | **Specific Gravity (G)** | $G = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}$ | **2.6 – 2.9** | IS 2386 Part III |
| 8 | **Water Absorption (WA)** | $\text{WA} = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_2} \times 100$ | **≤ 2 %** (≤ 0.6 % for BC) | IS 2386 Part III |
| 9 | **Soundness (Na₂SO₄, 5 cycles)** | $\text{Loss} = \dfrac{\text{Wt. loss}}{\text{Original Wt.}} \times 100$ | **≤ 12 %** (Fine), **≤ 18 %** (Coarse) | IS 2386 Part V |
| 10 | **Angularity Number (AN)** | $\text{AN} = \dfrac{67 - \% \text{Solid Volume}}{100} \times 67$ | **0 – 11** | IS 2386 Part I |
| 11 | **Crushing Strength (Rock)** | $f = \dfrac{P}{A}$ MPa | **≥ 70 MPa** for BC | IS 9143 |

Where, in the symbols above:
- $W_1$ = original dry weight of aggregate
- $W_2$ = weight of fines passing 2.36 mm sieve (after test) / SSD weight
- $W_3$ = weight of basket + aggregate in water / pycnometer + aggregate + water
- $W_4$ = weight of saturated surface dry (SSD) aggregate in air / pycnometer + water

## 2.3 Why These Properties Matter — Engineering Utility

| Desirable Property | Real-World Consequence of Failure |
|-------------------|------------------------------------|
| Low ACV | Rutting in wheel path, base course crushing |
| Low AIV | Shattering under sudden heavy loads (e.g., truck braking zones) |
| Low LAAV | Loss of surface texture, polishing, loss of skid resistance |
| Low Flakiness Index | Mix segregation, low stability, ravelling |
| Low Water Absorption | Freeze-thaw cracking, bitumen stripping |
| High Angularity | Improved Marshall Stability, better rutting resistance |
| Good Bitumen Adhesion | Prevents potholes, extends pavement life by 5–10 years |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"CHATS-WAG-SAB"* — **C**rushing, **H**ardness (LA), **A**brasion, **T**oughness (Impact), **S**hape, **W**ater absorption, **A**dhesion, **G**ravity, **S**oundness, **A**ngularity, **B**itumen affinity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV) — Worked Numerical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):** A sample of aggregate weighing **2,500 g** is subjected to the Aggregate Crushing Value test. After applying a load of **40 tonnes** at a uniform rate, the fines passing the **2.36 mm IS sieve** weigh **600 g**. Determine the ACV and comment on its suitability for use in (a) WBM base course, (b) Bituminous Concrete (BC) surface course.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the variables from the problem statement**

$$
W_1 \;=\; \text{Original dry weight of aggregate sample} \;=\; 2500 \text{ g}
$$

$$
W_2 \;=\; \text{Weight of fines passing 2.36 mm sieve after crushing} \;=\; 600 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Recall the governing formula**

$$
\text{ACV} \;=\; \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values**

$$
\text{ACV} \;=\; \frac{600}{2500} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the fraction**

$$
\text{ACV} \;=\; 0.24 \times 100 \;=\; 24\,\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation against IS/MoRTH limits**

For **WBM base course**, MoRTH permits ACV ≤ 30 % → **24 % < 30 %, ACCEPTED ✓**

For **Bituminous Concrete (BC) wearing course**, MoRTH requires ACV ≤ 26 % → **24 % < 26 %, ACCEPTED ✓**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The aggregate is *marginal-to-acceptable* for both applications. For critical wearing courses on NH/SH, a lower ACV (≤ 20 %) is preferable for enhanced rutting resistance.

---

## 3.2 Aggregate Impact Value (AIV) — Worked Numerical

**Problem Statement:** A standard AIV test was conducted on aggregate passing 12.5 mm and retained on 10 mm sieve. The original weight was **550 g**. After 15 blows of the hammer (14 kg, 380 mm drop), the fines passing 2.36 mm weighed **165 g**. Compute the AIV and classify the aggregate as **strong / fairly strong / weak** per IS 2386.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the variables**

$$
W_1 = 550 \text{ g} \quad \text{and} \quad W_2 = 165 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the AIV formula**

$$
\text{AIV} = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values**

$$
\text{AIV} = \frac{165}{550} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Carry out the division**

$$
\text{AIV} = 0.30 \times 100 = 30\,\%
$$

**Step 5 — Classification as per IS 2386 recommendations**

| AIV Range | Classification |
|-----------|----------------|
| ≤ 10 % | Exceptionally strong |
| 10–20 % | Very strong |
| 20–30 % | **Strong (this case = 30 %, borderline)** |
| 30–40 % | Fairly strong (acceptable for base course) |
| > 40 % | Weak (NOT suitable for wearing course) |

> **Result:** The aggregate is **strong / borderline to fairly strong** — suitable for base and sub-base course, but for high-volume NH wearing course, a tougher source (granite/basalt with AIV < 20 %) is recommended.

---

## 3.3 Specific Gravity — Analytical Derivation

The pycnometer method for specific gravity of coarse aggregate uses **four weight measurements**:

| Symbol | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| $W_1$ | Weight of empty pycnometer |
| $W_2$ | Weight of pycnometer + SSD aggregate |
| $W_3$ | Weight of pycnometer + aggregate + water (full) |
| $W_4$ | Weight of pycnometer + water only (full) |

**Derivation:**

The volume of the aggregate equals the volume of water displaced by the aggregate. The water displaced is:

$$
V_{\text{aggregate}} = V_{\text{pyc+water}} - V_{\text{water+aggregate void}}
$$

The volume of water displaced is equivalent to the loss in weight of the pycnometer system when the aggregate is introduced:

$$
V_{\text{aggregate}} = (W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)
$$

The weight of the SSD aggregate in air is:

$$
W_{\text{agg}} = W_2 - W_1
$$

Hence, the **apparent specific gravity** is:

$$
G = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)}
$$

**Problem Statement:** A pycnometer weighs **650 g** empty. With **1,500 g** of SSD aggregate, total weight is **2,150 g**. The pycnometer + water alone weighs **2,000 g**, while pycnometer + aggregate + water (full) weighs **3,200 g**. Compute $G$ and water absorption if oven-dry weight is **1,470 g**.

### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Assign the weights**

$$
W_1 = 650 \text{ g} \quad W_2 = 2150 \text{ g} \quad W_3 = 3200 \text{ g} \quad W_4 = 2000 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator (weight of aggregate in air)**

$$
W_2 - W_1 = 2150 - 650 = 1500 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator (volume of water displaced)**

$$
(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2) = (2000 - 650) - (3200 - 2150)
$$

$$
= 1350 - 1050 = 300 \text{ g (or cm}^3\text{, since } 1 \text{ g of water} = 1 \text{ cm}^3\text{)}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Specific Gravity formula**

$$
G = \frac{1500}{300} = 5.0
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **A specific gravity of 5.0 is unusually high for natural road aggregate** (typical range 2.6–2.9). The student is expected to flag this and suspect a *recording or measurement error*. If real, it indicates a heavy mineral aggregate (e.g., **hematite, magnetite, barite**) — used in radiation shielding, not in standard road works.

**Step 5 — Compute Water Absorption**

$$
\text{WA} = \frac{W_{\text{SSD}} - W_{\text{dry}}}{W_{\text{dry}}} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{1500 - 1470}{1470} \times 100 = \frac{30}{1470} \times 100 \approx 2.04\,\%
$$

This WA of **2.04 %** is **borderline acceptable** (≤ 2 % is the IS limit; a value just above it suggests the aggregate is slightly porous).

---

## 3.4 Combined Flakiness + Elongation Index — Analytical Reasoning

**Conceptual Derivation of the 0.6 and 1.8 Thresholds:**

The **mean sieve size** for an aggregate fraction passing sieve *D* (mm) and retained on sieve *d* (mm) is:

$$
\text{Mean size} = \frac{D + d}{2}
$$

A particle is classified as **flaky** if its **least thickness** is less than:

$$
0.6 \times \text{Mean size} = 0.6 \times \frac{D+d}{2}
$$

A particle is classified as **elongated** if its **greatest length** is more than:

$$
1.8 \times \text{Mean size} = 1.8 \times \frac{D+d}{2}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering "Why" for the 0.6 / 1.8 factors:** These are empirical ratios (originally proposed by British Standards BS 812) that statistically identify particles prone to breakage under compaction and traffic loads. A cube-shaped particle has all three dimensions equal, so it will *not* be classified as flaky or elongated.

**Problem:** In a sample test, 200 particles are retained on 12.5 mm and passing 20 mm sieve. Mean size = 16.25 mm. The thickness gauge marks 60 particles as flaky (thickness < 9.75 mm). The length gauge marks 36 particles as elongated. Compute FI and EI.

**Step 1 — Flakiness Index**

$$
\text{FI} = \frac{60}{200} \times 100 = 30\,\%
$$

**Step 2 — Elongation Index**

$$
\text{EI} = \frac{36}{200} \times 100 = 18\,\%
$$

**Step 3 — Combined assessment**

MoRTH requires FI + EI ≤ 30 % for bituminous mixes. Here, 30 + 18 = **48 % → REJECTED for wearing course**, but possibly acceptable for lower base course where limits are slightly relaxed.

---

## 3.5 Los Angeles Abrasion Value (LAAV) — Problem Walkthrough

**Standard charges for LA machine (per IS 2386 Part IV):**

| Grading | Number of Spheres | Total Mass (g) |
|---------|-------------------|----------------|
| A | 12 | 5000 ± 25 |
| B | 11 | 4584 ± 25 |
| C | 8 | 3330 ± 20 |
| D | 6 | 2500 ± 15 |
| E | 12 | 5000 ± 25 (for fine aggregate) |

**Problem:** A 5,000 g grading A sample is rotated 500 revolutions in the LA machine. Final retained weight on 1.7 mm sieve is **3,650 g**. Determine the LAAV.

**Step 1 — Apply the formula**

$$
\text{LAAV} = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$
\text{LAAV} = \frac{5000 - 3650}{5000} \times 100 = \frac{1350}{5000} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Final result**

$$
\text{LAAV} = 27\,\%
$$

**Acceptability:** For bituminous concrete wearing course on NH/SH, **LAAV ≤ 30 % is required** → **27 % ACCEPTED ✓**.

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

### **Q1. List any six desirable properties of road aggregates and state the test conducted for each.**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

| # | Property | Test |
|---|----------|------|
| 1 | Strength / resistance to crushing | Aggregate Crushing Value (IS 2386 Part IV) |
| 2 | Toughness / impact resistance | Aggregate Impact Value (IS 2386 Part IV) |
| 3 | Hardness / abrasion resistance | Los Angeles Abrasion Test (IS 2386 Part IV) |
| 4 | Particle shape — flakiness | Flakiness Index (IS 2386 Part I) |
| 5 | Durability / weathering resistance | Soundness Test with Na₂SO₄ (IS 2386 Part V) |
| 6 | Adhesion with bitumen | Static / Dynamic Immersion Test (IS 6241) |

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between "Flakiness Index" and "Elongation Index".** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Flakiness Index (FI) | Elongation Index (EI) |
|-----------|----------------------|-----------------------|
| Definition | % particles whose least dimension (thickness) < 0.6 × mean sieve size | % particles whose greatest dimension (length) > 1.8 × mean sieve size |
| Equipment | Thickness gauge (slot openings) | Length gauge (rod spacings) |
| IS limit | ≤ 25 % | ≤ 15 % |
| Reason for test | Flaky particles break during compaction | Elongated particles align and create anisotropy |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the procedure for the determination of **Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV)**. State the IS-code limits of ACV for (i) WBM base course and (ii) Bituminous Concrete (BC) surface course. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** In an ACV test, the original dry weight of aggregate was **2,800 g** and the weight of fines passing the 2.36 mm IS sieve after crushing was **700 g**. Determine the ACV and comment on its suitability for use in (i) WBM base course, (ii) Bituminous Concrete (BC) wearing course. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Apparatus (1 mark):**
- Steel cylinder, **15 cm dia × 13 cm height** (internal)
- Tamping rod, **16 mm dia × 45–60 cm** with hemispherical tip
- Compression testing machine — capacity **200 tonnes**
- IS sieve **2.36 mm**, weighing balance (± 0.5 g)

**Step 2 — Preparation of sample (1 mark):**
Pass aggregate through **12.5 mm IS sieve**, retain on **10 mm sieve**. The fraction between 10 mm and 12.5 mm is used. Take **~2,500 g** of this fraction and dry in oven at 100–110 °C for 4 hours, cool to room temperature.

**Step 3 — Filling the cylinder (1 mark):**
Place the aggregate in the steel cylinder in **three layers of approximately equal depth**. Tamp each layer **25 times** with the tamping rod, applying uniform pressure.

**Step 4 — Load application (1 mark):**
Place the plunger on top of the sample. Position the cylinder centrally on the compression testing machine. Apply load at a **uniform rate of 14 N/mm²/min (≈ 40 tonnes total)** such that the full load is reached in **10 minutes**, then hold for **2 seconds** before releasing.

**Step 5 — Sieving and weighing (1 mark):**
Remove the crushed aggregate carefully. Sieve the entire crushed material through the **2.36 mm IS sieve** for 1 minute. Weigh the **fines passing 2.36 mm** ($W_2$) to the nearest 0.5 g.

**Step 6 — Formula and IS limits (2 marks):**

$$
\text{ACV} = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

| Application | Max Permissible ACV |
|-------------|---------------------|
| WBM base course | 30 % |
| Bituminous Concrete (BC) surface course | 26 % |

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the data (1 mark):**
$W_1$ = 2800 g, $W_2$ = 700 g.

**Step 2 — Apply the formula (1 mark):**

$$
\text{ACV} = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100 = \frac{700}{2800} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify (1 mark):**

$$
\text{ACV} = \frac{1}{4} \times 100 = 25\,\%
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with WBM limit (2 marks):**
IS/MoRTH limit for WBM = 30 %. Since 25 % < 30 %, the aggregate is **ACCEPTABLE ✓** for WBM base course.

**Step 5 — Comparison with BC limit (2 marks):**
IS/MoRTH limit for BC = 26 %. Since 25 % < 26 %, the aggregate is **ACCEPTABLE ✓** for BC wearing course, though it is on the higher side. For high-volume NH/SH applications, a lower ACV (≤ 20 %) is recommended.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students *forget to convert* the result from a decimal (0.25) to a percentage (25 %). Always multiply by 100 in the final answer. Also, students often write "30 %" as the limit for *every* layer — be specific: **30 % for WBM**, **26 % for BC**, **30–35 % for lower base courses** (varies by spec).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** What is the **Los Angeles Abrasion Test**? Describe the apparatus, procedure, and grading of charges used. State the IS-code limits for bituminous concrete and base course applications. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** In a Los Angeles Abrasion Test, a sample weighing **5,000 g** (Grading B) is subjected to 500 revolutions. After the test, the weight retained on the **1.70 mm IS sieve** is **3,400 g**. Calculate the LAAV and comment on its suitability for (i) Bituminous Concrete (BC) and (ii) Water Bound Macadam (WBM) base course. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
The **Los Angeles (LA) Abrasion Test** (IS 2386 Part IV) determines the resistance of coarse aggregate to abrasion and impact by tumbling it with steel spheres in a rotating steel drum. The abrasion loss is expressed as a percentage of original weight.

**Step 2 — Apparatus (1 mark):**
- A hollow steel drum, **711 mm dia × 510 mm long**, mounted on a horizontal shaft.
- An **abrupt shelf (lifter bar)** inside the drum that lifts and drops the aggregate and spheres.
- A counter / revolution recorder.
- **Steel spheres**, each **46.0–47.6 mm dia**, weight **390–445 g** each.

**Step 3 — Procedure (1.5 marks):**
1. Take aggregate of specific grading and oven-dry to constant weight ($W_1$).
2. Place the sample and the prescribed number of steel spheres in the drum.
3. Rotate the drum at **30–33 rpm** for **500 revolutions**.
4. Discharge the contents, sieve through **1.70 mm IS sieve**.
5. Wash the retained material, oven-dry, and weigh ($W_2$).

**Step 4 — Grading of charges (1.5 marks):**

| Grading | Aggregate Size Range (mm) | Sample Mass (g) | Number of Spheres | Sphere Mass (g) |
|---------|---------------------------|-----------------|-------------------|------------------|
| A | 80 – 63 | 5,000 | 12 | 5,000 ± 25 |
| B | 63 – 50 | 5,000 | 11 | 4,584 ± 25 |
| C | 50 – 37.5 | 5,000 | 8 | 3,330 ± 20 |
| D | 37.5 – 25 | 5,000 | 6 | 2,500 ± 15 |
| E | 25 – 19 | 5,000 | 12 | 5,000 ± 25 |
| F | Aggregates smaller than 2.36 | 5,000 | 12 | 5,000 ± 25 |

**Step 5 — IS limits (2 marks):**

| Application | Max Permissible LAAV |
|-------------|----------------------|
| Bituminous Concrete (BC) | 30 % |
| Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM) | 35 % |
| WBM base course | 40 % |

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the data (1 mark):**
$W_1$ = 5,000 g; $W_2$ = 3,400 g.

**Step 2 — Apply the LAAV formula (1 mark):**

$$
\text{LAAV} = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify (1 mark):**

$$
\text{LAAV} = \frac{5000 - 3400}{5000} \times 100 = \frac{1600}{5000} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Final computation (1 mark):**

$$
\text{LAAV} = 0.32 \times 100 = 32\,\%
$$

**Step 5 — Check against limits (3 marks):**

| Application | Limit | Result | Verdict |
|-------------|-------|--------|---------|
| Bituminous Concrete (BC) | ≤ 30 % | 32 % | **NOT ACCEPTED ✗** |
| WBM base course | ≤ 40 % | 32 % | **ACCEPTED ✓** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (LAAV):** The formula is **NOT** $W_2/W_1 \times 100$ like ACV. LAAV is the *weight of material abraded away*, i.e., the **LOSS** in weight. The numerator is $W_1 - W_2$ (the fines generated and passed through the 1.70 mm sieve). Mixing this up with ACV is one of the **most common mistakes** and results in 0 marks for the entire numerical part.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's High-Yield Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Areas in this Topic:**
> 1. **Mixing up ACV and LAAV formulas** — ACV uses $W_2 / W_1$ (fines generated), LAAV uses $(W_1 - W_2) / W_1$ (loss in weight).
> 2. **Wrong grading of LA charges** — for grading C, you need 8 spheres, not 12.
> 3. **Forgetting the 0.6 and 1.8 factors** for flakiness and elongation tests.
> 4. **Confusing water absorption limit (≤ 2 %)** with bituminous-concrete limit (≤ 0.6 % for BC wearing course).
> 5. **Not specifying the test sieve** — always state **2.36 mm for ACV/AIV** and **1.70 mm for LA**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Use this as a one-page mental snapshot 5 minutes before the KTU exam.**

- [x] Aggregates form **90–95 %** of bituminous pavement mass → control aggregate quality to control pavement quality.
- [x] **ACV** = $W_2 / W_1 \times 100$, limit **≤ 30 %** for WBM, **≤ 26 %** for BC.
- [x] **AIV** = $W_2 / W_1 \times 100$, limit **≤ 30 %** for wearing, **≤ 35 %** for base.
- [x] **LAAV** = $(W_1 - W_2) / W_1 \times 100$, limit **≤ 30 %** for BC, **≤ 35 %** for DBM.
- [x] **Flakiness** = thickness < 0.6 × mean size; **Elongation** = length > 1.8 × mean size.
- [x] **FI limit ≤ 25 %**, **EI limit ≤ 15 %**, **FI + EI ≤ 30 %** for bituminous mixes.
- [x] **Specific Gravity** range: **2.6 – 2.9** (use pycnometer for coarse aggregate, density bottle for fine).
- [x] **Water Absorption** limit: **≤ 2 %** general, **≤ 0.6 %** for BC.
- [x] **Soundness** (5 cycles Na₂SO₄): loss **≤ 12 %** for fine, **≤ 18 %** for coarse.
- [x] **Angularity Number** is **0 for rounded**, up to **11 for angular**; higher = better inter-particle friction.
- [x] **Adhesion with bitumen** is verified by **Static Immersion Test** (IS 6241) — coating retention ≥ 95 % is acceptable.
- [x] **Flaky + Elongated + Dusty + Weakly cemented aggregates = POTHOLE-FORMING recipe** — reject at source.
- [x] **Always mention IS code and load/units** in the answer to gain examiner's confidence and partial-credit marks.
- [x] For 14-mark questions, present answers in a **structured sequence**: Theory → Formula → Substitution → Result → Comparison → Comment.
- [x] Mnemonic: **CHATS-WAG-SAB** (Crushing, Hardness, Abrasion, Toughness, Shape, Water absorption, Adhesion, Gravity, Soundness, Angularity, Bitumen affinity).

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bituminous Materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Bituminous materials are **hydrocarbon mixtures** derived from petroleum refining (asphalt) or coal processing (tar) that are widely used as **binders** in road construction. They are characterized by their **visco-elastic behavior**, **thermoplastic nature** (soften on heating, harden on cooling), and **waterproofing** capability.

**Key Constituents of Bitumen:**
- **Asphaltenes** → Provide body and hardness
- **Resins** → Provide adhesive and ductile properties
- **Aromatic oils** → Provide viscosity and fluidity
- **Saturated hydrocarbons** → Influence rheology

### Conceptual Analogy 🛣️
> [!NOTE]
> Think of bitumen as the **"Black Glue of Roads."** Just like fevicol sticks pieces of paper together to form a craft project, bitumen sticks aggregates (stones, sand, gravel) together to form a strong, durable, water-resistant road surface. If you heat bitumen (like melting glue), it becomes fluid; on cooling (like glue drying), it becomes solid and sticky.

---

## 1.2 Subgrade Soil

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **subgrade** is the prepared and compacted natural soil layer (or selected imported soil) that lies immediately below the pavement structure. It serves as the **foundation** upon which the entire pavement system rests. Its primary function is to **distribute the wheel load stresses** from the pavement layers to a uniformly acceptable level and to provide adequate **support** without excessive deformation.

**Ideal Subgrade Properties:**
- **Stable** under repeated traffic loads
- **Adequate bearing capacity** (high CBR value)
- **Low compressibility**
- **Proper drainage** characteristics
- **Resistance to frost/heave**

### Conceptual Analogy 🏗️
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine building a multi-storey house on a weak, marshy plot — it will sink and crack. A subgrade is the **"engineered soil bed"** for the road, similar to a strong RCC raft foundation under a building. Without a stable subgrade, even the best pavement layers will fail prematurely.

---

## 1.3 Pavement – Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **pavement** is a durable, engineered surface laid over a prepared subgrade to support vehicular traffic. It must provide a **smooth riding surface**, adequate **skid resistance**, and **load-bearing capacity** while being economical and durable.

---

## 1.4 Flexible Pavement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **flexible pavement** is a layered structure in which the **highest stress occurs at the top layer** (bituminous surface) and decreases with depth. It transmits the wheel load to the lower layers through **grain-to-grain transfer of stresses**. The pavement "flexes" or deflects under traffic loads.

### Conceptual Analogy 🍰
> [!NOTE]
> Think of a **layered cake (Black Forest)** where each layer is well-bonded and the top thin chocolate layer carries the weight, distributing it through softer underlying layers to the plate (subgrade). A flexible pavement behaves similarly — bitumen layer on top, base, sub-base, and subgrade below.

---

## 1.5 Rigid Pavement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **rigid pavement** is constructed using **Portland Cement Concrete (PCC)** as the principal structural element. It possesses **high flexural rigidity (high modulus of elasticity ~30,000 MPa)**, so it acts like a **slab** spanning over irregularities in the subgrade. Stress distribution is governed by **slab action** rather than layer thickness.

### Conceptual Analogy 🪨
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine placing a **thick, hard-bound book** over a soft sand bed. The book does not bend — it bridges the minor irregularities. Similarly, a rigid concrete slab **spans** small depressions or weak spots in the subgrade, distributing load over a wide area through **beam/slab action**.

---

## 1.6 Comparison Snapshot

| Property | Flexible Pavement | Rigid Pavement |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Material** | Bitumen + Aggregates | Portland Cement Concrete |
| **Modulus (E)** | Low (1500–5000 MPa) | Very High (~30,000 MPa) |
| **Load Transfer** | Grain-to-grain | Slab (beam) action |
| **Design Principle** | Layered elastic theory | Westergaard's theory |
| **Initial Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent (every 5–7 yrs) | Low (after 15–20 yrs) |
| **Life Span** | 15–20 years | 30–40 years |
| **Joint Requirement** | No joints | Expansion & contraction joints |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Bituminous Materials

> [!NOTE]
> Bituminous materials are broadly classified into three categories based on their origin and physical state.

### A. Asphalt
- Obtained as residue from **petroleum refining** (crude oil distillation)
- Most common binder for road construction
- Types: Natural asphalt (lake asphalt from Trinidad) and Petroleum asphalt

### B. Tar
- Obtained from **destructive distillation of coal** or wood
- Has higher **temperature susceptibility** than asphalt
- Rarely used today due to toxicity and carcinogenic properties

### C. Cutback Bitumen
- Asphalt blended with **volatile solvents** (naphtha, kerosene, diesel) to reduce viscosity
- Three grades:
  - **Rapid Curing (RC)** → Naphtha solvent
  - **Medium Curing (MC)** → Kerosene solvent
  - **Slow Curing (SC)** → Diesel solvent

### D. Bituminous Emulsion
- Asphalt droplets dispersed in **water** with an emulsifying agent
- **Three types** based on setting time:
  - **Rapid Setting (RS)** → Used for surface dressing
  - **Medium Setting (MS)** → Used for premix with coarse aggregate
  - **Slow Setting (SS)** → Used for premix with fine aggregate
- **Cationic** (positively charged) and **Anionic** (negatively charged) emulsions

### E. Modified Bitumen
- Bitumen blended with polymers (SBS, EVA, rubber) to improve:
  - Rutting resistance
  - Low temperature cracking
  - Fatigue life

---

## 2.2 Tests on Bituminous Materials

| Test Name | Property Measured | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Penetration Test** | Consistency/Hardness | Grade classification |
| **Ductility Test** | Ductility (cm) | Adhesion property |
| **Softening Point Test** | Temperature susceptibility | Heat resistance |
| **Flash & Fire Point Test** | Safety at high temperature | Fire safety |
| **Viscosity Test** | Flow behavior | Workability |
| **Specific Gravity Test** | Density | Mix design |
| **Marshall Stability Test** | Stability & flow of mix | Mix design |
| **Ductility, Solubility** | Purity | Quality control |

---

## 2.3 Penetration Test (IS 1203)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **penetration value** is the distance in **tenths of a millimeter (0.1 mm)** that a standard needle (100 g load, 5 sec, 25°C) penetrates vertically into a bitumen sample.

**Penetration Grades of Bitumen (as per IS 73):**
- **30/40, 50/60, 70/80, 80/100** — for hot climate / heavy traffic
- Higher penetration → softer bitumen
- Lower penetration → harder bitumen

---

## 2.4 Subgrade Soil — Critical Properties

### 2.4.1 California Bearing Ratio (CBR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CBR is the ratio** of the unit load required to cause a **standard plunger to penetrate 2.5 mm (or 5 mm) into the soil sample** at a known rate, to the **standard unit load** required to cause the same penetration in a **standard crushed stone aggregate**.

$$
\text{CBR (\%)} = \frac{\text{Unit Load on Test Specimen at 2.5 mm penetration}}{\text{Unit Load on Standard Aggregate at 2.5 mm penetration}} \times 100
$$

**Standard Loads (ASTM/IS):**
- 2.5 mm penetration → **1370 kg/cm² (or 6.9 MPa)**
- 5.0 mm penetration → **2055 kg/cm² (or 10.3 MPa)**

**Typical CBR Values:**
| Soil Type | CBR (%) |
|---|---|
| Clay (poor) | < 3 |
| Silt | 3 – 7 |
| Sandy gravel | 7 – 20 |
| Well-graded gravel | 20 – 50 |
| Crushed stone | 50+ |

### 2.4.2 Modulus of Subgrade Reaction (k)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **modulus of subgrade reaction (k)** is defined as the **load per unit area** required to produce a **unit deflection** of the subgrade (kg/cm²/cm or pci — pounds per cubic inch).

$$
k = \frac{q}{\delta}
$$

Where:
- $q$ = Pressure applied (kg/cm² or psi)
- $\delta$ = Deflection (cm or inch)

**Typical k values:** 2 to 50 kg/cm²/cm (commonly 5–20 pci for design)

### 2.4.3 Resilient Modulus (Mr)

$$
M_r = \frac{\sigma_d}{\epsilon_r}
$$

Where:
- $\sigma_d$ = Deviatoric stress
- $\epsilon_r$ = Recoverable elastic strain

**Relation with CBR (for preliminary design):**
$$
M_r \text{ (psi)} \approx 1500 \times \text{CBR (\%)} \quad \text{(Heukelom & Klomp, 1962)}
$$

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **MEMORIZE THE FOLLOWING TABLE** — these formulas appear in 80% of KTU Module 2 numerical problems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CBR** | $\text{CBR} = \dfrac{P_t}{P_s} \times 100$ | % |
| 2 | **Modulus of Subgrade Reaction (k)** | $k = \dfrac{q}{\delta}$ | kg/cm²/cm or pci |
| 3 | **Resilient Modulus** | $M_r = \dfrac{\sigma_d}{\epsilon_r}$ | psi or MPa |
| 4 | **Mr ↔ CBR (Heukelom)** | $M_r = 1500 \times \text{CBR}$ | psi |
| 5 | **Mr ↔ k (AASHTO)** | $k = \dfrac{M_r}{19.4}$ | pci |
| 6 | **Equivalent Single Axle Load (ESAL)** — 4th power law | $\text{ESAL} = \left(\dfrac{P}{18000}\right)^{4}$ | — |
| 7 | **Load Equivalency Factor (LEF)** | $\text{LEF} = \left(\dfrac{P_x}{P_{18}}\right)^{4}$ | — |
| 8 | **Group Index (GI) of soil** | $\text{GI} = 0.2a + 0.005ac + 0.01bd$ | — |
| 9 | **Plate Bearing — Deflection** | $\delta = \dfrac{1.18\, q\, a}{E_s}$ | cm |
| 10 | **Westergaard — Corner Stress (rigid)** | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{3P}{h^2}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{l}\right)^{1.2}\right]$ | kg/cm² |
| 11 | **Westergaard — Edge Stress (rigid)** | $\sigma_e = \dfrac{0.572\,P}{h^2}\left[4\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{l}{b}\right) + 1.069\right]$ | kg/cm² |
| 12 | **Radius of Relative Stiffness (l)** | $l = \left[\dfrac{Eh^3}{12(1-\mu^2)k}\right]^{1/4}$ | cm |
| 13 | **Design Traffic (N)** | $N = AADT \times 365 \times \text{VDF} \times \text{LDF} \times \text{DF} \times \text{GF}$ | ESAL |

**Key Variables:**
- $E$ = Modulus of elasticity of concrete
- $h$ = Slab thickness
- $\mu$ = Poisson's ratio (concrete ≈ 0.15)
- $k$ = Modulus of subgrade reaction
- $P$ = Wheel load
- $a$ = Radius of equivalent contact area
- $b$ = Radius of equivalent radius for edge loading
- $l$ = Radius of relative stiffness

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The **CBR test** is the most widely used subgrade strength test globally because:
> 1. It directly correlates with **pavement thickness** in design charts (IRC:37, AASHTO).
> 2. It can be conducted both in **laboratory** (soaked, unsoaked) and in **field (in-situ)**.
> 3. It empirically accounts for **soil type, density, and moisture** simultaneously.
>
> The **Westergaard equations** revolutionized rigid pavement design in the 1920s and are still the **basis of IRC:58 (India) and PCA (USA) design charts** for concrete roads.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CBR Calculation from Test Data

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **CBR test** was conducted on a subgrade soil specimen. The standard loads at 2.5 mm and 5.0 mm penetrations are **1370 kg/cm² and 2055 kg/cm²** respectively.
>
> The observed loads on the test specimen are:
> - At 2.5 mm → 420 kg/cm²
> - At 5.0 mm → 540 kg/cm²
>
> **Determine the CBR value of the soil.**

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Calculate the CBR at 2.5 mm penetration.

$$
\text{CBR}_{2.5} = \frac{\text{Test Load at 2.5 mm}}{\text{Standard Load at 2.5 mm}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{CBR}_{2.5} = \frac{420}{1370} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{CBR}_{2.5} = 0.3066 \times 100 = 30.66\%
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the CBR at 5.0 mm penetration.

$$
\text{CBR}_{5.0} = \frac{\text{Test Load at 5.0 mm}}{\text{Standard Load at 5.0 mm}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{CBR}_{5.0} = \frac{540}{2055} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{CBR}_{5.0} = 0.2628 \times 100 = 26.28\%
$$

**Step 3:** Apply IS Code rule for final CBR.

> [!NOTE]
> **As per IS 2720 (Part 16) and ASTM D1883**, if the CBR at 5.0 mm is **greater** than that at 2.5 mm, the test is **repeated**. If the same trend persists, the **CBR at 5.0 mm is adopted**.

In this problem, $\text{CBR}_{2.5} = 30.66\% > \text{CBR}_{5.0} = 26.28\%$

Therefore, we **adopt the higher value at 2.5 mm penetration.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Design CBR} = 30.66\% \approx 30.7\%}
$$

**Step 4:** Resilient Modulus (Heukelom-Klomp correlation).

$$
M_r = 1500 \times \text{CBR (\%)} = 1500 \times 30.66 = 45,990 \text{ psi}
$$

$$
M_r \approx 46{,}000 \text{ psi} \quad (\approx 317 \text{ MPa})
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent Single Axle Load (ESAL) — 4th Power Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **AASHTO Load Equivalency Factor** is based on the empirical **Fourth Power Law**, derived from the AASHTO Road Test (1958–1961).

### Theoretical Foundation

**Assumption:** The damage caused to a pavement by an axle load $P$ is proportional to the **4th power** of the ratio of the load to a standard reference load (18,000 lb or 80 kN single axle).

**Derivation:**

$$
\text{Damage Ratio} \propto \left(\frac{P_x}{P_{18}}\right)^{4}
$$

**Equivalency Factor (LEF) for an axle:**

$$
\text{LEF} = \left(\frac{P_x}{P_{18}}\right)^{4}
$$

**For a Dual Wheel Set (Tandem Axle):**

$$
\text{LEF}_{\text{tandem}} = \left(\frac{P_{t}}{2 \times P_{18}}\right)^{4}
$$

### Numerical Example

> A **single axle** of magnitude **24,000 kg** with **dual wheels** is to be converted to ESAL.

**Step 1:** Identify the loads.

$$
P_x = 24{,}000 \text{ kg}, \quad P_{18} = 18{,}000 \text{ kg (standard single axle)}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the 4th power law.

$$
\text{ESAL} = \left(\frac{24{,}000}{18{,}000}\right)^{4}
$$

$$
\text{ESAL} = \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{4}
$$

$$
\text{ESAL} = \left(1.3333\right)^{4}
$$

$$
\text{ESAL} = 1.3333^2 \times 1.3333^2
$$

$$
\text{ESAL} = 1.7778 \times 1.7778
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ESAL} = 3.160}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** One passage of the 24,000 kg single axle is equivalent to **3.16 standard 18,000 kg axles** in terms of pavement damage. This is why overloaded vehicles cause **disproportionately severe** pavement deterioration.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Westergaard's Edge Stress Equation (Rigid Pavement)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Westergaard (1925)** developed analytical equations for the maximum tensile stress in a concrete slab. The **edge loading** case is most critical for design.

### Step 1: Problem Definition

A concrete slab of thickness $h$ and modulus of elasticity $E$ is supported on a subgrade of modulus $k$, and a wheel load $P$ is applied at the **edge interior position**.

### Step 2: Radius of Relative Stiffness (l)

Define the characteristic length that combines slab and subgrade properties:

$$
l = \left[\frac{Eh^3}{12(1-\mu^2)k}\right]^{1/4}
$$

**Derivation Logic:**
- **Numerator** $E h^3$ → represents the **flexural rigidity (D)** of the slab.
- **Denominator** → represents the **subgrade support**.
- The fourth root converts the ratio of stiffness to a **length scale** (units: cm).

Where $D = \dfrac{Eh^3}{12(1-\mu^2)}$

### Step 3: Edge Stress Formula (Westergaard, 1948 — refined)

$$
\sigma_e = \frac{0.572\, P}{h^2} \left[ 4 \log_{10} \left(\frac{l}{b}\right) + 1.069 \right]
$$

**Where:**
- $b$ = Radius of equivalent circular contact area at edge
- For a wheel load $P$ on a circular contact area of radius $a$: $b = a$ for edge interior, and $b = \sqrt{1.6 a^2 + h^2} - 0.675 h$ for edge corner.

### Step 4: Sample Calculation

> Given: $P = 5000$ kg, $h = 20$ cm, $E = 3 \times 10^5$ kg/cm², $\mu = 0.15$, $k = 5$ kg/cm²/cm, $a = 15$ cm.

**Step 4a:** Calculate $l$:

$$
l^4 = \frac{(3 \times 10^5)(20)^3}{12(1 - 0.15^2)(5)}
$$

$$
l^4 = \frac{(3 \times 10^5)(8000)}{12(1 - 0.0225)(5)}
$$

$$
l^4 = \frac{2.4 \times 10^9}{12 \times 0.9775 \times 5}
$$

$$
l^4 = \frac{2.4 \times 10^9}{58.65} = 4.093 \times 10^7
$$

$$
l = (4.093 \times 10^7)^{1/4} = 80.0 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 4b:** Calculate $b$ for edge loading:

$$
b = a = 15 \text{ cm} \quad \text{(for interior edge)}
$$

**Step 4c:** Calculate edge stress $\sigma_e$:

$$
\sigma_e = \frac{0.572 \times 5000}{20^2} \left[ 4 \log_{10}\left(\frac{80}{15}\right) + 1.069 \right]
$$

$$
\sigma_e = \frac{2860}{400} \left[ 4 \log_{10}(5.333) + 1.069 \right]
$$

$$
\sigma_e = 7.15 \left[ 4 \times 0.7270 + 1.069 \right]
$$

$$
\sigma_e = 7.15 \left[ 2.908 + 1.069 \right] = 7.15 \times 3.977
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_e = 28.43 \text{ kg/cm}^2}
$$

**Step 4d:** Compare with concrete's flexural strength (say MR = 45 kg/cm²):

Since $\sigma_e (28.43) < M_R (45)$, the slab is **SAFE** ✅

---

## 3.4 Group Index (GI) Derivation for Subgrade Soil

The **Group Index** is an empirical value used in the AASHTO Soil Classification System to assess the quality of a subgrade soil.

$$
\text{GI} = 0.2a + 0.005ac + 0.01bd
$$

**Where:**
- $a = F - 35$ (where $F$ = % passing 0.075 mm sieve)
  - If $F \leq 35$, $a = 0$
  - If $F > 75$, $a = 40$ (capped)
- $b = F - 15$ (where $F$ = % passing 0.075 mm sieve)
  - If $F \leq 15$, $b = 0$
- $c = LL - 40$ (where $LL$ = Liquid Limit)
  - If $LL \leq 40$, $c = 0$
- $d = PI - 10$ (where $PI$ = Plasticity Index)
  - If $PI \leq 10$, $d = 0$

**Lower GI → Better subgrade** (0 is best, 20 is worst).

### Sample Calculation

> Subgrade soil data: $F = 65\%$, $LL = 42$, $PI = 18$.

**Step 1:**
$$
a = F - 35 = 65 - 35 = 30
$$

**Step 2:**
$$
c = LL - 40 = 42 - 40 = 2
$$

**Step 3:**
$$
b = F - 15 = 65 - 15 = 50
$$

**Step 4:**
$$
d = PI - 10 = 18 - 10 = 8
$$

**Step 5:** Compute GI:

$$
\text{GI} = 0.2(30) + 0.005(30)(2) + 0.01(50)(8)
$$

$$
\text{GI} = 6.0 + 0.30 + 4.0
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{GI} = 10.30}
$$

```mermaid

    classDef load fill:#5a1a1a,stroke:#ff5252,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef sub fill:#0d3b27,stroke:#00e676,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    A["WHEEL LOAD P"]:::load

    C["BASE COURSE\nWBM / WMM / Crusher Run\n100 to 300 mm"]:::layer
    D["SUBBASE COURSE\nGranular / Moorum\n100 to 300 mm"]:::layer
    E["PREPARED SUBGRADE\nCompacted Natural Soil\n300 to 500 mm"]:::sub
    F["NATURAL SUBGRADE\nIn Situ Soil"]:::sub

    A --> B

    C --> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Wheel load is applied at the top.
- Stress is **highest** at the surface course and **diminishes** with depth.
- Each layer transmits load to the next through **inter-particle friction**.
