**(a)** Calculate the **air resistance, rolling resistance, and total resistance** at the design speed.
**(b)** If the truck is to climb a **4% gradient** at the same speed, calculate the **additional power required** to overcome the gradient. What is the **total power required** for the combined motion?

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Resistances at 80 km/h on a level road:**

**Step 1: Calculate air resistance.**

$$R_a = \dfrac{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V^2}{21.15}$$

$$R_a = \dfrac{1.2 \times 0.6 \times 6.5 \times (80)^2}{21.15}$$

$$R_a = \dfrac{1.2 \times 0.6 \times 6.5 \times 6400}{21.15} = \dfrac{29952}{21.15} = 1416.13 \text{ N}$$

$$R_a \approx 1416.1 \text{ N} \approx 1.416 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Calculate rolling resistance.**

$$R_r = f \cdot W = 0.015 \times 120{,}000 = 1800 \text{ N} = 1.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Total resistance on level road.**

$$R_{total} = R_a + R_r = 1.416 + 1.8 = 3.216 \text{ kN}$$

**Part (b) — Additional power for 4% gradient:**

**Step 4: Calculate gradient resistance.**

For a 4% grade, $G = 0.04$ (small angle, so $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = 0.04$).

$$R_g = W \cdot G = 120{,}000 \times 0.04 = 4800 \text{ N} = 4.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Calculate additional power for gradient.**

$$P_{gradient} = \dfrac{R_g \cdot V}{3600} = \dfrac{4800 \times 80}{3600} = \dfrac{384000}{3600} = 106.67 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6: Calculate power for level motion.**

$$P_{level} = \dfrac{R_{total} \cdot V}{3600} = \dfrac{3216 \times 80}{3600} = 71.47 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7: Total power required.**

$$P_{total} = P_{level} + P_{gradient} = 71.47 + 106.67 = 178.14 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note to Students:** The "additional power" is asked separately. Present both values clearly with units to earn full marks. **(7 + 7 = 14 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: SSD and Off-Tracking**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A vehicle is travelling at a design speed of **V = 100 km/h** on a road with a longitudinal coefficient of friction **f = 0.35**. Calculate the **Stopping Sight Distance (SSD)** as per IRC. Assume perception-reaction time **t = 2.5 s**.

**(b)** A car of wheelbase **L = 3.0 m** is negotiating a horizontal curve of radius **R = 50 m**. Calculate the **off-tracking width** using the IRC formula. What is the **extra widening required** for a two-lane road if the clearance is **0.6 m per lane**?

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Stopping Sight Distance:**

**Step 1: Calculate lag distance.**

$$d_{lag} = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t = 0.278 \times 100 \times 2.5 = 69.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Calculate braking distance.**

$$l_{braking} = \dfrac{V^2}{254 \cdot f} = \dfrac{(100)^2}{254 \times 0.35} = \dfrac{10000}{88.9} = 112.49 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Calculate total SSD.**

$$SSD = d_{lag} + l_{braking} = 69.5 + 112.49 = 181.99 \text{ m}$$

$$SSD \approx 182 \text{ m}$$

**Part (b) — Off-Tracking and Widening:**

**Step 1: Apply the IRC off-tracking formula.**

$$W_{off} = R - \sqrt{R^2 - L^2}$$

$$W_{off} = 50 - \sqrt{(50)^2 - (3.0)^2}$$

$$W_{off} = 50 - \sqrt{2500 - 9} = 50 - \sqrt{2491}$$

$$W_{off} = 50 - 49.91 = 0.09 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Calculate extra widening for two-lane road.**

$$W_{extra} = W_{off} + \text{Clearance per lane} \times \text{Number of lanes}$$

$$W_{extra} = 0.09 + (0.6 \times 2) = 0.09 + 1.20 = 1.29 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Verify with simplified formula (since R >> L).**

$$W_{off} \approx \dfrac{L^2}{2R} = \dfrac{9}{100} = 0.09 \text{ m} \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Self-check:** The two formulas give the same answer (0.09 m), confirming the calculation. The simplified formula is valid here because $L/R = 3/50 = 0.06 \ll 1$. **(7 + 7 = 14 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Read Before You Write!):**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Disaster:** Writing V in m/s but using 254 in the formula costs **2 marks instantly**. Always use V in **km/h** with 254, and V in m/s with the raw $V^2 / (2gf)$ form.
> 2. **Forgetting the Reaction Time:** A question that asks "calculate SSD" expects **both** lag and braking distances. Writing only the braking part forfeits **3 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing f and Grade (G):** Rolling resistance is $f \cdot W$, gradient resistance is $W \cdot G$. Mixing them up is the **#1 grading error** in KTU Module 3 papers.
> 4. **Not Converting kN to N:** Air resistance formula expects N. If W is given in kN, multiply by 1000 first.
> 5. **Missing Units in Final Answer:** KTU examiners can (and do) deduct 0.5 marks for writing "SSD = 182" without "metres" or "m".
> 6. **Wrong Rounding:** Use at least **2 decimal places** in intermediate steps. Final answer to 1 decimal place is acceptable.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vehicle Characteristics (KTU Module 3):**

- ☐ **SSD Formula:** $SSD = 0.278Vt + V^2/(254f)$ — memorize verbatim.
- ☐ **t = 2.5 s** is the **IRC standard** perception-reaction time; do not use 1.5 s or 3 s in board exams.
- ☐ **f = 0.35 to 0.50** is the longitudinal friction range; **f = 0.40** is the typical design value for plain roads.
- ☐ **Tractive Effort:** $TE = P \times 3600 / V$ — the "3600" is non-negotiable.
- ☐ **Power Required:** $P = R \times V / 3600$ — use this when resistance is given and power is asked.
- ☐ **Off-tracking:** $W_{off} = R - \sqrt{R^2 - L^2}$ — or simplified $L^2/(2R)$ when R >> L.
- ☐ **Extra Widening on Curves = Off-tracking + (Clearance per lane × Number of lanes).**
- ☐ **Ruling Gradient** is set by the design vehicle's P/W ratio; **limiting gradient** is for slow-moving heavy vehicles; **exceptional gradient** is for extraordinary terrain constraints.
- ☐ **Air resistance dominates above 60 km/h**; rolling resistance dominates below 60 km/h.
- ☐ **Power-to-weight ratio** is typically **8–10 kW/tonne** for trucks and **50–75 kW/tonne** for passenger cars — this dictates the ruling gradient.
- ☐ **Minimum turning radius** is governed by wheelbase and steering lock angle, typically **6–7 m** for cars and **10–12 m** for trucks.
- ☐ **Hill-climbing power formula:** $P = W \cdot V \cdot (\sin\theta + f \cos\theta) / 3600$ — for grades, **always use sin and cos together** with rolling resistance.
- ☐ **Grade % = (Rise / Run) × 100**; convert to decimal (divide by 100) before using in formulas.
- ☐ **Vehicle dimensions in IRC design:** Length = 12 m for trucks; Width = 2.5 m; Height = 4.75 m (with 5 m clearance for double-deckers).
- ☐ **Speed conversion constant:** 1 km/h = 5/18 m/s = **0.2778 m/s**. The reciprocal gives **0.278** in the SSD formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Macroscopic (Volume
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET502 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Macroscopic (Volume

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Introduction to Macroscopic Studies

**Traffic Engineering** is broadly classified into two fundamental approaches for analyzing traffic stream behavior:

1. **Microscopic Approach** – Studies the behavior of *individual* vehicles and their interactions (e.g., car-following theory, lane-changing models, headway distribution).
2. **Macroscopic Approach** – Treats the traffic stream as a *continuous fluid*, similar to fluid dynamics, and studies aggregate parameters like flow, density, and speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Macroscopic (Volume) Studies** form the *first pillar* of traffic stream analysis. They quantify the *quantity* of vehicles passing a point, section, or roadway over a defined period, expressed in standard units.

The **Macroscopic Approach** describes traffic as a continuum, where the entire flow is aggregated into measurable bulk parameters. The three primary macroscopic parameters are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| Volume (Flow) | $q$ or $v$ | Rate at which vehicles pass a point |
| Density | $k$ | Number of vehicles per unit length of road |
| Speed | $u$ or $\bar{u}_s$ | Rate of motion of vehicles |

For Module 3, the focus is exclusively on the **Volume (Flow)** parameter — the most fundamental and frequently measured macroscopic variable.

---

## 1.2 Definition of Traffic Volume

> [!NOTE]
> **Traffic Volume** is the *number of vehicles* (or passengers/persons) that pass a given point on a roadway, or that occupy a given length of roadway, during a specified time interval.

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Traffic Volume is defined as the *actual number of vehicles* observed or predicted to be traveling over a specified section of road or lane during a given unit of time, typically expressed in **vehicles per hour (veh/h)** or **vehicles per day (vpd)**.

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy — The River Analogy

Imagine a **river flowing through a narrow bridge**. You can measure the river in two ways:

- **How much water passes a point** (e.g., litres per second) — this is *flow* or *volume*.
- **How densely the water molecules are packed in a segment** — this is *density*.
- **How fast the water moves** — this is *speed*.

Similarly, on a road:
- **Volume** = how many vehicles cross a point per hour (like river flow past the bridge).
- **Density** = how many vehicles occupy a kilometre of road (like molecular density).
- **Speed** = how fast the vehicles are moving (like water velocity).

Just as hydrologists measure a river in *cubic metres per second*, traffic engineers measure road flow in *vehicles per hour*.

---

## 1.3 Units and Standard Metrics

The standard units of traffic volume are:

- **veh/h** — vehicles per hour (used for short-duration counts and peak hour analysis)
- **vpd** — vehicles per day (used for daily/annual planning)
- **PCU/h** — Passenger Car Units per hour (used for heterogeneous traffic, as in Indian conditions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PCU (Passenger Car Unit)** is a *conceptual* standard vehicle unit used to convert heterogeneous traffic into a homogeneous equivalent. The PCU value of a vehicle depends on its *static and dynamic* characteristics, primarily length, width, and speed.

Typical PCU values (as per IRC:64-1990 and Indian practice):

| Vehicle Type | PCU Equivalent |
|--------------|----------------|
| Car / Jeep / Van | **1.0** |
| Two-wheeler (Scooter / Motorbike) | **0.5** |
| Auto-rickshaw | **1.0** |
| Light Commercial Vehicle (LCV) | **1.5** |
| Bus / Truck (Heavy Vehicle) | **3.0** |
| Bicycle | **0.5** |
| Pedestrian | **0.2** |

> [!NOTE]
> The PCU values are *not constants*; they vary with **grade**, **speed**, and **proportion of vehicles** in the traffic stream. For exam purposes, the standard values listed above are universally accepted.

---

## 1.4 Why Macroscopic Volume Studies Are Essential

Volume data is the **foundational input** for virtually every transportation engineering decision:

- **Highway Planning & Design** — Determining the number of lanes, road width, and geometric design.
- **Capacity Analysis** — Comparing actual volume with road capacity.
- **Level of Service (LOS) Determination** — Assessing the quality of traffic flow.
- **Pavement Design** — Estimating cumulative load (ESAL calculations).
- **Traffic Signal Design** — Computing cycle length, green time, and phase plans.
- **Economic Appraisal** — Computing user benefits for cost-benefit analysis.
- **Accident Studies** — Correlating accident rates with exposure (volume).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Macroscopic Parameters — A Triad

The traffic stream is characterized by three interrelated macroscopic variables:

1. **Volume / Flow (q)** — Number of vehicles per unit time passing a point.
2. **Density (k)** — Number of vehicles per unit length of roadway.
3. **Speed (u)** — Distance travelled per unit time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Equation of Traffic Flow:**
> $$q = k \cdot u$$
> This is the *master relationship* connecting all three macroscopic parameters. It is also called the **Fundamental Equation of Traffic Stream** or the **Continuity Equation** (in its most basic form).

Where:
- $q$ = flow rate (vehicles/hour)
- $k$ = density (vehicles/km)
- $u$ = space-mean speed (km/h)

---

## 2.2 Types of Volume Measurements

### 2.2.1 Based on Duration of Count

| Type | Duration | Primary Use |
|------|----------|-------------|
| **Instantaneous Volume** | < 1 minute | Spot checks, manual counts |
| **Hourly Volume** | 1 hour | Peak hour analysis |
| **Daily Volume** | 24 hours | ADT calculation |
| **Weekly Volume** | 7 days | Weekly variation studies |
| **Annual Volume** | 1 year | AADT, trend analysis |

### 2.2.2 Based on Direction and Lane

| Type | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **One-way Volume** | Vehicles travelling in a single direction |
| **Two-way Volume** | Sum of both directional volumes |
| **Directional Split (DS)** | Percentage of volume in the peak direction |
| **Lane Volume** | Volume in a specific lane |

### 2.2.3 Based on Vehicle Type

- **Classified Volume Count** — Vehicles are categorized by type (car, bus, truck, etc.).
- **Unclassified Volume Count** — Total vehicles counted without type segregation.

---

## 2.3 Key Volume-Based Design Parameters

### 2.3.1 Average Daily Traffic (ADT)

> [!NOTE]
> **ADT (Average Daily Traffic)** is the *total volume* of traffic during a given period (more than one day and less than one year), divided by the *number of days* in that period.

$$ADT = \frac{\text{Total Volume in a Given Period (greater than 1 day, less than 1 year)}}{\text{Number of Days in that Period}}$$

### 2.3.2 Average Annual Daily Traffic (AADT)

$$AADT = \frac{\text{Total Volume during a Year}}{\text{365 Days}}$$

> AADT is the *most important* long-term planning parameter.

### 2.3.3 Average Daily Traffic on a Given Day

$$\text{ADT}_{\text{day}} = \frac{\text{Total 24-hour volume on that day}}{1}$$

### 2.3.4 Peak Hour Volume (PHV)

The **maximum hourly volume** observed during a 24-hour period. It is critical for:
- Signal design
- Capacity check
- Geometric design of intersections

### 2.3.5 Design Hourly Volume (DHV)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DHV** is typically the **30th highest hourly volume** of the year (also denoted as **30 HV**). It represents the hourly volume used for design purposes — high enough to accommodate most conditions, but not the absolute peak (which would be uneconomical to design for).

$$DHV = 30^{th} \text{ Highest Hourly Volume of the Year}$$

The logic: Designing for the *absolute peak* (1st HV) is uneconomical, as it occurs only once a year. Designing for the *30th HV* covers all but about 29 hours of the year, balancing cost and service.

### 2.3.6 Peak Hour Factor (PHF)

$$PHF = \frac{\text{Hourly Volume}}{4 \times \text{Peak 15-minute Volume within that Hour}}$$

- $PHF = 1.0$ indicates perfectly uniform flow within the hour.
- $PHF < 1.0$ indicates peaking within the hour (typical).

### 2.3.7 K-Factor and D-Factor

$$K = \frac{\text{Peak Hour Volume (PHV)}}{AADT}$$

$$D = \frac{\text{Peak Directional Volume during PHV}}{PHV \text{ (both directions)}}$$

These factors are used to estimate design volumes from AADT in the *Highway Capacity Manual (HCM)* methodology.

---

## 2.4 Methods of Volume Count

### 2.4.1 Manual Counting

- **Method:** Human observers tally vehicles using tally marks, hand counters, or data sheets.
- **Advantages:** Flexibility, ability to classify by vehicle type and movement.
- **Disadvantages:** Labour-intensive, prone to human error, limited to short durations.
- **Best Use:** Intersection turning movement counts, classified counts, short-duration studies.

### 2.4.2 Automatic Counting

- **Method:** Uses pneumatic tubes, inductive loops, radar, video, or piezoelectric sensors.
- **Advantages:** Continuous 24-hour data collection, high accuracy, low long-term cost.
- **Disadvantages:** High initial cost, may not classify vehicles accurately, requires calibration.
- **Best Use:** Long-term ADT/AADT monitoring, trend analysis.

### 2.4.3 Permanent Count Stations

- Continuous, year-round automatic counting stations installed by road authorities.
- Used to establish seasonal/weekly variation factors.

### 2.4.4 Portable / Temporary Count Stations

- Short-duration (24–48 hours typically) automatic counts used to develop *coverage counts* that are expanded to AADT using seasonal factors from permanent stations.

---

## 2.5 Volume Variation

Traffic volume is **not constant**; it varies with time. Understanding variation is critical for:

1. Converting short-duration counts to AADT.
2. Identifying peak periods for design.
3. Scheduling maintenance activities.

### 2.5.1 Temporal Variation Categories

| Variation Type | Cause | Use |
|----------------|-------|-----|
| **Hourly Variation** | Daily peak periods | Peak hour design, signal timing |
| **Daily Variation** | Weekday vs. weekend | Weekly design |
| **Monthly / Seasonal Variation** | Holidays, weather, agricultural cycles | Annual factor calculation |
| **Yearly / Trend Variation** | Population growth, development | Long-range planning |

### 2.5.2 Expansion Factors (for converting short counts to AADT)

$$AADT = V_i \times H_i \times D_i \times M_i$$

Where:
- $V_i$ = 24-hour volume on a particular day
- $H_i$ = Hourly expansion factor
- $D_i$ = Daily expansion factor
- $M_i$ = Monthly expansion factor

---

## 2.6 Volume Studies — Practical Applications

| Application | Volume Parameter Used |
|-------------|----------------------|
| Pavement design | ESAL (Equivalent Single Axle Load) — derived from AADT and vehicle classification |
| Intersection design | Peak hour turning volumes |
| Signal design | PHV and PHF |
| Economic analysis | AADT and vehicle operating costs |
| Road safety | Accident rate per million vehicle-km (uses AADT) |
| Environmental analysis | Volume × emission factor |

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every essential formula** for the Macroscopic (Volume) topic. Master this for KTU examinations.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Fundamental Flow Equation | $q = k \cdot u$ | $q$: veh/h, $k$: veh/km, $u$: km/h |
| 2 | Average Daily Traffic (ADT) | $ADT = \frac{\text{Total Volume}}{n \text{ days}}$ | Volume in veh, $n$ in days |
| 3 | Average Annual Daily Traffic | $AADT = \frac{\text{Annual Volume}}{365}$ | Volume in veh |
| 4 | Peak Hour Factor (PHF) | $PHF = \frac{HV}{4 \times V_{15}}$ | HV: hourly volume, $V_{15}$: peak 15-min volume |
| 5 | K-Factor | $K = \frac{PHV}{AADT}$ | Both in veh/h or veh/day consistently |
| 6 | D-Factor | $D = \frac{DPHV}{PHV_{total}}$ | Both in veh/h |
| 7 | Design Hourly Volume | $DHV = 30^{th} HV$ | veh/h |
| 8 | Expansion to AADT | $AADT = V_i \times H_i \times D_i \times M_i$ | Dimensionless factors |
| 9 | Flow from Headway | $q = \frac{3600}{\bar{h}}$ | $\bar{h}$: average time headway in seconds |
| 10 | Density from Spacing | $k = \frac{1000}{\bar{s}}$ | $\bar{s}$: average space headway in metres |

> **Real-World Utility:** These equations are the *engineering language* of traffic flow. They are used in every traffic impact study, highway design project, and intersection analysis in the world. Mastering them is equivalent to mastering the grammar of traffic engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fundamental Equation of Traffic Flow ($q = k \cdot u$)

### 3.1.1 Starting Concept

Consider a stretch of road of length **L** kilometres. At an instant, the number of vehicles in this stretch is the **density (k)** times the length:

$$\text{Vehicles in stretch} = k \times L$$

### 3.1.2 Movement of Vehicles

Suppose all these vehicles move with a common speed **u** km/h. In one hour, each vehicle travels a distance of **u** km. Therefore, all vehicles originally in the stretch of length L will pass a downstream point within the hour — *provided* L is exactly the distance they cover in one hour, i.e., $L = u$ km.

> In other words, the vehicles occupying a length of **u km** (at density k) will all cross any point in **1 hour**.

### 3.1.3 Mathematical Step-Up

Step 1 — Number of vehicles in a length of $u$ km:

$$N = k \times u \quad \text{vehicles}$$

Step 2 — These $N$ vehicles cross the reference point in 1 hour, so the flow is:

$$q = \frac{N}{1 \text{ hour}} = k \times u$$

Step 3 — Therefore, the **Fundamental Equation of Traffic Flow** is:

$$\boxed{q = k \cdot u}$$

Where:
- $q$ = flow rate (vehicles/hour)
- $k$ = density (vehicles/km)
- $u$ = space-mean speed (km/h)

### 3.1.4 Unit Check

$$[q] = [k] \cdot [u] = \left[\frac{\text{veh}}{\text{km}}\right] \cdot \left[\frac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}\right] = \frac{\text{veh}}{\text{h}} \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Peak Hour Factor (PHF) from First Principles

### 3.2.1 Concept

Within a peak hour, the flow rate is **not uniform**. It fluctuates between sub-periods (e.g., 15-minute intervals). The PHF quantifies this peaking effect.

### 3.2.2 Definition Components

- **Hourly Volume (HV):** Total vehicles counted in the peak hour = $V_{15,1} + V_{15,2} + V_{15,3} + V_{15,4}$
- **Peak 15-minute Volume ($V_{15}$):** Maximum volume in any 15-minute interval within that hour.

### 3.2.3 Derivation

Step 1 — If the flow were *perfectly uniform* for the full hour, the volume in 15 minutes would be:

$$V_{15, \text{uniform}} = \frac{HV}{4}$$

Step 2 — The **Peak 15-minute Volume** is observed as the maximum:

$$V_{15} = \max(V_{15,1}, V_{15,2}, V_{15,3}, V_{15,4})$$

Step 3 — The PHF is the ratio of the average rate (over the hour) to the peak rate (over 15 min), expressed as a dimensionless number:

$$PHF = \frac{\text{Average hourly flow rate}}{\text{Peak 15-minute flow rate}}$$

Step 4 — Converting to volumes:

$$PHF = \frac{HV / 1 \text{ h}}{V_{15} / 0.25 \text{ h}} = \frac{HV}{4 \times V_{15}}$$

$$\boxed{PHF = \frac{HV}{4 \times V_{15, \text{peak}}}}$$

> **Range:** $0.25 \leq PHF \leq 1.0$
> - $PHF = 1.0$ → Uniform flow throughout the hour.
> - $PHF < 1.0$ → Peaking within the hour (typical in urban conditions, usually 0.7–0.95).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Expansion of Short-Count Data to AADT

### 3.3.1 The Problem

A traffic engineer conducts a **24-hour volume count** on a Wednesday in March and obtains a volume $V_i$. The goal is to estimate the **AADT** for that year.

### 3.3.2 The Correction Factors

To convert the 24-hour count to AADT, three correction factors are applied:

1. **Hourly Expansion Factor ($H_i$):**
   - Converts hourly counts to 24-hour volume for the day.
   - If the count is for less than 24 hours, $H_i$ scales it up.
   - If the count is exactly 24 hours, $H_i = 1.0$.

$$H_i = \frac{\text{Total 24-hour volume on a typical day}}{\text{Volume during the time the count was made}}$$

2. **Daily Expansion Factor ($D_i$):**
   - Converts a single day's volume to *average weekly* volume.

$$D_i = \frac{\text{Average total volume for week (Sun–Sat)}}{\text{Average volume for the particular day of week}}$$

   - Equivalently, $D_i = \frac{\text{Average 24-hour volume for all 7 days}}{\text{24-hour volume on the counted day}}$

3. **Monthly Expansion Factor ($M_i$):**
   - Converts average daily volume for the month to average daily volume for the year.

$$M_i = \frac{\text{AADT for the year}}{\text{ADT for the particular month}}$$

### 3.3.3 The Composite Expansion

Step 1 — Convert the short count to a 24-hour volume:

$$V_{24} = V_i \times H_i$$

Step 2 — Convert the day's volume to an average day of the week:

$$ADT_{\text{week}} = V_{24} \times D_i$$

Step 3 — Convert the month's average to the year's average:

$$\boxed{AADT = V_i \times H_i \times D_i \times M_i}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical 14-Mark Problem Style)

### Problem Statement

A traffic engineer conducted a volume survey on a rural highway. The following data were obtained from a **6-hour count** (8:00 AM to 2:00 PM) on a **Tuesday in July**:

- Total vehicles counted in 6 hours: **2,400 vehicles**
- Average 24-hour volume on a typical day: **5,200 vehicles**
- Average total weekly volume: **33,600 vehicles**
- Average volume on Tuesdays: **4,800 vehicles**
- Average ADT for July: **4,400 vehicles**
- AADT for the year: **4,000 vehicles**

**Tasks:**
1. Compute the **AADT** from the short count.
2. Compute the **K-factor** if the peak hour volume observed is 480 veh/h.
3. Compute the **Peak Hour Factor (PHF)** if the peak 15-minute volume is 140 veh.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute $H_i$ (Hourly Expansion Factor):**

$$H_i = \frac{\text{Average 24-hour volume}}{\text{Volume during the count period}}$$

$$H_i = \frac{5200}{2400} = 2.1667$$

> This means the 6-hour count represents $1/2.1667 = 46.15\%$ of the daily volume.

**Step 2 — Compute $D_i$ (Daily Expansion Factor):**

$$D_i = \frac{\text{Average volume for the week}}{\text{Average volume on the counted day (Tuesday)}}$$

$$D_i = \frac{33600 / 7}{4800} = \frac{4800}{4800} = 1.0$$

> Since we used the *average* daily volume for Tuesday in the denominator, $D_i = 1.0$ exactly.

**Step 3 — Compute $M_i$ (Monthly Expansion Factor):**

$$M_i = \frac{AADT}{ADT \text{ for July}}$$

$$M_i = \frac{4000}{4400} = 0.9091$$

**Step 4 — Compute AADT:**

$$AADT = V_i \times H_i \times D_i \times M_i$$

$$AADT = 2400 \times 2.1667 \times 1.0 \times 0.9091$$

$$AADT = 2400 \times 1.9697$$

$$\boxed{AADT \approx 4727 \text{ veh/day}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute K-factor:**

$$K = \frac{PHV}{AADT} = \frac{480}{4727}$$

$$\boxed{K = 0.1016 \approx 0.10 \text{ (or 10.16\%)}}$$

> This is typical for a rural highway (K typically 0.08–0.12 for rural, 0.10–0.15 for urban).

**Step 6 — Compute PHF:**

$$PHF = \frac{HV}{4 \times V_{15}} = \frac{480}{4 \times 140} = \frac{480}{560}$$

$$\boxed{PHF = 0.857}$$

> A PHF of 0.857 indicates moderate peaking within the hour — typical for non-congested conditions.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Flow from Time Headway (Inverse Relationship)

### 3.5.1 Concept

If the **average time headway** (the time gap between consecutive vehicles at a point) is $\bar{h}$ seconds, then in 3600 seconds (1 hour), the number of vehicles passing is:

$$q = \frac{3600}{\bar{h}} \text{ veh/h}$$

### 3.5.2 Derivation

Step 1 — Time headway $h$ is the time between successive vehicles. The reciprocal $1/h$ has units of vehicles per second.

Step 2 — In one hour (3600 seconds), the number of vehicles is:

$$q = \frac{3600}{\bar{h}}$$

> **Example:** If $\bar{h} = 2$ s, then $q = 3600/2 = 1800$ veh/h.

Similarly, if the **average space headway** (distance between consecutive vehicles) is $\bar{s}$ metres, then in 1 km (1000 m), the number of vehicles is:

$$k = \frac{1000}{\bar{s}} \text{ veh/km}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Volume Problems

| # | Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula |
|---|--------------|-------|------|-------------|
| 1 | ADT Calculation | Total volume, days | ADT | $ADT = V/n$ |
| 2 | AADT from Short Count | $V_i, H_i, D_i, M_i$ | AADT | $AADT = V_i \times H_i \times D_i \times M_i$ |
| 3 | K-Factor | PHV, AADT | K | $K = PHV/AADT$ |
| 4 | D-Factor | Directional PHV, Total PHV | D | $D = DPHV/PHV$ |
| 5 | PHF | HV, $V_{15}$ | PHF | $PHF = HV/(4 \times V_{15})$ |
| 6 | Design Hourly Volume | Hourly volume ranking | DHV | 30th HV |
| 7 | Flow from Density & Speed | $k$, $u$ | $q$ | $q = k \times u$ |
| 8 | Flow from Headway | $\bar{h}$ (seconds) | $q$ | $q = 3600/\bar{h}$ |
| 9 | Density from Spacing | $\bar{s}$ (metres) | $k$ | $k = 1000/\bar{s}$ |
| 10 | PCU Conversion | Vehicle count, PCU values | PCU flow | $q_{PCU} = \sum(n_i \times PCU_i)$ |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Define traffic volume. What are the different units used to express traffic volume? Also explain the concept of PCU.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Traffic Volume:** Traffic volume is the number of vehicles passing a given point on a roadway during a specified time interval.
>
> **Units of Traffic Volume:**
> 1. **vehicles per hour (veh/h)** — for short-term counts and peak hour analysis.
> 2. **vehicles per day (vpd)** — for daily and annual planning.
> 3. **PCU per hour (PCU/h)** — for heterogeneous traffic conditions.
>
> **Concept of PCU (Passenger Car Unit):** PCU is a conceptual standard vehicle unit used to convert a mixed traffic stream into a homogeneous equivalent. It accounts for the relative space occupancy and dynamic performance of different vehicle types. For example, a bus is equivalent to 3.0 PCUs, while a two-wheeler is 0.5 PCU, as per IRC:64-1990.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**What is meant by Peak Hour Factor (PHF)? What does a PHF value of 0.85 indicate about the traffic flow?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Peak Hour Factor (PHF):** PHF is the ratio of the total hourly volume to four times the peak 15-minute volume within that hour:
> $$PHF = \frac{HV}{4 \times V_{15, \text{peak}}}$$
> It ranges from 0.25 (extreme peaking) to 1.0 (uniform flow).
>
> **Interpretation of PHF = 0.85:** A PHF value of 0.85 indicates that the flow within the hour is **moderately non-uniform**. The peak 15-minute flow rate is approximately $1/0.85 = 1.18$ times higher than the average hourly rate. This is typical of urban/suburban traffic with some signal coordination effects. A PHF of 1.0 would represent perfectly uniform flow (rare in practice).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define traffic volume studies. Explain with a neat sketch the different methods of conducting volume counts. Discuss the relative merits and demerits of manual and automatic counting methods. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 6-hour volume count conducted on a Tuesday in May gave the following data:
- Total volume: 1,800 vehicles
- Average 24-hour volume: 4,500 vehicles
- Average total weekly volume: 28,000 vehicles
- Average volume on Tuesdays: 3,500 vehicles
- Average ADT for May: 3,800 vehicles
- AADT for the year: 4,000 vehicles

Compute the AADT. Also determine the K-factor if the peak hour volume during the count period is 400 veh/h. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a)

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Traffic volume studies involve the systematic measurement and recording of the number of vehicles (and their types) passing a point, section, or length of roadway over a defined time period. The primary purpose is to obtain quantitative data on the *quantity of traffic*, which forms the basis for planning, design, capacity analysis, and operational decisions.

**Methods of Volume Count (3 Marks):**

**Sketch/Block Diagram:**
- Manual Counting (Tally method, mechanical hand counters)
- Automatic Counting:
  * Pneumatic tube
  * Inductive loop
  * Piezoelectric sensor
  * Video image detection
  * Radar-based detection

**Merits and Demerits (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | Manual Counting | Automatic Counting |
|--------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Accuracy for classified counts** | High (visual classification) | Moderate (sensor-based) |
| **Cost for short duration** | Low | High (equipment) |
| **Cost for long duration** | Very high (manpower) | Low (one-time installation) |
| **Data type** | Classified + movements | Total volume (sometimes classified) |
| **Duration limitation** | Short (typically < 12 h continuous) | 24/7 continuous |
| **Error sources** | Fatigue, missed counts | Calibration drift, weather effects |

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b)

**Step 1 — Compute $H_i$ (Hourly Expansion Factor):**

$$H_i = \frac{\text{Average 24-hour volume}}{\text{Volume during the count period}}$$

$$H_i = \frac{4500}{1800} = 2.5$$

**Step 2 — Compute $D_i$ (Daily Expansion Factor):**

$$D_i = \frac{\text{Average volume per day of the week}}{\text{Average volume on the counted day}}$$

$$D_i = \frac{28000 / 7}{3500} = \frac{4000}{3500} = 1.1429$$

**Step 3 — Compute $M_i$ (Monthly Expansion Factor):**

$$M_i = \frac{AADT}{ADT \text{ for May}}$$

$$M_i = \frac{4000}{3800} = 1.0526$$

**Step 4 — Compute AADT:**

$$AADT = V_i \times H_i \times D_i \times M_i$$

$$AADT = 1800 \times 2.5 \times 1.1429 \times 1.0526$$

$$AADT = 1800 \times 3.0079 = 5414.2$$

$$\boxed{AADT \approx 5414 \text{ veh/day}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute K-factor:**

$$K = \frac{PHV}{AADT} = \frac{400}{5414}$$

$$\boxed{K = 0.0739 \approx 0.074 \text{ (or 7.39\%)}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The peak hour carries about 7.4% of the AADT, which is typical of a **low-volume rural highway** (K = 0.08–0.12 is rural range; this is at the lower end, indicating rural character).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** What are the various volume-based design parameters used in transportation engineering? Explain ADT, AADT, DHV, and 30 HV in detail with their significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The following data were obtained at an urban intersection:

| Time Interval | Vehicles (15-min count) |
|---------------|--------------------------|
| 8:00 – 8:15 AM | 130 |
| 8:15 – 8:30 AM | 145 |
| 8:30 – 8:45 AM | 160 |
| 8:45 – 9:00 AM | 125 |

Compute:
(i) Hourly volume for 8:00 – 9:00 AM.
(ii) Peak 15-minute volume.
(iii) Peak Hour Factor (PHF).
(iv) If the AADT is 12,000 veh/day and the peak hour is the design hour, compute the K-factor. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Volume-based design parameters are aggregate traffic measures that guide decisions on lane numbers, intersection geometry, signal timing, and pavement thickness.

**ADT — Average Daily Traffic (1.5 Marks):**

$$ADT = \frac{\text{Total volume during a period (1 day to < 1 year)}}{\text{Number of days}}$$

- Used for: Short to medium-term planning, design between major surveys.
- Limitation: Does not capture seasonal variation.

**AADT — Average Annual Daily Traffic (1.5 Marks):**

$$AADT = \frac{\text{Total annual volume}}{365}$$

- Used for: Long-term planning, federal funding allocation, pavement design (as input to ESALs), highway capacity analysis.
- Considered the **most important and stable** long-term volume measure.

**DHV — Design Hourly Volume (1.5 Marks):**

- The hourly volume used for **design purposes**.
- It is *not* the absolute peak (1st HV) because that would be uneconomical.
- It is *not* the average, because that would lead to frequent congestion.

**30 HV — 30th Highest Hourly Volume (1.5 Marks):**

- Specifically, the volume that is **exceeded only 29 times** in the year.
- Used as DHV in conventional practice.
- Rationale: Designing for the 30th HV serves about 99.7% of the hours in a year economically.
- For urban roads, the **50th HV** is sometimes used; for low-volume rural roads, **100th–200th HV** may be used.

**Significance Summary:**
- ADT/AADT → long-range planning and pavement design.
- DHV / 30 HV → geometric design, intersection design, signal timing.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b)

**Given Data:**

| 15-min Interval | Volume (veh) |
|-----------------|---------------|
| 8:00 – 8:15 | 130 |
| 8:15 – 8:30 | 145 |
| 8:30 – 8:45 | 160 |
| 8:45 – 9:00 | 125 |

**Part (i) — Hourly Volume (8:00 AM – 9:00 AM):**

$$HV = 130 + 145 + 160 + 125$$

$$\boxed{HV = 560 \text{ vehicles}}$$

**Part (ii) — Peak 15-minute Volume:**

$$V_{15, \text{peak}} = \max(130, 145, 160, 125) = 160$$

$$\boxed{V_{15, \text{peak}} = 160 \text{ vehicles}}$$

(occurs in the interval 8:30 – 8:45 AM)

**Part (iii) — Peak Hour Factor (PHF):**

$$PHF = \frac{HV}{4 \times V_{15, \text{peak}}}$$

$$PHF = \frac{560}{4 \times 160} = \frac{560}{640}$$

$$\boxed{PHF = 0.875}$$

> This indicates moderate peaking; the 15-min peak rate is $1/0.875 = 1.143$ times the average hourly rate.

**Part (iv) — K-factor:**

$$K = \frac{PHV}{AADT} = \frac{560}{12000}$$

$$\boxed{K = 0.0467 \text{ (or 4.67\%)}}$$

> This is at the *lower end* of typical urban K-values (urban K is usually 0.08–0.12). A low K suggests that the morning peak is not very pronounced relative to daily volume — possibly a mixed-use area with steady demand.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ADT and AADT:** ADT is for a period *less than a year but more than one day*. AADT is specifically the *annual* average. Marks lost in definitions.
>
> 2. **Incorrect D-factor formula:** Students often write $D = PHV_{peak}/PHV_{total}$ using the wrong direction. The D-factor is the fraction of *peak-direction* flow in the *total peak-hour* flow.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply D_i by 7 or confuse $D_i$ formula:** The daily expansion factor is the ratio of *average daily* to *the specific day's* volume, not the total weekly volume to that day.
>
> 4. **Sign convention in PHF:** PHF = 1 is **uniform** flow (not 0); PHF close to 0 is **extreme peaking**. Confusing this costs conceptual marks.
>
> 5. **Units mismatch in K-factor:** PHV in veh/h, AADT in veh/day — students often divide directly without unit consistency. K should be computed in the same hourly basis (e.g., convert AADT to hourly by dividing by 24 if needed for some HCM computations).
>
> 6. **Skipping the "units check" step:** KTU examiners reward clear step-by-step solutions. Always write the unit after every numerical answer.
>
> 7. **Mixing 30 HV and 1st HV:** 30 HV is the *30th highest*, not the *highest* (1st). Designing for the 1st HV is uneconomical; KTU questions frequently test this conceptual clarity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Macroscopic (Volume) Studies**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Traffic Volume:** Number of vehicles passing a point per unit time (veh/h or vpd).
- ☐ **ADT:** Total volume in a period (1 day to < 1 year) ÷ number of days.
- ☐ **AADT:** Total annual volume ÷ 365.
- ☐ **PHV:** Maximum hourly volume in a day.
- ☐ **DHV:** 30th highest hourly volume of the year (or 50th HV in urban areas).
- ☐ **30 HV:** Volume exceeded only 29 times in the year.
- ☐ **PHF:** $HV / (4 \times V_{15,\text{peak}})$, range 0.25–1.0.
- ☐ **K-factor:** $PHV / AADT$.
- ☐ **D-factor:** Peak directional PHV / Total PHV.
- ☐ **PCU:** Standard vehicle unit for heterogeneous traffic conversion.

### Essential Equations
- ☐ $q = k \cdot u$ (Fundamental flow equation)
- ☐ $q = 3600 / \bar{h}$ (Flow from headway in seconds)
- ☐ $k = 1000 / \bar{s}$ (Density from spacing in metres)
- ☐ $AADT = V_i \times H_i \times D_i \times M_i$ (Expansion formula)

### Practical Knowledge
- ☐ Manual counting → classified data, short duration, intersections.
- ☐ Automatic counting → continuous, ADT/AADT, permanent stations.
- ☐ Volume varies: hourly, daily, monthly, yearly.
- ☐ Expansion factors convert short counts to AADT.
- ☐ 30 HV balances economy and adequacy of design.

### Typical KTU Marks Distribution
- ☐ Part A: 1 question × 3 marks = 3 marks (definition/short problem).
- ☐ Part B: 1 question × 14 marks (with internal choice, sub-parts 7+7).
- ☐ Module 3 contributes ~20% of total marks in PCCET502.

> **Final Tip:** For KTU 2024 Scheme, focus on the *derivation* of the fundamental equation and the *expansion factor methodology* — these are the highest-weightage, most frequently tested topics in macroscopic volume studies.
