<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Traffic Stream Characteristics

The **traffic stream** is defined as a group of vehicles traveling in a defined directional flow along a roadway, treated as a continuous fluid-like body for analytical purposes. The behavior of this stream is quantified using two fundamentally different approaches:

1. **Macroscopic Approach** – Treats traffic as a whole (like a fluid), measuring aggregate quantities.
2. **Microscopic Approach** – Studies individual vehicle interactions and their relative positions in time and space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The macroscopic parameters are **Speed (u)**, **Density (k)**, and **Flow (q)**.
> The microscopic parameters are **Time Headway (hₜ)** and **Space Headway (hₛ)**.
> The fundamental relation connecting them is **q = u × k = 1/hₜ = u/hₛ**.

---

## 1.2 Macroscopic Characteristics of Traffic Stream

### (a) Speed (u)
The rate of motion of vehicles, expressed as the distance traveled per unit time.

- **Time Mean Speed (uₜ)**: Arithmetic mean of speeds of all vehicles passing a point over a specified time interval.
- **Space Mean Speed (uₛ)**: Harmonic mean of speeds; equal to the length of the roadway segment divided by the average travel time of vehicles traversing it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Units:**
> - Speed: **km/hr** (or m/s in SI)
> - Time Mean Speed: **uₜ = (1/n) Σ uᵢ**
> - Space Mean Speed: **uₛ = n / Σ(1/uᵢ)**
> where *n* = number of vehicles observed.

### (b) Density (k)
The number of vehicles occupying a unit length of roadway at a given instant.
- **Units: vehicles/km** (commonly abbreviated as **veh/km**)
- **Jam Density (kⱼ)**: Maximum density when vehicles are bumper-to-bumper and speed = 0.

### (c) Flow (q) or Volume (v)
The number of vehicles passing a point or cross-section per unit time.
- **Units: vehicles/hour (vph or veh/hr)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine a river. *Flow* is like the amount of water passing a point per second, *density* is the number of fish per meter of river, and *speed* is how fast the fish swim. The product **q = u × k** mirrors **Discharge = Velocity × Area** in fluid mechanics.

---

## 1.3 Microscopic Characteristics of Traffic Stream

### (a) Time Headway (hₜ)
The time interval between successive vehicles crossing a fixed point, measured from the same reference point on each vehicle (typically the front bumper).

- **Units: seconds (s)**

### (b) Space Headway (hₛ)
The distance between successive vehicles, measured front-to-front (or rear-to-front) at a given instant.

- **Units: meters (m)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse Relationship between Macroscopic and Microscopic Parameters:**
> - Flow q = 3600 / hₜ (when hₜ is in seconds and q is in veh/hr)
> - Density k = 1000 / hₛ (when hₛ is in meters and k is in veh/km)

---

## 1.4 Fundamental Diagram Concept

A **Fundamental Diagram of Traffic Flow** is a graphical representation of the relationships between the three macroscopic parameters (u, k, q). It captures the **steady-state behavior** of traffic and is the cornerstone of traffic flow theory.

> [!NOTE]
> The three relationships plotted are:
> 1. **Speed–Density (u–k)** relationship
> 2. **Density–Flow (k–q)** relationship
> 3. **Speed–Flow (u–q)** relationship
>
> All three are derived from the **fundamental equation: q = u × k**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Macroscopic Parameters

### Speed – The Two Variants
- **Time Mean Speed (uₜ)**: Easy to measure with a stopwatch at a fixed point. Tends to **overestimate** the average because faster vehicles are observed more frequently.
- **Space Mean Speed (uₛ)**: Represents the actual travel speed over a length of road. **Always less than or equal to uₜ** (Mathematically: uₛ ≤ uₜ).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** The relation between uₜ and uₛ is:
> uₜ = uₛ + (σ² / uₛ), where σ² is the variance of speeds.
> For homogeneous traffic, uₛ ≈ uₜ.

### Density – The "Congestion" Indicator
- Density is the **most difficult** macroscopic parameter to measure directly (requires aerial observation or detectors over a stretch).
- It is a **static / instantaneous** parameter (unlike flow and speed which are measured over time).

### Flow – The "Service Rate"
- Flow is the most commonly measured parameter in practice (using loop detectors or manual counts).
- **Capacity (qₘₐₓ)**: The maximum sustainable flow rate on a given roadway.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Microscopic Parameters

### Time Headway (hₜ)
- Measured between **front bumpers** of successive vehicles at a fixed point.
- **Minimum hₜ ≈ 1.5 to 2.0 seconds** in normal traffic (driver perception-reaction time).
- As hₜ decreases → density increases → flow increases (until optimum).

### Space Headway (hₛ)
- Measured between **front bumpers** of two successive vehicles at a given instant.
- **Minimum hₛ ≈ 5 to 7 m** (vehicle length + safe stopping distance).
- At jam density: hₛ = average vehicle length ≈ 5–6 m.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas, units, and boundary conditions required for KTU 2024 ESE**

| **Parameter / Concept** | **Formula** | **Units / Boundary** |
|---|---|---|
| Fundamental flow relation | $q = u \times k$ | $q$ in veh/hr, $u$ in km/hr, $k$ in veh/km |
| Time mean speed | $u_t = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} u_i$ | km/hr |
| Space mean speed | $u_s = \dfrac{n}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (1/u_i)}$ | km/hr |
| Time Headway to Flow | $q = \dfrac{3600}{h_t}$ | $h_t$ in s, $q$ in veh/hr |
| Space Headway to Density | $k = \dfrac{1000}{h_s}$ | $h_s$ in m, $k$ in veh/km |
| Relation between hₛ and hₜ | $h_s = u \times h_t$ | $u$ in m/s, $h_t$ in s, $h_s$ in m |
| Greenshields speed–density | $u = u_f \left(1 - \dfrac{k}{k_j}\right)$ | $u_f$ = free speed, $k_j$ = jam density |
| Greenshields flow–density | $q = u_f \cdot k - \dfrac{u_f}{k_j}\cdot k^2$ | Parabolic |
| Greenshields speed–flow | $q = k_j \cdot u - \dfrac{k_j}{u_f}\cdot u^2$ | Parabolic (inverted) |
| Maximum flow (capacity) | $q_{max} = \dfrac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}$ | veh/hr |
| Speed at capacity | $u_m = \dfrac{u_f}{2}$ | km/hr |
| Density at capacity | $k_m = \dfrac{k_j}{2}$ | veh/km |
| uₜ vs uₛ relation | $u_t = u_s + \dfrac{\sigma^2}{u_s}$ | $\sigma^2$ = variance of spot speeds |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real-World Applications

1. **Roadway Design**: Greenshield's model helps in determining lane capacity and Level of Service (LOS).
2. **Traffic Signal Design**: Saturation flow rate is derived using time headway values at signalized intersections.
3. **Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS)**: Real-time density and flow measurements use loop detectors and the same macroscopic relationships.
4. **Highway Capacity Manual (IRC: 64 & HCM 2010)**: Built fundamentally on speed-flow-density relationships.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Relationship q = u × k

Consider a stream of vehicles moving at a uniform speed *u* with density *k* over a short segment of length *L* (in km).

**Step 1:** The number of vehicles in segment *L* is:
$$
N = k \times L
$$

**Step 2:** The time taken by this platoon to cross a downstream point is:
$$
t = \dfrac{L}{u} \quad (\text{hours, since } u \text{ is in km/hr})
$$

**Step 3:** The flow (vehicles per hour) is:
$$
q = \dfrac{N}{t} = \dfrac{k \times L}{L / u} = u \times k
$$

**Conclusion:**
$$
\boxed{q = u \times k}
$$
> This is the **fundamental equation of traffic flow** — analogous to *Q = A × V* in fluid mechanics.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Relation Between Time Headway and Flow

Consider vehicles crossing a fixed point at equal time intervals of *hₜ* seconds.

**Step 1:** In 1 hour = 3600 seconds, the number of such intervals is:
$$
n = \dfrac{3600}{h_t}
$$

**Step 2:** By definition, the number of vehicles crossing per hour is the flow:
$$
q = \dfrac{3600}{h_t}
$$

**Conclusion:**
$$
\boxed{q = \dfrac{3600}{h_t} \;\; (\text{veh/hr})}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Relation Between Space Headway and Density

Consider vehicles spaced at a uniform distance *hₛ* meters (front-to-front) along a road.

**Step 1:** In a length of 1000 meters (1 km), the number of such spacing intervals is:
$$
n = \dfrac{1000}{h_s}
$$

**Step 2:** This number represents the density in vehicles per km:
$$
k = \dfrac{1000}{h_s}
$$

**Conclusion:**
$$
\boxed{k = \dfrac{1000}{h_s} \;\; (\text{veh/km})}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Relation Between Space Headway and Time Headway

**Step 1:** Start with the speed-time-distance relationship:
$$
u = \dfrac{h_s}{h_t}
$$

**Step 2:** Rearranging:
$$
\boxed{h_s = u \times h_t}
$$
> *Important:* Here, *u* must be in **m/s** (since hₛ is in meters and hₜ in seconds).
> Conversion: $u \text{ (m/s)} = u \text{ (km/hr)} / 3.6$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Macroscopic vs Microscopic Parameters

| **Basis** | **Macroscopic Parameters** | **Microscopic Parameters** |
|---|---|---|
| Nature of measurement | Aggregate / collective | Individual / pair-wise |
| Parameters | u, k, q | hₛ, hₜ |
| Measurement ease | Flow & speed are easy; density is hard | Easy to measure from video/photos |
| Variability | Low (smoothed out) | High (vehicle-to-vehicle) |
| Used in | Macroscopic traffic flow models | Car-following models |
| Application | Highway capacity, LOS | Microscopic simulation, safety analysis |
| Time dependence | Average over a time period | Instantaneous measurement |

---

## 3.6 Greenshield's Model — Detailed Analysis (Without Derivation)

**Model Assumption:** Speed decreases **linearly** as density increases.

**Mathematical Form:**
$$
u = u_f - \dfrac{u_f}{k_j} \cdot k
$$

Or equivalently:
$$
u = u_f \left(1 - \dfrac{k}{k_j}\right)
$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
- When $k = 0$ (no vehicles): $u = u_f$ (free flow speed, maximum)
- When $k = k_j$ (jam density): $u = 0$ (vehicles stop)

### (a) Derivation of the Flow–Density Equation

Substituting $u = u_f\left(1 - \dfrac{k}{k_j}\right)$ into $q = u \times k$:

$$
q = k \times u_f\left(1 - \dfrac{k}{k_j}\right)
$$

$$
q = u_f \cdot k - \dfrac{u_f}{k_j} \cdot k^2
$$

$$
\boxed{q = u_f \cdot k - \dfrac{u_f}{k_j} \cdot k^2}
$$

This is a **parabolic** equation in *k*. Setting $\dfrac{dq}{dk} = 0$ gives the maximum flow.

### (b) Derivation of Maximum Flow (Capacity, qₘₐₓ)

Differentiating the q–k equation with respect to *k*:
$$
\dfrac{dq}{dk} = u_f - \dfrac{2 u_f}{k_j} \cdot k
$$

Setting $\dfrac{dq}{dk} = 0$:
$$
u_f - \dfrac{2 u_f}{k_j} \cdot k_m = 0
$$

$$
k_m = \dfrac{k_j}{2}
$$

Substituting $k_m$ back into the q–k equation:
$$
q_{max} = u_f \cdot \dfrac{k_j}{2} - \dfrac{u_f}{k_j} \cdot \left(\dfrac{k_j}{2}\right)^2
$$

$$
q_{max} = \dfrac{u_f \cdot k_j}{2} - \dfrac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{max} = \dfrac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4}}
$$

### (c) Derivation of Speed at Maximum Flow (uₘ)

Using Greenshield's speed–density equation at $k = k_m = k_j / 2$:
$$
u_m = u_f \left(1 - \dfrac{k_j/2}{k_j}\right) = u_f \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2}\right) = \dfrac{u_f}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{u_m = \dfrac{u_f}{2}}
$$

### (d) Derivation of the Speed–Flow Equation

From $u = u_f\left(1 - \dfrac{k}{k_j}\right)$:
$$
k = k_j \left(1 - \dfrac{u}{u_f}\right)
$$

Substituting into $q = u \times k$:
$$
q = u \cdot k_j \left(1 - \dfrac{u}{u_f}\right)
$$

$$
q = k_j \cdot u - \dfrac{k_j}{u_f} \cdot u^2
$$

$$
\boxed{q = k_j \cdot u - \dfrac{k_j}{u_f} \cdot u^2}
$$

This is an **inverted parabolic** equation in *u*.

### (e) Greenshield's Model – Summary of Critical Values

| **Condition** | **Density (k)** | **Speed (u)** | **Flow (q)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Free flow (k = 0) | $0$ | $u_f$ | $0$ |
| **At Capacity** | $k_j/2$ | $u_f/2$ | $u_f k_j / 4$ |
| Jam condition (u = 0) | $k_j$ | $0$ | $0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The roadway operates at maximum flow (capacity) at a **midway point** — both at extremely low and extremely high densities, the flow approaches zero.

---

## 3.7 Step-by-Step Numerical Worked Example (Greenshield's Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Style Numerical:** Practice this format before the exam.

**Given:**
- Free mean speed $u_f = 80$ km/hr
- Jam density $k_j = 100$ veh/km

**Find:**
(a) The relationship between speed and density.
(b) The relationship between flow and density.
(c) Maximum flow (capacity) and corresponding speed and density.

### Solution:

**Part (a):** Speed–Density Relationship
$$
u = u_f\left(1 - \dfrac{k}{k_j}\right) = 80\left(1 - \dfrac{k}{100}\right) = 80 - 0.8k
$$

**Part (b):** Flow–Density Relationship
$$
q = u \cdot k = (80 - 0.8k)\cdot k = 80k - 0.8k^2
$$

**Part (c):** Maximum flow and corresponding conditions
$$
q_{max} = \dfrac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4} = \dfrac{80 \times 100}{4} = 2000 \;\; \text{veh/hr}
$$
$$
u_m = \dfrac{u_f}{2} = \dfrac{80}{2} = 40 \;\; \text{km/hr}
$$
$$
k_m = \dfrac{k_j}{2} = \dfrac{100}{2} = 50 \;\; \text{veh/km}
$$

**Verification:** $q_{max} = u_m \times k_m = 40 \times 50 = 2000$ veh/hr ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Traffic Stream Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Traffic Stream] --> B[Macroscopic Characteristics]
    A --> C[Microscopic Characteristics]
    
    B --> B1[Speed u]
    B --> B2[Density k]
    B --> B3[Flow q]
    
    C --> C1[Time Headway ht]
    C --> C2[Space Headway hs]
    
    B1 --> D[Fundamental Equation q = u x k]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    
    C1 --> E[Inverse Relations]
    C2 --> E
    
    E --> E1[q = 3600 / ht]
    E --> E2[k = 1000 / hs]
    E --> E3[hs = u x ht]
    
    D --> F[Fundamental Diagrams]
    E --> F
    
    F --> F1[Speed Density u vs k]
    F --> F2[Density Flow k vs q]
    F --> F3[Speed Flow u vs q]
    
    F1 --> G[Greenshields Model]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    
    G --> G1[uf = free mean speed]
    G --> G2[kj = jam density]
    G --> G3[qmax = uf x kj / 4]
    G --> G4[um = uf / 2]
    G --> G5[km = kj / 2]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Greenshield's Model Logical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Parameters] --> B[uf Free Speed]
    A --> C[kj Jam Density]
    
    B --> D[Speed Density Equation]
    C --> D
    D --> D1[Linear Decreasing]
    
    B --> E[Flow Density Equation]
    C --> E
    E --> E1[Parabolic]
    
    B --> F[Speed Flow Equation]
    C --> F
    F --> F1[Inverted Parabolic]
    
    D1 --> G[Critical Analysis]
    E1 --> G
    F1 --> G
    
    G --> G1[Find dq/dk = 0]
    G1 --> G2[km = kj / 2]
    G2 --> G3[um = uf / 2]
    G3 --> G4[qmax = uf x kj / 4]
    
    G4 --> H[Output Capacity and Critical Values]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Time-Space Diagram Concept for Headways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Time Space Diagram] --> P2[Trajectory of Vehicle 1]
    P1 --> P3[Trajectory of Vehicle 2]
    P1 --> P4[Trajectory of Vehicle 3]
    
    P2 --> Q1[Vertical Distance Between Trajectories at Fixed Time = Space Headway hs]
    P3 --> Q1
    P4 --> Q1
    
    P2 --> Q2[Horizontal Distance Between Trajectories at Fixed Point = Time Headway ht]
    P3 --> Q2
    P4 --> Q2
    
    Q1 --> R[hs in meters]
    Q2 --> S[ht in seconds]
    
    R --> T[Relation hs = u x ht]
    S --> T
    
    T --> U[Macroscopic q = u / hs]
    T --> V[Macroscopic k = 1 / hs]
    T --> W[Macroscopic q = 1 / ht]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Time Headway and Space Headway. How are they related to macroscopic traffic flow parameters?**

#### Model Answer:
**Time Headway (hₜ):** The time interval between two successive vehicles crossing a fixed point, measured between corresponding points (e.g., front bumpers) of the vehicles. Unit: **seconds**.

**Space Headway (hₛ):** The distance between two successive vehicles, measured between corresponding points (front-to-front) at a given instant. Unit: **meters**.

**Relations:**
- Flow: $q = \dfrac{3600}{h_t}$ (veh/hr)
- Density: $k = \dfrac{1000}{h_s}$ (veh/km)
- $h_s = u \times h_t$ (with *u* in m/s)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What are the macroscopic characteristics of a traffic stream? State the fundamental equation relating them.**

#### Model Answer:
The three macroscopic characteristics of a traffic stream are:
1. **Speed (u)** – Distance travelled per unit time (km/hr).
2. **Density (k)** – Number of vehicles per unit length of road (veh/km).
3. **Flow (q)** – Number of vehicles passing a point per unit time (veh/hr).

**Fundamental Equation:**
$$
\boxed{q = u \times k}
$$

> **Alternative forms:**
> - $q = u / h_s$ (since $k = 1/h_s$)
> - $q = 3600 / h_t$ (direct from time headway)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Question Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the microscopic characteristics of a traffic stream. Derive the fundamental relations between the microscopic and macroscopic parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A roadway has space mean speed of 60 km/hr and density of 20 veh/km. Determine the flow, time headway, and space headway on the road. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**
- Microscopic parameters are **Time Headway (hₜ)** and **Space Headway (hₛ)**.
- The derivations shown in **Section 3.2, 3.3, 3.4** of these notes must be reproduced step-by-step:
  - $q = 3600 / h_t$
  - $k = 1000 / h_s$
  - $h_s = u \times h_t$
- Connect these to the macroscopic relation $q = u \times k$.

**Part (b):**

**Given:** $u_s = 60$ km/hr, $k = 20$ veh/km

**Step 1 — Flow:**
$$
q = u \times k = 60 \times 20 = 1200 \;\; \text{veh/hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Space Headway:**
$$
h_s = \dfrac{1000}{k} = \dfrac{1000}{20} = 50 \;\; \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Time Headway:**
Convert speed: $u = 60 / 3.6 = 16.667$ m/s

$$
h_t = \dfrac{h_s}{u} = \dfrac{50}{16.667} = 3.0 \;\; \text{s}
$$

**Verification:** $q = 3600 / 3.0 = 1200$ veh/hr ✓

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Sample Paper]`

**(a)** State and explain Greenshield's linear speed-density model. Using this model, derive the equations for: (i) flow-density, (ii) speed-flow, and (iii) the maximum flow conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** On a two-lane highway, the free mean speed is observed to be 90 km/hr and the jam density is 120 veh/km. Using Greenshield's model, find: (i) the maximum flow, (ii) the corresponding speed and density, and (iii) the flow when density is 90 veh/km. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**
- State Greenshield's model: $u = u_f \left(1 - \dfrac{k}{k_j}\right)$
- Derive the three relationships and maximum flow conditions (as in **Section 3.6** of these notes).

**Part (b):**

**Given:** $u_f = 90$ km/hr, $k_j = 120$ veh/km

**(i) Maximum flow:**
$$
q_{max} = \dfrac{u_f \cdot k_j}{4} = \dfrac{90 \times 120}{4} = 2700 \;\; \text{veh/hr}
$$

**(ii) Speed and density at maximum flow:**
$$
k_m = \dfrac{k_j}{2} = \dfrac{120}{2} = 60 \;\; \text{veh/km}
$$
$$
u_m = \dfrac{u_f}{2} = \dfrac{90}{2} = 45 \;\; \text{km/hr}
$$

**(iii) Flow at k = 90 veh/km:**
$$
u = u_f\left(1 - \dfrac{k}{k_j}\right) = 90\left(1 - \dfrac{90}{120}\right) = 90 \times 0.25 = 22.5 \;\; \text{km/hr}
$$
$$
q = u \times k = 22.5 \times 90 = 2025 \;\; \text{veh/hr}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Many students forget to convert km/hr to m/s when computing $h_t = h_s / u$. Always remember to divide by 3.6.
> 2. **Greenshield's Formula Sign:** The model is $u = u_f - (u_f / k_j) \cdot k$, **not** $u = u_f + (\ldots)$. Speed **decreases** with density.
> 3. **Maximum Flow Location:** $k_m$ is **not** at the average of k; it is exactly at $k_j/2$. A common student error is writing $k_m = k_j/3$ or $k_j/4$.
> 4. **Flow = 0 Conditions:** Both at $k = 0$ and $k = k_j$, flow is zero. Plot this on the parabola in your exam answer for full marks.
> 5. **Boundary Conditions:** Always verify your solution satisfies $u \geq 0$ and $0 \leq k \leq k_j$. If not, the model is being misapplied.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE 2024**

### 🔑 Must-Know Definitions
- **Speed (u):** Time-mean speed (at a point) vs Space-mean speed (over a length).
- **Density (k):** Vehicles per km; hardest to measure directly.
- **Flow (q):** Vehicles per hour; the most measured parameter.
- **Time Headway (hₜ):** Time gap between vehicles at a point (seconds).
- **Space Headway (hₛ):** Distance gap between vehicles at an instant (meters).

### 🔑 Fundamental Equations
- $q = u \times k$ (the master equation)
- $q = 3600 / h_t$
- $k = 1000 / h_s$
- $h_s = u \times h_t$ (u in m/s)

### 🔑 Greenshield's Model — Critical Values
- Speed–Density: $u = u_f(1 - k/k_j)$ (linear, decreasing)
- Flow–Density: $q = u_f \cdot k - (u_f/k_j) \cdot k^2$ (parabolic)
- Speed–Flow: $q = k_j \cdot u - (k_j/u_f) \cdot u^2$ (inverted parabolic)
- **$q_{max} = u_f \cdot k_j / 4$** ← most-tested formula
- $u_m = u_f / 2$, $k_m = k_j / 2$

### 🔑 Boundary Behaviour
- At $k = 0$: $u = u_f$, $q = 0$ (free flow, no vehicles)
- At $k = k_j$: $u = 0$, $q = 0$ (jam, no movement)
- At $k = k_j/2$: $q = q_{max}$ (capacity, maximum throughput)

### 🔑 Two Variants of Speed (Common Confusion)
- $u_t = (1/n)\sum u_i$ (arithmetic mean, at a point)
- $u_s = n / \sum(1/u_i)$ (harmonic mean, over a length)
- Always: $u_s \leq u_t$

### 🔑 Key Conversion
- $1 \text{ km/hr} = 1/3.6 \text{ m/s} = 5/18 \text{ m/s}$
- $1 \text{ m/s} = 3.6 \text{ km/hr}$

### 🔑 Engineering Applications (for 1–2 mark sub-questions)
- Capacity design
- LOS determination
- Signal timing (saturation flow)
- Macroscopic vs Microscopic simulation models
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacity and Level of Service (Concept Only). Traffic Surveys: Data Collection and Analysis - Volume
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCET502 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Capacity and Level of Service (Concept Only). Traffic Surveys: Data Collection and Analysis - Volume # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Traffic Capacity > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Highway Capacity Manual, HCM Paradigm):** > **Traffic Capacity** is the maximum number of vehicles (or persons) that can reasonably be expected to traverse a point or a uniform section of a lane or roadway during a specified time period, usually one hour, under prevailing roadway, traffic, and control conditions. **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a highway like a water pipe. Just as a pipe can only carry a certain maximum flow rate of water (gallons per minute) before the pressure builds up and overflow occurs, a road can only carry a maximum number of vehicles per hour before traffic "spills over" into jams. The capacity is that critical flow threshold. ### 1.1.1 Types of Capacity (KTU Syllabus Favourite) > [!IMPORTANT] > The concept of capacity is **NOT** a single number — it is a family of three progressive thresholds. 1. **Basic Capacity (Theoretical Maximum):** - The maximum number of passenger cars that can pass a given point on a lane or roadway during one hour, under **ideal roadway and traffic conditions** (no trucks, no pedestrians, level grade, perfect lane width). - It is a *theoretical ceiling* that can **never be achieved** in the real world. 2. **Possible Capacity:** - The maximum number of vehicles that can pass a point on a roadway during one hour, under **prevailing roadway and traffic conditions**. - It is the *realistic maximum* under existing site conditions. 3. **Practical Capacity (Design Capacity):** - The maximum number of vehicles that can pass a point on a roadway during one hour, **without the traffic density being so great as to cause unreasonable delay, hazard, or restriction to the driver's freedom to manoeuvre**. - It is the value **engineers use for design purposes**. - Typically, Practical Capacity ≈ **50% of Basic Capacity** for uninterrupted flow facilities. > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick:** **B → P → P** : **B**asic → **P**ossible → **P**ractical. Think of it as *"Big Pipe → Possible Pipe → Practical Pipe"* — each step accounts for more real-world friction. ### 1.1.2 Factors Affecting Capacity | Category | Specific Factors | |----------|------------------| | **Roadway (Road) Factors** | Lane width, shoulder width, lateral clearance, grade, alignment (curves), surface condition, number of lanes | | **Traffic Factors** | Vehicle composition (PCU mix), directional split, lane distribution, driver skill, age of drivers | | **Environmental Factors** | Weather (rain, fog, snow), lighting (day/night), visibility | | **Control Factors** | Speed limit, signals, signs, one-way/two-way operation | --- ## 1.2 Level of Service (LOS) > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024):** > **Level of Service (LOS)** is a **qualitative measure** describing the operational conditions within a traffic stream, and their perception by motorists and/or passengers. It is generally described in terms of factors such as **speed, travel time, freedom to manoeuvre, traffic interruptions, comfort, convenience, and safety**. **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a restaurant. Even though the *capacity* (maximum seating) might be 100 people, the *experience* at 30 people (relaxed dining) versus 95 people (cramped, slow service) is vastly different. LOS is the **"experience rating"** of the road — not just the count of cars, but *how it feels to drive there*. ### 1.2.1 LOS Designations (A to F) The Highway Capacity Manual (HCM) defines **six levels of service**, designated **A through F**, with A being the *best* and F being the *worst*: | LOS | Description (Plain English) | Operating Condition | |-----|------------------------------|----------------------| | **A** | Free-flow, empty road | Maximum mobility, no restrictions | | **B** | Stable flow, light traffic | Minor friction between vehicles | | **C** | Stable flow, mid-range | Speeds near free-flow, manoeuvres restricted | | **D** | Approaching unstable flow | High density, small speed drops | | **E** | Unstable flow, near capacity | Stop-and-go, low speeds, no freedom to manoeuvre | | **F** | Breakdown / Forced flow | Stop-and-go jams, demand exceeds capacity | > [!WARNING] > **Key Distinction:** Capacity is a **QUANTITATIVE** (numerical) measure. Level of Service is a **QUALITATIVE** (descriptive/graded) measure. The two are *related* but *not the same* — a road at LOS E may not be at capacity, and a road at capacity is typically at LOS E or F. ### 1.2.2 The Capacity-LOS Relationship > Capacity corresponds to the **upper boundary of LOS E**. The transition from LOS E to LOS F represents the **breakdown of flow** — the moment when demand exceeds capacity. > [!TIP] > **Design Rule (KTU/IRC):** Highways are typically **designed to operate at LOS C or D** at the design hour, leaving headroom for peak surges and future growth. --- ## 1.3 Traffic Volume Studies > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** > **Traffic Volume** is the **number of vehicles (or persons) passing a point on a roadway during a specified time interval**, usually expressed as **vehicles per hour (vph)** or **vehicles per day (vpd)**. **Intuitive Analogy:** Traffic volume is the **"headcount at the door"** of a building. Just as a building manager counts how many people enter per hour to plan exits, fire safety, and staffing, traffic engineers count vehicles to plan lane numbers, signal timings, and pavement design. ### 1.3.1 Volume Terminology (KTU Standard) | Term | Expansion | Meaning | |------|-----------|---------| | **AADT** | Annual Average Daily Traffic | Total yearly volume ÷ 365 days | | **ADT** | Average Daily Traffic | Total volume over a period ÷ number of days in that period | | **DHV** | Design Hourly Volume | The hourly volume used for design (often 30th highest hour) | | **PHF** | Peak Hour Factor | Ratio of hourly volume to peak 15-min rate × 4 | | **PCU** | Passenger Car Unit | A common unit to convert mixed traffic into equivalent passenger cars | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Capacity — The Underlying Logic The reasoning chain behind the **three-tier capacity model** (Basic → Possible → Practical) is built on progressively relaxing assumptions: - **Step 1 — Idealise the system:** Strip away all real-world complications (perfect geometry, only cars, perfect drivers, daylight, no weather). What remains is the **Basic Capacity** — the absolute upper bound. - **Step 2 — Apply site constraints:** Now bring back the real roadway and traffic conditions (lane width, grade, vehicle mix). This gives **Possible Capacity** — what *could* physically pass. - **Step 3 — Apply the human-factors cushion:** Drivers refuse to operate in a "crush" condition. Reduce Possible Capacity by a **Level of Service factor** so that travel remains acceptable. This gives **Practical Capacity**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mathematical Relationship:** > $$\text{Possible Capacity} = \text{Basic Capacity} \times \prod (\text{Adjustment Factors})$$ > $$\text{Practical Capacity} = \text{Possible Capacity} \times \text{LOS Factor}$$ --- ## 2.2 Level of Service — The Six-Tier Logic Each LOS level is defined by a **range of service measure values** (speed, density, or v/c ratio) that correspond to a perceived quality of service: - **LOS A → B → C:** Free-flow zone. Density is low, speed is high, and the **v/c ratio (volume-to-capacity ratio)** is small (< 0.50 typically). - **LOS D:** The "design sweet spot." Volume is high but stable; v/c ≈ 0.80–0.90. - **LOS E:** Operating at the *edge* of capacity. v/c ≈ 1.00. Any perturbation triggers breakdown. - **LOS F:** Demand > Capacity. Forced flow, queue formation, stop-and-go waves. > [!TIP] > **Service Measure Used (as per HCM):** > - **Multilane Highways / Freeways** → **Density** (pc/km/ln) > - **Two-Lane Highways** → **Percent Time Spent Following (PTSF) + Average Travel Speed** > - **Signalised Intersections** → **Average Control Delay (sec/veh)** --- ## 2.3 Traffic Volume — Why We Measure It Volume data is the **input fuel** for nearly every transportation engineering decision: 1. **Highway Design** → Determines number of lanes, pavement thickness (ESAL counts). 2. **Intersection Design** → Determines signal phasing, lane widening. 3. **Capacity & LOS Analysis** → Compare volume against capacity to assign LOS. 4. **Economic Justification** → Cost-benefit analysis of road projects. 5. **Prioritisation of Improvements** → Identifies the most congested corridors. 6. **Trend Analysis & Forecasting** → Growth rate estimation for future design years. --- ## 2.4 Methods of Volume Count > [!NOTE] > Volume counts are classified by **duration** and **method**. ### 2.4.1 By Duration | Type | Duration | Use | |------|----------|-----| | **Cordon Count** | Short (peak hours) | Vehicles entering/leaving a specific area (CBD) | | **Screen-line Count** | Short | Counts across an imaginary line (river, railway) | | **Peak Hour Count** | 1 hour | Intersection design, signal timing | | **24-Hour Count** | 1 day | Daily volume patterns | | **7-Day Count** | 1 week | Weekly variation, weekly expansion factors | | **Annual Count** | Continuous | AADT determination (permanent count stations) | ### 2.4.2 By Method (Manual vs. Automatic) | Method | Equipment | Best For | |--------|-----------|----------| | **Manual Count** | Tally sheets, clickers | Classified counts, turning movements, pedestrian counts | | **Pneumatic Tube** | Rubber tubes + counter | Short-term volume, speed, classification | | **Inductive Loop** | Wire embedded in pavement | Permanent count stations, long-term AADT | | **Piezoelectric Sensor** | Pressure-sensitive | Vehicle classification, weight (WIM) | | **Video / Camera** | Computer vision | Conflict studies, trajectory analysis | | **Radar / LIDAR** | Microwave sensors | Speed + volume simultaneously | | **GPS Probe Data** | Floating car data | O-D, travel time, network-wide volume | --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---------|---------------| | 1 | $V = \dfrac{N}{t}$ | Basic volume count (vehicles per unit time) | | 2 | $\text{AADT} = \dfrac{\text{Total Annual Volume}}{365}$ | Annual average daily traffic | | 3 | $\text{ADT} = \dfrac{\text{Total Volume in Period}}{\text{No. of Days in Period}}$ | Average daily traffic over a defined period | | 4 | $\text{PHF} = \dfrac{\text{Hourly Volume}}{4 \times \text{Peak 15-min Volume}}$ | Peak Hour Factor — measures peaking within an hour | | 5 | $V_{15} = \dfrac{V_h}{4 \times \text{PHF}}$ | Peak 15-min flow rate (used in HCM for LOS) | | 6 | $\text{DHV} = k \times \text{AADT}$ | Design hourly volume (k ≈ 8%–12% for urban; 12%–18% for rural) | | 7 | $V_{\text{PCU}} = \sum_i (V_i \times \text{PCU}_i)$ | Total volume in PCU/hr (mixed traffic) | | 8 | $\text{v/c} = \dfrac{V}{\text{Capacity}}$ | Volume-to-capacity ratio (LOS indicator) | | 9 | $\text{Possible} = \text{Basic} \times \prod f_i$ | Capacity reduction by adjustment factors | | 10 | $\text{Practical} = \text{Possible} \times \text{LOS Factor}$ | Design capacity after LOS adjustment | > [!TIP] > **PCU Equivalents (IRC:64-1990, India) — Must memorise:** > > | Vehicle Type | PCU Factor | > |--------------|-----------| > | Car / Jeep / Van | **1.0** | > | Two-wheeler / Motorbike | **0.5** | > | Auto-rickshaw | **1.0** (or 0.5 in some codes) | > | Light Commercial Vehicle (LCV) | **1.5** | > | Truck / Bus (Heavy) | **3.0** | > | Cycle | **0.5** | > | Pedestrian | **0.5** (per IRC:103) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Check (KTU Valuation):** Always state units. Volume → *veh/hr* or *PCU/hr*. AADT → *veh/day*. PHF is **dimensionless**. Density → *pc/km/ln*. --- ## 2.6 Design Hourly Volume (DHV) — The KTU Favourite > [!NOTE] > The **30th Highest Hourly Volume (30 HV)** is the volume exceeded only 29 times in a year. It is the **de facto design hour** for rural highways in the USA (HCM) and India (IRC). **Reasoning chain:** - Using the absolute *Peak Hour* (1st HV) wastes money (over-design). - Using the *Average Hour* causes congestion (under-design). - The **30th HV** balances economics and adequacy — it represents a *high but not extreme* hour. > [!TIP] > **Rule of thumb:** 30th HV ≈ **15%** of AADT (for rural) and **8–10%** of AADT (for urban). --- ## 2.7 Peak Hour Factor (PHF) — Worked Insight PHF quantifies the **"burstiness"** of traffic within the peak hour: - If 15-min flow is uniform → PHF = 1.00 - If all flow is concentrated in one 15-min block → PHF = 0.25 **Engineering use:** PHF is needed to convert the hourly count into the **sub-hourly design flow rate** used in HCM signalised intersection analysis. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Peak Hour Factor (PHF) **Starting Premise:** The Peak Hour Factor captures the relationship between the *actual hourly volume* and the *peak 15-minute flow rate* (multiplied by 4 to represent what the hourly volume *would have been* if the peak 15 minutes had been sustained). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Let } V_h &= \text{Total volume during the peak hour} \\[4pt] V_{15} &= \text{Volume during the peak 15-min interval within that hour} \\[4pt] \text{Equivalent sustained hourly flow} &= 4 \times V_{15} \\[4pt] \text{Peak Hour Factor (PHF)} &= \dfrac{\text{Actual Hourly Volume}}{\text{Equivalent Sustained Hourly Flow}} \\[4pt] \text{PHF} &= \dfrac{V_h}{4 \times V_{15}} \quad \text{(dimensionless, 0.25 ≤ PHF ≤ 1.00)} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** - PHF = 1.0 → traffic is **perfectly uniform** throughout the hour. - PHF = 0.5 → peak 15-min carries **half** of the entire hour's volume (very "peaky"). **Reverse Application (finding peak 15-min flow from hourly volume):** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{15} &= \dfrac{V_h}{4 \times \text{PHF}} \end{aligned} $$ This is the value plugged into HCM intersection analysis as the **peak flow rate**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Design Hourly Volume from AADT **Starting Premise:** Not all hours of the year are equal. The engineer needs a single *design hour* volume that is high enough to handle peaks but not so extreme as to be wasteful. **Logic steps:** 1. Rank the **8760 hours of the year** in *descending order* of traffic volume. 2. The **1st HV** = annual peak (extreme, ~30–50 hours/year). 3. The **30th HV** = volume exceeded only 29 hours/year. 4. Empirically (US data, also adopted in IRC): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{30th HV} &\approx (0.12 \text{ to } 0.18) \times \text{AADT} \quad \text{(rural)} \\[4pt] \text{30th HV} &\approx (0.08 \text{ to } 0.12) \times \text{AADT} \quad \text{(urban)} \end{aligned} $$ 5. For **design purposes**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DHV} &= k \times \text{AADT}, \quad \text{where } k = \dfrac{\text{30th HV}}{\text{AADT}} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Mixed-Traffic Volume in PCU/hr **Starting Premise:** Roads carry a *heterogeneous* mix — cars, two-wheelers, buses, trucks. They occupy different road spaces and have different operational impacts. We must convert all of them to a **common unit — the Passenger Car Unit (PCU)**. **Logic steps:** 1. Let the traffic stream have *n* vehicle categories with counts $N_1, N_2, \ldots, N_n$. 2. Let each category have a PCU equivalent $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$. 3. The equivalent *passenger-car flow* is the **weighted sum**: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{PCU}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i \times p_i \\[4pt] &= N_{\text{car}} \times 1.0 + N_{\text{2W}} \times 0.5 + N_{\text{truck}} \times 3.0 + \ldots \end{aligned} $$ 4. **Unit conversion** if data is given in veh/day and we need PCU/hr: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{PCU/hr}} &= \dfrac{V_{\text{PCU/day}}}{24} \quad \text{(rough estimate)} \\[4pt] \text{or, better:} \quad V_{\text{PCU/hr}} &= V_{\text{PCU/day}} \times k \quad \text{where } k = \text{24-hr to peak-hr factor} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Relationship Between Capacity, Volume, and LOS **Starting Premise:** The Level of Service is fundamentally a measure of how "loaded" a road is, expressed as the **volume-to-capacity ratio (v/c)**. **Logic chain:** 1. Let $V$ = observed hourly volume (PCU/hr). 2. Let $C$ = capacity of the facility (PCU/hr). 3. The **degree of saturation**: $$ \begin{aligned} X &= \dfrac{V}{C} \quad \text{(dimensionless)} \end{aligned} $$ 4. **LOS mapping (typical thresholds for uninterrupted flow):** | v/c Ratio (X) | LOS | |:-------------:|:---:| | $X \leq 0.35$ | A | | $0.35 < X \leq 0.55$ | B | | $0.55 < X \leq 0.75$ | C | | $0.75 < X \leq 0.90$ | D | | $0.90 < X \leq 1.00$ | E | | $X > 1.00$ | F | 5. **LOS E corresponds to $X \approx 1.0$** (operating at capacity). 6. **LOS F corresponds to $X > 1.0$** (demand exceeds capacity — flow breakdown). > [!IMPORTANT] > Note: These v/c thresholds are **approximate** and vary by facility type. The HCM uses **density** (pc/km/ln) as the primary service measure for freeways and **delay** for intersections. KTU exam problems typically use **v/c ratio as a proxy** unless specified otherwise. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Demonstration (Inline Mini-Problem) **Problem:** During a peak hour, a count shows **Cars = 400, Two-wheelers = 300, Auto = 100, Buses = 20, Trucks = 50**. Peak 15-min volume = **250 veh**. Find the **PCU/hr volume** and the **Peak Hour Factor (PHF)**. **Solution Steps:** Step 1 — Compute PCU/hr: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{PCU/hr}} &= (400 \times 1.0) + (300 \times 0.5) + (100 \times 1.0) + (20 \times 3.0) + (50 \times 3.0) \\[4pt] &= 400 + 150 + 100 + 60 + 150 \\[4pt] &= \mathbf{860 \text{ PCU/hr}} \end{aligned} $$ Step 2 — Compute PHF (assuming peak 15-min = 250 veh): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PHF} &= \dfrac{V_h}{4 \times V_{15}} = \dfrac{400+300+100+20+50}{4 \times 250} = \dfrac{870}{1000} = \mathbf{0.87} \end{aligned} $$ Step 3 — Interpretation: PHF = 0.87 indicates **moderately peaked** traffic. Peak 15-min is higher than the rest of the hour. --- ## 3.6 Conversion Logic: When 15-min volume is given instead of hourly > [!IMPORTANT] > Many HCM problems give you the *15-min flow* and ask for hourly volume. The conversion is the **inverse** of PHF: $$ \begin{aligned} V_h &= \text{PHF} \times 4 \times V_{15} \end{aligned} $$ Be careful with units — $V_{15}$ is in **veh/15-min**, and $V_h$ comes out in **veh/hr** because we multiply by 4. # 4. The six LOS boxes hang below — they are *states* that a road can be in relative to Practical Capacity. --- ## 4.2 Diagram: Traffic Survey Methodology — Block Architecture This block diagram shows the **classification of traffic surveys** with Volume Studies highlighted, as required by the KTU syllabus. ```mermaid flowchart TD rootA["TRAFFIC SURVEYS"] rootA --> nodeB["VOLUME STUDIES"] rootA --> nodeC["SPEED STUDIES"] rootA --> nodeD["ORIGIN-DESTINATION STUDIES"] rootA --> nodeE["PARKING STUDIES"] rootA --> nodeF["ACCIDENT STUDIES"] nodeB --> nodeG["BY DURATION"] nodeB --> nodeH["BY METHOD"] nodeG --> nodeI["Cordon Count"] nodeG --> nodeJ["Screen-line Count"] nodeG --> nodeK["Peak Hour Count"] nodeG --> nodeL["24-Hour Count"] nodeG --> nodeM["Continuous Annual Count"] nodeH --> nodeN["Manual Tally"] nodeH --> nodeO["Pneumatic Tube"] nodeH --> nodeP["Inductive Loop"] nodeH --> nodeQ["Video Camera"] nodeH --> nodeR["Radar / LIDAR"] nodeH --> nodeS["GPS Probe Data"] nodeB --> nodeT["OUTPUT METRICS"] nodeT --> nodeU["AADT veh per day"] nodeT --> nodeV["ADT veh per day"] nodeT --> nodeW["DHV veh per hr"] nodeT --> nodeX["Peak Hour Factor"] nodeT --> nodeY["PCU per hr"] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Volume studies branch into *by duration* and *by method*, ultimately producing the *five key output metrics* every transportation engineer must know. --- ## 4.3 Diagram: Volume Count Presentation — Typical Formats This flowchart shows the **data processing pipeline** from raw field counts to published traffic statistics. ```mermaid flowchart LR rawA["Raw Count
