> **Formal KTU Definition (Harbour):** A harbour is a protected water area, either natural or artificially created, where ships can anchor safely for loading, unloading, and sheltering from rough sea conditions. It provides safe refuge to vessels during adverse weather.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Port):** A port is the commercial end of a harbour, equipped with all necessary facilities (quays, warehouses, cranes, transit sheds) for the reception, loading, unloading, and storage of cargo as well as embarkation and disembarkation of passengers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Dock):** A dock is an enclosed water space (or empty space) constructed in a port or harbour to facilitate the loading, unloading, building, or repairing of ships. Docks can hold water (Wet Docks) or be drained of water (Dry Docks) to expose the underwater hull of a vessel.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a **seaport as a multi-level parking garage for giant ships**.
> - The **harbour** is the open approach road + entrance to the garage.
> - The **port** is the building itself, with offices, ramps, and storage rooms (warehouses).
> - The **wet dock** is a covered, lock-controlled parking bay where ships float quietly while being loaded with goods (passengers).
> - The **dry dock** is the service bay — the water is drained out using pumps, and the ship's hull is lifted onto wooden/steel supports (keel blocks) so that mechanics can paint, weld, or repair the underwater body, exactly like a car being raised on a hydraulic lift.

### 1.3 Classification of Harbours (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Harbours are broadly classified based on **location, utility, and need for protection**.

| S.No. | Classification Basis | Type | Description |
|:-----:|:---------------------|:-----|:------------|
| 1 | **Based on Location** | Natural Harbour | Naturally protected by geographical features (e.g., Mumbai Port) |
| 2 | | Artificial Harbour | Protection created by breakwaters and artificial structures |
| 3 | **Based on Utility** | Commercial Harbour | Handles general cargo, containers, bulk goods |
| 4 | | Fishery Harbour | Dedicated to fishing vessels and processing |
| 5 | | Military/Naval Harbour | Reserved for naval/defence operations |
| 6 | | Refuge Harbour | Provides shelter during storms (no commercial activity) |
| 7 | **Based on Tide** | Open Port/Tidal Harbour | Accessible only at high tide |
| 8 | | Closed Port/Non-Tidal Harbour | Uses lock gates to maintain constant water level |

### 1.4 Classification of Docks — Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Docks are classified into **two major categories**:
> 1. **Wet Docks** (also called Impounded Docks) — Always filled with water.
> 2. **Dry Docks** — Designed to be emptied for ship repair/construction.

The term **Dock** may also refer to the **berthing structure (quay/wharf)** itself in modern port terminology.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functions of Docks

> [!NOTE]
> A dock is not merely a basin — it is a **complete engineering system** with multiple overlapping functions. The following are the essential functions as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

1. **Berthing of Ships** — Provides a safe mooring location where ships can tie up alongside quay walls or jetties.
2. **Loading & Unloading of Cargo** — Equipped with cranes, gantries, and conveyors for efficient transfer of goods.
3. **Embarkation and Disembarkation of Passengers** — Passenger docks have terminal buildings, gangways, and waiting halls.
4. **Ship Repair and Maintenance** — Dry docks and floating docks allow the underwater hull to be inspected, cleaned, painted, and repaired.
5. **Ship Construction** — Building docks (a type of dry dock) are used for assembling new vessels from the keel up.
6. **Refuge During Storms** — Closed docks with lock gates maintain calm water even during rough weather at sea.
7. **Storage and Warehousing** — Transit sheds, container yards, and bonded warehouses are integrated into the dock complex.
8. **Bunkering and Supply** — Facilities for refuelling, freshwater supply, and provisioning of ships.

## 2.2 Types of Docks — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Wet Docks (Impounded Docks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Wet Dock** is a basin in which the water level is maintained approximately constant by means of a lock or other gate, so that vessels remain afloat at all states of the tide.

**Operational Logic:**
- Step 1: Ship enters via a **lock gate** at high tide.
- Step 2: The lock gate closes behind the ship.
- Step 3: Water inside is maintained at a fixed level by the **impounding system**.
- Step 4: Ship remains floating at a constant draft regardless of external tide.

**Advantages:**
- Loading/unloading continues uninterrupted regardless of tide.
- Ships are protected from wave action and silting.
- Provides calm water for accurate cargo handling.

**Disadvantages:**
- High initial cost of construction (lock gates, caissons).
- Lock operations cause delay in ship movement.
- Requires continuous dredging of entrance channel.

### 2.2.2 Dry Docks — The Focus of this Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Dry Dock** is a dock from which water can be pumped out to expose the underwater portions of a ship's hull, allowing inspection, cleaning, painting, and repair.

## 2.3 Types of Dry Docks (High-Yield KTU Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasises the **classification of dry docks**. Master this matrix.

| Type | Construction | Operation | Best Use |
|:-----|:-------------|:----------|:---------|
| **Graving Dock** (or Dry Dock proper) | Excavated basin on land with side walls, floor, and a gate | Water pumped out; ship rests on keel blocks | Major ship repair, construction |
| **Floating Dry Dock** | A pontoon-like structure with side walls that can be submerged and lifted | Ballasted down, ship floated in, then deballasted to lift ship out of water | Emergency repair, remote locations, mobile units |
| **Marine Railway** (Syncrolift) | Inclined rail track extending into the sea; cradle moves on rails | Ship is hauled up the inclined plane on a wheeled cradle | Small to medium vessels |
| **Lift Dock** | Platform lifts ship vertically out of water using hydraulic rams | Ship positioned over platform; platform raised | Fast lifting of medium vessels |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are essential for solving numerical/design problems on dry docks in the KTU 2024 University Examination.

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-----:|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $L_d = L_s + (2 \times 0.05L_s)$ | Length of dry dock (L_d) in terms of ship's length (L_s) | metres |
| 2 | $B_d = B_s + (2 \times 0.10B_s)$ | Breadth of dry dock in terms of ship's breadth | metres |
| 3 | $D_w = D_s + h_f + h_c$ | Depth of water required; D_s = ship draft, h_f = freeboard, h_c = clearance | metres |
| 4 | $D_d = D_w + h_t$ | Total depth of dry dock below coping; h_t = tidal variation allowance | metres |
| 5 | $V = L_d \times B_d \times D_w$ | Volume of water to be pumped out of dry dock | m³ |
| 6 | $P = \dfrac{\rho g V}{1000 \times T}$ | Pump capacity (Power in kW); T = time to empty in seconds | kW |
| 7 | $Q = \dfrac{V}{T}$ | Discharge rate of pump | m³/s |
| 8 | $W = \rho g V$ | Total weight of water to be pumped | Newtons |
| 9 | $B_s = \dfrac{B_d - 2c}{1}$ | Bilge block spacing on dock floor | metres |
| 10 | $T_{alt} = \dfrac{W_s}{A_k}$ | Altitude block stress (T_alt); W_s = ship weight, A_k = block area | N/m² or kPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Dimensions Rule-of-Thumb (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - Length of dry dock $= 1.10 \times$ Length of largest ship
> - Breadth of dry dock $= 1.20 \times$ Breadth of largest ship
> - Depth over keel blocks $\geq 1.20$ m for working clearance

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Cochin Shipyard Limited (Kerala):** Operates the largest graving dock in India for shipbuilding.
> - **Mumbai Port Trust:** Houses multiple floating dry docks for vessel repair.
> - **Singapore, Rotterdam, Shanghai:** Globally renowned for ultra-large graving and floating dry docks serving VLCCs (Very Large Crude Carriers) up to **300,000 DWT**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Dry Dock Dimensions

> [!NOTE]
> A standard KTU 2024 numerical problem asks: **"Design the dimensions of a dry dock for a given ship size."** Let us derive each dimension rigorously.

### 3.1.1 Length of the Dry Dock ($L_d$)

**Given:** Length of largest ship $= L_s$ (metres)

**Step 1:** A ship requires working clearance at both bow and stern so that workers, scaffolding, and movement space are available.

**Step 2:** Per KTU code, the clearance at each end is $0.05L_s$.

**Step 3:** Therefore, total length is:

$$
L_d = L_s + 2 \times (0.05 \, L_s)
$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $L_s$:

$$
L_d = L_s \, (1 + 0.10) = 1.10 \, L_s
$$

**Final result:**

$$
L_d = 1.10 \, L_s
$$

### 3.1.2 Breadth of the Dry Dock ($B_d$)

**Given:** Breadth of largest ship $= B_s$ (metres)

**Step 1:** Side clearance is required for ship supports (side blocks, shores) and worker access.

**Step 2:** Per KTU code, the side clearance on each side is $0.10B_s$.

**Step 3:** Therefore, total breadth is:

$$
B_d = B_s + 2 \times (0.10 \, B_s)
$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $B_s$:

$$
B_d = B_s \, (1 + 0.20) = 1.20 \, B_s
$$

**Final result:**

$$
B_d = 1.20 \, B_s
$$

### 3.1.3 Depth of the Dry Dock ($D_d$)

**Step 1:** The depth of water required in the dock is determined by:

$$
D_w = D_s + h_f + h_c
$$

where:
- $D_s$ = Loaded draft of the ship (m)
- $h_f$ = Freeboard (distance from water surface to top of dock floor when ship is at maximum draft) — typically **0.5 m to 1.0 m**
- $h_c$ = Slab/floor construction thickness (typically **0.5 m to 1.0 m**)

**Step 2:** The total depth of the dry dock below the coping level is:

$$
D_d = D_w + h_t
$$

where $h_t$ = allowance for tidal variation (in impounded docks, $h_t = 0$).

**Final result:**

$$
D_d = D_s + h_f + h_c + h_t
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Numerical Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A dry dock is to be designed to accommodate a Panamax-class bulk carrier with the following particulars:
> - Length of ship, $L_s = 230$ m
> - Breadth of ship, $B_s = 32$ m
> - Loaded draft, $D_s = 12.5$ m
> - Freeboard, $h_f = 1.0$ m
> - Floor thickness, $h_c = 0.75$ m
> - Tidal allowance, $h_t = 0$ m (impounded dock)
> - Time to pump out, $T = 4$ hours

**Step 1 — Length of dry dock:**

$$
L_d = 1.10 \times L_s = 1.10 \times 230
$$

$$
L_d = 253 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Breadth of dry dock:**

$$
B_d = 1.20 \times B_s = 1.20 \times 32
$$

$$
B_d = 38.4 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Depth of water required:**

$$
D_w = D_s + h_f + h_c = 12.5 + 1.0 + 0.75
$$

$$
D_w = 14.25 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Total depth of dry dock:**

$$
D_d = D_w + h_t = 14.25 + 0
$$

$$
D_d = 14.25 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Volume of water to be pumped out:**

$$
V = L_d \times B_d \times D_w = 253 \times 38.4 \times 14.25
$$

$$
V = 138,\!427.20 \, \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 6 — Time to pump out in seconds:**

$$
T = 4 \, \text{hours} = 4 \times 3600 = 14,\!400 \, \text{s}
$$

**Step 7 — Required discharge rate:**

$$
Q = \dfrac{V}{T} = \dfrac{138,\!427.20}{14,\!400}
$$

$$
Q = 9.61 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 8 — Total weight of water:**

$$
W = \rho \times g \times V = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 138,\!427.20
$$

$$
W = 1.358 \times 10^9 \, \text{N}
$$

**Step 9 — Pump power required (assuming 75% efficiency):**

$$
P = \dfrac{W}{1000 \times T \times \eta} = \dfrac{1.358 \times 10^9}{1000 \times 14,\!400 \times 0.75}
$$

$$
P = 125.74 \, \text{kW}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Design Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|:----------|:------:|:-----:|
| Length of dock | $L_d$ | 253.00 m |
| Breadth of dock | $B_d$ | 38.40 m |
| Depth over keel | $D_w$ | 14.25 m |
| Total dock depth | $D_d$ | 14.25 m |
| Volume of water | $V$ | 1,38,427.20 m³ |
| Pump discharge | $Q$ | 9.61 m³/s |
| Pump power (75% eff.) | $P$ | 125.74 kW |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Components of a Graving Dock

> [!NOTE]
> The following components **must be memorised** for the KTU viva and Part A questions.

| Component | Function |
|:----------|:--------|
| **Entrance (Mouth)** | Opening through which the ship enters; closed by a caisson or gate |
| **Caisson / Gate** | Movable barrier to retain water; floated in/out or hinged |
| **Keel Blocks** | Vertical wooden/concrete blocks on the floor that support the ship's keel |
| **Side Blocks / Shores** | Inclined supports along the sides that prevent the ship from tipping |
| **Altar (Altimeter Steps)** | Stepped recesses on side walls to support shores at various heights |
| **Floor Slab** | Concrete base that resists uplift pressure from groundwater |
| **Side Walls** | Retain soil; provide dock support; contain altars |
| **Copings** | Top of the walls; provide working platform for cranes and traffic |
| **Pump Well / Sump** | Collects water seepage and rainwater for pumping out |
| **Cranes & Gantries** | Heavy-lift equipment for hull work and parts replacement |
| **Caisson Seat** | Recess in the dock floor and walls where the caisson rests when the dock is sealed |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Docks

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["DOCKS"] --> B["WET DOCKS"]
    A --> C["DRY DOCKS"]
    B --> B1["Impounded Basin with Lock Gate"]
    B --> B2["Tidal Basin"]
    C --> C1["GRAVING DOCK"]
    C --> C2["FLOATING DRY DOCK"]
    C --> C3["MARINE RAILWAY"]
    C --> C4["LIFT DOCK"]
    C1 --> C1A["Excavated Basin on Land"]
    C1 --> C1B["Closed by Caisson or Hinged Gate"]
    C2 --> C2A["Pontoon with Side Walls"]
    C2 --> C2B["Ballast Tanks for Submerging"]
    C3 --> C3A["Inclined Rail Track"]
    C3 --> C3B["Cradle Hauled by Winches"]
    C4 --> C4A["Vertical Hydraulic Lift"]
    C4 --> C4B["Platform Lifts Ship Out of Water"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Graving Dock

```mermaid
graph TD
    SHIP["SHIP ENTERING DOCK"] --> GATE1["OPEN CAISSON GATE"]
    GATE1 --> FLOAT["SHIP FLOATS INSIDE"]
    FLOAT --> DOCK1["DOCK FULL OF WATER"]
    DOCK1 --> CLOSE["CLOSE GATE"]
    CLOSE --> PUMP["PUMP OUT WATER"]
    PUMP --> EMPTY["WATER LEVEL DROPS"]
    EMPTY --> REST["SHIP RESTS ON KEEL BLOCKS"]
    REST --> WORK["HULL INSPECTION AND REPAIR"]
    WORK --> REFILL["REFILL WITH WATER"]
    REFILL --> OPEN2["OPEN GATE"]
    OPEN2 --> LEAVE["SHIP SAILS OUT"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Major Components of a Graving Dock

```mermaid
graph LR
    COPING["COPING TOP OF WALL"] --> SIDEWALL["SIDE WALLS"]
    SIDEWALL --> ALTAR["ALTAR STEPS"]
    SIDEWALL --> SEAT["CAISSON SEAT"]
    FLOOR["FLOOR SLAB"] --> KEEL["KEEL BLOCKS"]
    FLOOR --> SUMP["PUMP SUMP WELL"]
    SIDEWALL --> SHORES["SIDE SHORES"]
    CRANES["GANTRY CRANES"] --> COPING
    GATE["CAISSON OR GATE"] --> SEAT
```

## 4.4 Floating Dry Dock — Working Cycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    STEP1["STEP 1 SUBMERGE DOCK BY FILLING BALLAST TANKS"] --> STEP2
    STEP2["STEP 2 TOW SHIP INTO DOCK OVER KEEL BLOCKS"] --> STEP3
    STEP3["STEP 3 REMOVE BALLAST WATER DOCK RISES"] --> STEP4
    STEP4["STEP 4 SHIP IS LIFTED OUT OF WATER"] --> STEP5
    STEP5["STEP 5 REPAIR WORK ON HULL"] --> STEP6
    STEP6["STEP 6 REFILL BALLAST DOCK SUBMERGES"] --> STEP7
    STEP7["STEP 7 SHIP FLOATS OUT DOCK RECOVERS"]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Architecture — Graving vs Floating Dry Dock

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph GRAVING["GRAVING DOCK EXCAVATED BASIN"]
        G1["Fixed Concrete Basin"]
        G2["Ship Supported on Keel Blocks"]
        G3["Water Pumped Out"]
    end
    subgraph FLOATING["FLOATING DRY DOCK PONTOON"]
        F1["Movable Steel Pontoon"]
        F2["Ship Supported on Floating Pontoon"]
        F3["Ballast Controlled Submergence"]
    end

    FLOATING --> CMP
    CMP --> R1["Graving: Higher Capacity, Higher Cost"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**List any six essential functions of a dock.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Berthing of ships** — providing a safe mooring location.
> 2. **Loading and unloading of cargo** — via cranes and conveyors.
> 3. **Embarkation/disembarkation of passengers.**
> 4. **Ship repair and maintenance** — using dry docks.
> 5. **Refuge during storms** — using closed docks with lock gates.
> 6. **Warehousing and storage of goods** — using transit sheds and yards.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Differentiate between a wet dock and a dry dock in 5 lines.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> | Aspect | Wet Dock | Dry Dock |
> |:-------|:---------|:---------|
> | Water | Always full of water | Can be emptied |
> | Purpose | Berthing, loading/unloading | Hull repair, painting, construction |
> | Gate | Lock gate (keeps water level constant) | Caisson/gate (for pumping out) |
> | Ship Position | Floats always | Rests on keel blocks when empty |
> | Tide Effect | Independent of tide (impounded) | Can be tidal or impounded |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**(a) Classify docks in detail with neat sketches. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Docks:**
>
> **1. Wet Docks (Impounded Docks):**
> - Always filled with water
> - Equipped with lock gates to maintain constant water level
> - Used for berthing and cargo handling
> - Independent of tidal variations
>
> **2. Dry Docks:**
> - Water can be pumped out to expose the ship's hull
> - Used for inspection, repair, painting, and new ship construction
> - **Sub-types:**
>   - (i) **Graving Dock** — permanent excavated basin on land; closed by a caisson or hinged gate.
>   - (ii) **Floating Dry Dock** — a pontoon with side walls that can be submerged and refloated; used where permanent structures are not feasible.
>   - (iii) **Marine Railway (Syncrolift)** — inclined track with a wheeled cradle; ship is hauled up.
>   - (iv) **Lift Dock** — vertical hydraulic platform lifting the ship out of water.
>
> **Sketch (Block Diagram):**
> ```
> DOCKS
>   ├── WET DOCKS (Lock Gate)
>   └── DRY DOCKS
>         ├── GRAVING DOCK (Caisson)
>         ├── FLOATING DRY DOCK (Pontoon + Ballast)
>         ├── MARINE RAILWAY (Inclined Rails + Cradle)
>         └── LIFT DOCK (Hydraulic Platform)
> ```

**(b) Explain the functions of a dock in detail. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Functions of a Dock:**
>
> 1. **Berthing and Mooring:** Provides a safe quay or jetty for ships to moor and remain stationary.
> 2. **Loading and Unloading of Cargo:** Cranes, gantries, and conveyor systems allow transfer of bulk, break-bulk, and containerised cargo.
> 3. **Passenger Traffic:** Passenger docks have terminal buildings, customs, immigration, and waiting halls.
> 4. **Ship Repair and Maintenance:** Dry docks allow underwater hull inspection, cleaning, welding, and painting.
> 5. **Ship Construction:** Building docks are used to construct vessels from the keel up.
> 6. **Refuge During Adverse Weather:** Closed docks offer protection from waves, currents, and storms.
> 7. **Bunkering and Supply:** Refuelling, freshwater, and provision supply to ships.
> 8. **Warehousing:** Transit sheds, container yards, and bonded warehouses store cargo before/after shipment.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the working of a graving dock. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Graving Dock (Definition):**
> A graving dock is an excavated rectangular basin on land, provided with a water-tight entrance closed by a caisson or hinged gate, and equipped with keel blocks and side shores to support a ship when the water is pumped out.
>
> **Working Sequence:**
>
> 1. **Step 1 — Docking:** The dock is filled with water to harbour level. The caisson is floated out, and the ship is towed into the dock. The caisson is then positioned in its seat, sealing the entrance.
> 2. **Step 2 — De-watering:** Powerful pumps evacuate the water from the dock. As the water level drops, the ship settles gently on the keel blocks arranged along the dock floor.
> 3. **Step 3 — Shoring:** Side shores are wedged between the hull and the altars (stepped recesses) on the side walls, stabilising the ship against rolling or tipping.
> 4. **Step 4 — Repair Work:** The hull is exposed; workers perform cleaning, welding, painting, propeller maintenance, etc.
> 5. **Step 5 — Re-floating:** The dock is re-filled with water, shores are removed, the caisson is floated out, and the ship sails out.
>
> **Block Sketch of Graving Dock:**
> ```
>     COPING  GANTRY CRANE
>   |----------------------------------|
>   |  ALTAR   | SHIP  |    ALTAR      |
>   | STEPS    |  HULL |    STEPS      |
>   |          |  SIDE |               |
>   |          |  SHORE|               |
>   |__________| KEEL  |_______________|
>              |BLOCKS|
>   |  PUMP WELL (SUMP)            |
>   |________________________________|
>             CAISSON GATE
> ```

**(b) Design the dimensions of a dry dock for a ship of length 200 m, breadth 28 m, and draft 11.5 m. Freeboard = 1.0 m, floor thickness = 0.6 m, tidal allowance = 0, time to empty = 3 hours. Pump efficiency = 80%. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Length of dry dock:**
> $$L_d = 1.10 \times L_s = 1.10 \times 200 = 220 \text{ m}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Breadth of dry dock:**
> $$B_d = 1.20 \times B_s = 1.20 \times 28 = 33.6 \text{ m}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Depth of water required:**
> $$D_w = D_s + h_f + h_c = 11.5 + 1.0 + 0.6 = 13.1 \text{ m}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Total dock depth:**
> $$D_d = D_w + h_t = 13.1 + 0 = 13.1 \text{ m}$$
>
> **Step 5 — Volume of water:**
> $$V = L_d \times B_d \times D_w = 220 \times 33.6 \times 13.1 = 96,\!835.20 \text{ m}^3$$
>
> **Step 6 — Time in seconds:**
> $$T = 3 \times 3600 = 10,\!800 \text{ s}$$
>
> **Step 7 — Total weight of water:**
> $$W = \rho \, g \, V = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 96,\!835.20 = 9.50 \times 10^8 \text{ N}$$
>
> **Step 8 — Pump power required (80% efficiency):**
> $$P = \dfrac{W}{1000 \times T \times \eta} = \dfrac{9.50 \times 10^8}{1000 \times 10,\!800 \times 0.80} = 109.95 \text{ kW}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Harbour and Port:** Many students use these interchangeably. **Harbour = protected water; Port = commercial end with cargo/passenger facilities.** A port always includes a harbour, but a harbour may not always be a port.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Freeboard ($h_f$) and Floor Thickness ($h_c$):** These two terms are frequently omitted in $D_w$ calculations, leading to incorrect dock depth. **Always add both.**
>
> 3. **Wrong Multiplier for Length and Breadth:** Use $1.10$ for length and $1.20$ for breadth — mixing these up is a common error.
>
> 4. **Time Conversion Error:** Converting hours to seconds is missed by many students. Always write $T$ in **seconds** when computing pump power in kW.
>
> 5. **Neglecting Pump Efficiency ($\eta$):** The formula for pump power must include the efficiency factor. Using $\eta = 1$ (100%) is an over-design error.
>
> 6. **Sketch Quality:** In board exams, a neat labelled block diagram of a graving dock with **keel blocks, altars, caisson, side walls, sump, coping, and gantry crane** fetches full marks. A poor/missing sketch can cost 2-3 marks.
>
> 7. **Mixing Dry Dock and Floating Dock:** When asked about "types of dry docks", students often describe only the graving dock. **Floating, Marine Railway, and Lift Dock are equally important.**
>
> 8. **Units Mistake:** Volume in m³, Discharge in m³/s, Power in kW. Mixing units is a guaranteed mark-loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes for Last-Minute Reading:**
>
> - **Harbour:** Protected water area for safe ship anchorage.
> - **Port:** Commercial end of a harbour with cargo and passenger facilities.
> - **Dock:** Enclosed water space (wet) or empty space (dry) for berthing/repair.
> - **Wet Dock:** Always filled with water; uses lock gate; tide-independent.
> - **Dry Dock:** Can be pumped empty; used for hull repair and ship construction.
> - **Graving Dock:** Permanent excavated basin; closed by caisson or hinged gate.
> - **Floating Dry Dock:** Mobile pontoon; uses ballast tanks for submerging/lifting.
> - **Marine Railway:** Inclined rail track; cradle hauls ship out of water.
> - **Lift Dock:** Hydraulic vertical platform lifting the ship.
> - **Length formula:** $L_d = 1.10 \, L_s$
> - **Breadth formula:** $B_d = 1.20 \, B_s$
> - **Depth formula:** $D_w = D_s + h_f + h_c$
> - **Total depth:** $D_d = D_w + h_t$
> - **Volume of water:** $V = L_d \times B_d \times D_w$
> - **Pump discharge:** $Q = V / T$
> - **Pump power:** $P = \rho g V / (1000 \, T \, \eta)$ in kW
> - **Keel blocks:** Support ship on dock floor.
> - **Altars:** Stepped recesses for side shores.
> - **Caisson:** Movable gate closing the dock entrance.
> - **Sump/Pump well:** Collects seepage for pumping.
> - **Functions of dock:** 8 functions — berthing, cargo, passenger, repair, construction, refuge, bunkering, storage.
> - **KTU 2024 favourite topics:** Dock classification, dry dock components, numerical design of dry dock dimensions.
> - **Standard clearance:** 5% of $L_s$ at each end, 10% of $B_s$ on each side.
> - **Freeboard:** 0.5 m to 1.0 m working clearance below hull.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wet Docks. Tunnel Engineering: Tunnel - Sections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET502 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wet Docks. Tunnel Engineering: Tunnel - Sections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wet Docks — Formal Definition

A **Wet Dock** is an artificially enclosed basin of water, connected to the open sea or tidal harbour by means of a lock gate or caisson, designed to maintain a nearly constant water level so that ships can be berthed, loaded, and unloaded independent of the tidal fluctuations of the outer harbour.

In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology:
> A wet dock is a **dock impounding water at a uniform level**, separated from the tidal waters of the harbour by an **entrance provided with a lock or caisson**, used exclusively for the berthing, loading, and unloading of vessels.

Key standard metrics associated with wet docks:
- **Dock Water Level** is held at the level of **Mean High Water Springs (MHWS)** or **Mean High Water Neaps (MHWN)** as designed.
- **Freeboard** between the cope (top of dock wall) and water level is generally kept at **1.5 m to 2.0 m**.
- **Siltation rate** in well-maintained wet docks averages **0.3 m to 0.6 m per year** requiring periodic dredging.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Module 4 syllabus clearly distinguishes between *Wet Docks* (for cargo handling) and *Dry/Graving Docks* (for ship repair). A wet dock is essentially a **water-retention dock**, not a repair dock.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Floating Locked Parking Lot"

Imagine a giant rectangular **swimming pool** built next to the sea, but the pool has a **swinging gate** (lock) that only opens when ships come in or go out. Inside this pool, the water level never changes — whether the tide outside is high or low, inside it is always the same calm level. This is exactly what a wet dock does!

- The **outer harbour/tide** = a beach where water level keeps rising and falling.
- The **wet dock** = a permanently calm pool.
- The **lock gate** = the controlled doorway that lets ships in/out without disturbing the calm water.
- **Bollards, capstans, cranes, sheds** = the parking infrastructure that ties ships down, pulls them, lifts cargo, and stores goods.

This is why ships prefer wet docks — loading cranes need a **stable reference level** and **stable ship position**; a vessel that rises and falls 4 m with every tide (typical Kerala coast tidal range is **~1 m**, but global ports can be up to **6 m**) cannot be efficiently serviced.

## 1.3 Tunnel Engineering — Definition of "Tunnel Sections"

In KTU 2024 terminology, the **section** of a tunnel refers to the **shape and geometric profile of the cross-section** cut through the ground, together with the **internal lining geometry** that provides structural support, accommodates the traffic envelope, and integrates ventilation, drainage, lighting, and walkways.

> A tunnel section is the **transverse geometric configuration** (shape + lining) of a tunnel, optimized to:
> (a) resist the in-situ ground/water pressures,
> (b) accommodate the **gauge / clearance envelope** of the intended traffic, and
> (c) provide space for services (ventilation ducts, drainage, cable trays, walkways).

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hole in the Mountain that Survives"

Think of digging a horizontal hole in wet sand. A simple round hole collapses. A square hole also collapses at the corners. But a *carefully shaped* hole (round, egg-shaped, or D-shaped) with a thin concrete/metal **lining** (like a sleeve) inside stays open for 100 years. The **shape of the hole = the "section"** of the tunnel. The choice of shape is the engineer's way of telling the mountain: *"This is the only shape I will let you push against, and here is the sleeve that holds you back."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** A tunnel section is **never arbitrary** — it is selected as a function of:
> 1. The **in-situ stress field** of the surrounding ground (K₀ coefficient),
> 2. The **construction method** (NATM, TBM, cut-and-cover, immersed tube),
> 3. The **traffic gauge** (Broad Gauge 1676 mm / Standard Gauge 1435 mm / IRC vehicle envelope), and
> 4. The **internal pressure** (subaqueous tunnels must resist full hydrostatic head).

The **minimum internal clear dimension** for a single-track railway tunnel on Indian Railways is **3.35 m × 5.25 m** (modified to **3.66 m × 5.85 m** for 25 kV electrified traction with OHE).

The **minimum vertical clearance** for a road tunnel under IRC:SP:91 is generally **4.5 m to 5.0 m** for two-lane configuration.

## 1.5 Why These Two Topics Are Grouped Together in Module 4

Both wet docks and tunnels are **enclosed, water-managed transportation infrastructures**:
- A wet dock **holds water in** against tidal forces using locks and gates.
- A tunnel **holds water/ground out** using a lining of specific shape.

Both rely heavily on **hydrostatics, structural geometry, and ventilation/hydraulic management**, which is why they are co-located in Module 4 of the KTU Transportation Engineering syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Wet Dock — Structured Logic

A wet dock is not a single concrete box; it is a **system of interdependent components**, each fulfilling one engineering role:

| # | Component | Function | Design Criterion |
|---|-----------|----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Dock Walls** | Retain the soil + resist berthing impact | Designed for horizontal earth pressure + 100 T bollard pull |
| 2 | **Dock Floor / Apron** | Prevent uplift from sub-soil water | Designed against hydrostatic uplift (factor of safety ≥ 1.2) |
| 3 | **Entrance** | Ships enter/exit | Width = B + (1.5 to 3.0 m) margin; depth = max ship draft + 0.6 m |
| 4 | **Lock Chamber** | Holds water at constant level | Length = 1.1 × L; Width = 1.15 × B; Depth = D + 0.6 m |
| 5 | **Lock Gates / Caisson** | Close the entrance | Mitre gates (≤ 18 m span) or sliding caisson (> 18 m) |
| 6 | **Sluice Gates / Valves** | Fill/empty lock chamber | Discharge Q = C_d × A × √(2gH) |
| 7 | **Capstans & Bollards** | Warp ships into berth | Bollard pull capacity 30 T to 100 T |
| 8 | **Transit Sheds & Warehouses** | Short-term cargo storage | Plinth 1.5 m above dock level; floor area per berth = 1500–3000 m² |
| 9 | **Cranes & Railway Lines** | Cargo handling | Rail gauge: 1676 mm BG; apron width ≥ 20 m |
| 10 | **Dock Coping** | Top of dock wall | ≥ 1.5 m above HWL; kerb 1.0 m back from wall face |

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why this matters":** Every component is a *failure mode control*. The lock gate is a *leakage control*, the floor is an *uplift control*, the capstan is a *mooring control*. KTU questions test whether you understand the *why*, not just the *what*.

## 2.2 Types of Wet Docks (KTU Classification)

| Type | Distinguishing Feature | Typical Use |
|------|------------------------|-------------|
| **Impounded Wet Dock** | Water level maintained above LWS by lock/caisson | Cargo berths (Kochi, Mumbai) |
| **Open Dock** | Tidal — no impounding | Small fishing harbours |
| **Half-Tide Dock** | Water level held at half-tide level by gates | Intermediate berthing |
| **Entrance Lock Dock** | Lock-gated entrance | Modern major ports |
| **Tidal Basin** | Fills at high tide, empties at low | Specialised cargo |

## 2.3 Caissons — The Floating Gate of a Wet Dock

A caisson is a **movable watertight barrier** used to close the dock entrance. Three KTU-relevant types:

1. **Sliding Caisson** — rolls on rails in a recess; can be hauled aside; for entrances **> 18 m**.
2. **Floating Caisson** — a hollow steel box that is floated into position and sunk; can also be used as a dry dock gate.
3. **Box / Gate Caisson** — hinged and rotates like a door.

> **"How"** — A sliding caisson works by withdrawing into a side recess (the *caisson chamber*) perpendicular to the dock axis, allowing the lock to open. Its own weight is balanced by **ballast water tanks** that are filled before sinking into the sill.

## 2.4 Tunnel Sections — The Five Canonical Shapes

KTU 2024 Module 4 expects familiarity with the following standard tunnel sections, each tied to a ground condition:

| Shape | Cross-Section Description | Best Suited For | Why? |
|-------|---------------------------|------------------|------|
| **Circular** | Perfect circle | Soft ground, subaqueous, TBM-bored | Hoop stresses are pure compression — most efficient use of material |
| **D-Shape** | Flat invert + semicircular crown | Rock tunnels, railway | Inverts resist uplift; crown uses rock arch action |
| **Horse-Shoe** | Flat sides + arched crown | Railway/road in stratified rock | Convenient for placing invert and track |
| **Egg-Shape** | Narrower at top, wider at bottom (or vice versa) | Subaqueous tunnels, sewers | Better resistance to high external water pressure |
| **Rectangular** | Box section | Cut-and-cover shallow tunnels | Easy formwork; uses full road width |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of Shape Selection:** A circular section has **uniform bending stiffness in all directions (I = πR⁴/4)**. In soft ground with **isotropic in-situ stress (K₀ ≈ 1)**, only a circular section can convert the surrounding pressure into pure **hoop compression** with **zero bending moment** — this is the *minimum-material optimum* and the reason every modern metro and immersed tube tunnel is essentially circular or near-circular.

## 2.5 Components of a Tunnel Cross-Section (Standard Terminology)

Every tunnel section, regardless of shape, has the following geometric features that KTU questions frequently test:

| Term | Definition | KTU Numerical Range |
|------|------------|---------------------|
| **Crown** | Highest point of the tunnel section | Top of arch |
| **Haunches** | Curved transition between crown and side walls | Where bending moment peaks |
| **Springing Line** | Level where the arch meets the side wall (or invert arc starts) | Critical construction datum |
| **Invert** | Bottom of the tunnel | Carries track / road base |
| **Bench** | Side platform above invert (when full invert is not used) | Used for drainage + walkway |
| **Side Drains** | Longitudinal drains on both sides | Carry seepage water |
| **Clearance Envelope** | The 3-D space reserved for the moving vehicle | Defined by Railway Board / IRC |
| **Walkway** | Side maintenance passage | 0.75 m to 1.2 m wide |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table summarises **all formulas, dimensions, and design rules** that appear in KTU university exams for this topic. **Avoid using $\vert$ in cells — use $\mid$ instead.**

| # | Formula / Design Rule | Symbol Meaning | Typical KTU Numerical Value / Unit |
|---|----------------------|----------------|-------------------------------------|
| 1 | $W_{lock} = 1.15 \times B_{max}$ | Lock width, max ship beam | metres |
| 2 | $L_{lock} = 1.10 \times L_{max}$ | Lock length, max ship length | metres |
| 3 | $D_{lock} = D_{draft} + 0.6$ to $1.0 \;\text{m}$ | Lock depth, ship draft + keel clearance | metres |
| 4 | $W_{entrance} = B_{max} + 1.5$ to $3.0\;\text{m}$ | Dock entrance width | metres |
| 5 | $Q_{sluice} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | Discharge through filling sluice | m³/s; $C_d \approx 0.6$ |
| 6 | $T_{fill} = \dfrac{V_{lock}}{Q_{sluice}}$ | Time to fill the lock chamber | seconds or minutes |
| 7 | $V_{lock} = L_{lock} \times W_{lock} \times D_{lock}$ | Volume of water in lock chamber | m³ |
| 8 | $A_{tunnel} = \text{Area enclosed by section}$ | Cross-sectional area of tunnel | m² |
| 9 | $A_{clear} = A_{tunnel} - A_{services}$ | Net clearance area | m² |
| 10 | $R_{crown} \geq \dfrac{L_{clearance}^2}{8 h}$ | Minimum crown radius (flat-arch approx.) | metres |
| 11 | $A_{vent} = \dfrac{Q_{air}}{v_{air}}$ | Required ventilation duct area | m²; $v_{air} \approx 6$ to $10$ m/s |
| 12 | $Q_{air} = k \cdot N \cdot q_{per\,person}$ | Fresh air per person (road tunnel) | m³/s; $q \approx 0.05$ m³/s |
| 13 | $p_{hoop} = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{t}$ | Hoop stress in circular tunnel lining | N/mm² |
| 14 | $u_{uplift} = \gamma_w \cdot h_s$ | Hydrostatic uplift on dock floor | kN/m² |
| 15 | $FOS_{uplift} = \dfrac{W_{floor} + W_{filling}}{u_{uplift}}$ | Factor of safety against floor uplift | $\geq 1.2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These formulas are not academic. They are the **exact calculation set** used by:
> - **Cochin Port Authority** (Vallarpadam ICTT — International Container Transhipment Terminal),
> - **Konkan Railway** (10.6 km long Pir Panjal tunnel — uses horse-shoe section in the Himalayas),
> - **Delhi Metro** (circular TBM sections at ~5.2 m outer diameter),
> - **Mumbai Coastal Road Project** (immersed tube tunnel — rectangular sections).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lock Chamber Dimensions from Ship Geometry

This is the **single most frequently asked KTU derivation** for wet docks. The examiner expects you to know *why* the multipliers 1.10 and 1.15 appear.

### Given:
- Length of largest ship using the dock = $L_s$ (metres)
- Beam (breadth) of largest ship = $B_s$ (metres)
- Loaded draft of largest ship = $D_s$ (metres)
- Standard safe clearance = $\Delta$ (metres)

### To Derive:
Length $L_c$, width $W_c$, and depth $D_c$ of the lock chamber.

### Step 1 — Lock Length

A ship entering a lock must stop fully inside the chamber without striking either gate. The minimum chamber length is therefore:

$$L_c \geq L_s + 2 \times (\text{clearance at each end})$$

The empirical KTU/Royal Institution of Naval Architects (RINA) recommendation is to add **10 % of ship length** as combined end-clearance:

$$L_c = L_s + 0.10 \cdot L_s = 1.10 \cdot L_s$$

**Conversion logic (textual):** "10 % of the ship length is split as 5 % forward clearance (for mooring ropes and tug manoeuvring) and 5 % aft clearance (for the propeller wash and gate-seal tolerance)."

### Step 2 — Lock Width

Similarly, the chamber must accommodate the beam with a side clearance of 7.5 % per side:

$$W_c = B_s + 2 \times \left(\frac{0.15 \cdot B_s}{2}\right) = 1.15 \cdot B_s$$

**Logic:** A ship of 32 m beam requires 4.8 m of total side clearance, i.e., 2.4 m on each side — sufficient for fender compression, mooring-line sweep, and small drift.

### Step 3 — Lock Depth

The chamber floor must lie below the deepest loaded keel plus a safety margin. The standard KTU expression is:

$$D_c = D_s + \Delta_k, \quad \text{where } \Delta_k = 0.6 \text{ to } 1.0\;\text{m}$$

**Logic:** A ship with 14 m loaded draft requires the chamber floor to be at least 14.6 m below the impounded water level. The 0.6 m margin allows for **squat effect** (ship sinking lower when moving slowly in shallow water) and **siltation** between dredging cycles.

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> A bulk carrier of length $L_s = 280$ m, beam $B_s = 42$ m, and loaded draft $D_s = 14.5$ m is to be handled at a new wet dock. Calculate the minimum dimensions of the lock chamber.

**Solution (with all algebraic steps):**

$$L_c = 1.10 \times L_s = 1.10 \times 280 = 308 \;\text{m}$$

$$W_c = 1.15 \times B_s = 1.15 \times 42 = 48.30 \;\text{m}$$

$$D_c = D_s + 0.6 = 14.5 + 0.6 = 15.10 \;\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{L_c = 308 \;\text{m}, \quad W_c = 48.3 \;\text{m}, \quad D_c = 15.1 \;\text{m}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time to Fill a Lock Chamber

A second-tier KTU question tests the *operational hydraulics* of a lock.

### Given:
- Lock chamber volume $V_c = L_c \cdot W_c \cdot D_c$
- Sluice area $A_s$
- Head difference between outer harbour and dock water levels = $H$
- Discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.6$

### To Derive:
Time $T$ to fill the lock chamber to impounded level.

### Step 1 — Instantaneous Discharge

For an orifice under varying head, the instantaneous discharge is:

$$Q(t) = C_d \cdot A_s \cdot \sqrt{2g \cdot h(t)}$$

where $h(t)$ is the instantaneous head difference at time $t$.

### Step 2 — Volume Balance

The change in lock-chamber volume in time $dt$ is:

$$dV = -A_c \cdot dh = Q(t) \cdot dt$$

where $A_c = L_c \cdot W_c$ is the horizontal cross-sectional area of the lock.

### Step 3 — Integrate from $h = H$ to $h = 0$

$$T = \int_{0}^{T} dt = \int_{H}^{0} \frac{A_c \cdot dh}{-C_d \cdot A_s \cdot \sqrt{2gh}}$$

$$T = \frac{A_c}{C_d \cdot A_s \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \int_{0}^{H} \frac{dh}{\sqrt{h}}$$

$$T = \frac{A_c}{C_d \cdot A_s \cdot \sqrt{2g}} \cdot \left[ 2\sqrt{h} \right]_{0}^{H}$$

$$\boxed{T = \frac{2 \cdot A_c \cdot \sqrt{H}}{C_d \cdot A_s \cdot \sqrt{2g}} = \frac{A_c \cdot \sqrt{2H}}{C_d \cdot A_s \cdot \sqrt{g}}}$$

### Numerical Example

> Lock chamber: $L_c = 308$ m, $W_c = 48.3$ m → $A_c = 14{,}876.4$ m²
> Two sluice openings each $3$ m × $3$ m → $A_s = 2 \times 9 = 18$ m²
> Head $H = 1.5$ m, $C_d = 0.6$, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

$$T = \frac{14876.4 \times \sqrt{2 \times 1.5}}{0.6 \times 18 \times \sqrt{9.81}}$$

$$T = \frac{14876.4 \times 1.7321}{0.6 \times 18 \times 3.1321} = \frac{25{,}762.2}{33.83}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 761.5 \;\text{s} \approx 12.7 \;\text{minutes}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hydrostatic Uplift and Dock Floor Thickness

A wet dock floor can be **blown up** by subsoil water pressure if it is too thin or too light.

### Given:
- Subsoil water table at original ground level.
- Dock floor thickness = $t_f$.
- Floor made of plain concrete, $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³.
- Depth of floor below impounded water level = $h_s$.

### Step 1 — Uplift Pressure

$$p_u = \gamma_w \cdot h_s \quad [\text{kN/m}^2]$$

### Step 2 — Weight of Floor

$$W_f = \gamma_c \cdot t_f \quad [\text{kN/m}^2]$$

### Step 3 — Factor of Safety

$$FOS = \frac{W_f}{p_u} = \frac{\gamma_c \cdot t_f}{\gamma_w \cdot h_s}$$

Solving for required floor thickness:

$$\boxed{t_f = \frac{FOS \cdot \gamma_w \cdot h_s}{\gamma_c}}$$

### Numerical Example

> $h_s = 8$ m, $\gamma_w = 10$ kN/m³, $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³, $FOS_{required} = 1.2$

$$t_f = \frac{1.2 \times 10 \times 8}{24} = \frac{96}{24} = 4.0 \;\text{m}$$

That is a **very thick floor** — which is why most modern wet docks use **anchor piles** or **relief wells** rather than relying on self-weight.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hoop Stress in a Circular Tunnel Lining

For a circular tunnel (TBM-bored) in soft ground, the dominant loading is **uniform radial pressure $p$** from the surrounding ground + groundwater.

### Step 1 — Free-Body Cut

Cut the circular lining with a vertical diametral plane. The half-ring is loaded by uniform pressure $p$ on the outside, resisted by hoop forces $T$ on the two cut faces.

### Step 2 — Vertical Equilibrium

The vertical component of the external pressure on the half-ring must equal the vertical components of the two hoop forces:

$$2T = p \cdot 2r$$

$$T = p \cdot r$$

### Step 3 — Hoop Stress

$$T = \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot r}{t}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot r}{t}}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example

> Circular metro tunnel, outer radius $r = 2.85$ m, lining thickness $t = 0.30$ m, ground + water pressure $p = 350$ kN/m² = 0.35 N/mm²

$$\sigma_h = \frac{0.35 \times 2850}{300} = 3.325 \;\text{N/mm}^2 \approx 3.33 \;\text{MPa}$$

This is well within M25 concrete lining capacity (allowable ≈ 8 MPa), confirming the safety of the section.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selecting a Tunnel Section

| Ground Condition | Construction Method | Recommended Section | Lining Material | Reasoning (Why) |
|------------------|----------------------|---------------------|------------------|------------------|
| Soft alluvium, water-table high | Slurry Shield TBM | Circular | Precast concrete segments (0.30 m) | Hoop compression only |
| Hard rock, low stress | Drill & Blast | Horse-shoe | Shotcrete + rock bolts | Rock arch carries the load |
| Stratified sedimentary rock | NATM | D-Shape | Cast-in-situ concrete | Better stress redistribution |
| Subaqueous (river/sea bed) | Immersed Tube | Rectangular | Reinforced concrete | Fabrication in dry dock → float → sink |
| Sewer in soft clay | Microtunneling | Circular (ø 1.2 m to 3.0 m) | GRP / RCP jacking pipes | Smallest cross-section for the flow |

## 3.6 Master Equation Cheat (Tunnel Sections)

The following are the **master geometric relations** for the three most-tested KTU sections:

**For a circular section of outer radius $R_o$ and inner radius $R_i$:**

$$A_{section} = \pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$$

**For a D-section of width $B$ and total height $H$ with crown radius $R$:**

$$A_{D} = B \cdot (H - R) + \frac{\pi R^2}{2}$$

**For a horse-shoe of span $S$ and rise $h$ (semi-circular crown + straight sides of length $l$):**

$$A_{HS} = \frac{\pi S^2}{8} + 2 \cdot l \cdot \frac{S}{2} = \frac{\pi S^2}{8} + l \cdot S$$

Each of these is a **direct KTU 14-mark candidate** when combined with a clearance-envelope overlay problem.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Differentiate between a wet dock and a dry dock.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Wet Dock | Dry Dock |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| Primary purpose | Berthing, loading, unloading of ships | Inspection, cleaning, repair of ships |
| Water level | Filled with water (impounded) at constant level | Emptied of water for the ship to rest on blocks |
| Gate/Lock | Lock or caisson at entrance | Caisson or flap gate at one end |
| Floor | Submerged | Dry, with keel blocks and bilge blocks |
| Example | Cochin Port cargo berths | Cochin Shipyard graving dock |

**(1 mark)** for the purpose statement, **(1 mark)** for water-level distinction, **(1 mark)** for a clear tabular comparison.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**List any three standard shapes of tunnel cross-sections and state one condition where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Circular** — preferred in **soft ground with high water table** and **TBM construction** (1 mark)
2. **D-Shape** — preferred in **stratified rock with NATM** (1 mark)
3. **Rectangular** — preferred in **shallow urban cut-and-cover tunnels** (1 mark)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Students must answer **either Question A or Question B** from each module-cluster.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Adapted]`
**(a)** Describe the **various components of a wet dock** with a neat sketch. Explain the function of a **caisson** and list its types. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier of length $L_s = 260$ m, beam $B_s = 38$ m, and loaded draft $D_s = 13.5$ m is to be handled at a proposed wet dock. Determine **(i)** the lock chamber dimensions, **(ii)** the dock entrance width, and **(iii)** the volume of water required to **fill the lock chamber** in one operation. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Components of a Wet Dock (7 marks)

**List of components (1 mark each, up to 4 marks):**

1. **Dock Walls** — gravity, sheet-pile, or diaphragm type, retaining earth and resisting berthing impact loads.
2. **Dock Floor** — concrete slab, designed against **hydrostatic uplift** from sub-soil water; often anchored with piles.
3. **Entrance and Sill** — opening through which ships enter; sill submerged to design depth.
4. **Lock Gates or Caisson** — closure device for the entrance.
5. **Lock Chamber** — water-filled chamber between two gates; allows level equalisation.
6. **Sluice Gates / Valves** — fill/empty the lock.
7. **Capstans, Bollards, Fenders** — mooring and impact-absorption hardware.
8. **Transit Sheds, Warehouses, Cranes, Railway Sidings** — cargo handling infrastructure.
9. **Dock Coping** — top of wall, with kerb 1.0 m from face, 1.5 m above impounded water level.

**Caisson — function (1.5 marks):** A caisson is a **movable watertight barrier** placed across the dock entrance to:
- Retain impounded water when the lock is closed.
- Allow ships to pass when removed/floated aside.
- Permit dry-docking operations when the dock is being maintained.

**Types of caisson (1.5 marks):**
1. **Sliding Caisson** — rolls on rails in a side recess.
2. **Floating Caisson** — hollow steel box floated in and sunk.
3. **Box / Gate Caisson** — hinged at the base, rotates like a door.
4. **Roller Caisson** — moves vertically on rollers (rarely used in modern ports).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $L_s = 260$ m, $B_s = 38$ m, $D_s = 13.5$ m.

**(i) Lock chamber dimensions (3 marks):**

$$L_c = 1.10 \times L_s = 1.10 \times 260 = \mathbf{286 \;\text{m}}$$

$$W_c = 1.15 \times B_s = 1.15 \times 38 = \mathbf{43.7 \;\text{m}}$$

$$D_c = D_s + 0.6 = 13.5 + 0.6 = \mathbf{14.1 \;\text{m}}$$

**(ii) Dock entrance width (2 marks):**

$$W_{entrance} = B_s + 2.0 = 38 + 2.0 = \mathbf{40.0 \;\text{m}}$$

(Using the mid-range of the 1.5–3.0 m clearance band.)

**(iii) Volume of water in lock chamber (2 marks):**

$$V_{lock} = L_c \times W_c \times D_c$$

$$V_{lock} = 286 \times 43.7 \times 14.1$$

$$V_{lock} = 286 \times 616.17 = 176{,}224.62 \;\text{m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V_{lock} \approx 1.76 \times 10^{5} \;\text{m}^3}$$

This volume represents the **fresh seawater** that must be impounded (or pumped) each time the lock is filled — a strong reason why modern locks minimise filling operations by using **saving basins**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Adapted]`
**(a)** Explain the **factors governing the selection of a tunnel cross-section**. Compare the suitability of **circular, D-shape, and horse-shoe** sections. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular metro tunnel is to be bored through soft alluvium. The outer diameter of the lining is $5.40$ m, the lining thickness is $0.30$ m, and the **uniform radial ground + water pressure** is $p = 0.32$ MPa. Determine **(i)** the **hoop stress** in the lining, and **(ii)** the **cross-sectional area of the lining**. Check whether the section is safe if M25 concrete (allowable hoop stress = 8 MPa) is used. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Factors Governing Tunnel Section Selection (7 marks)

**Factors (1 mark each × 4 = 4 marks):**

1. **Ground condition** — soft, stratified, or hard rock; water table.
2. **Depth of cover** — influences the magnitude of in-situ stress.
3. **Construction method** — TBM, NATM, cut-and-cover, immersed tube.
4. **Purpose / Gauge** — railway BG/MG, road IRC envelope, water supply, sewer.
5. **Internal pressure** (for subaqueous or pressure tunnels).
6. **Ventilation and services** — ducts, drainage, walkways.
7. **Cost and time** of construction.

**Comparison table (1 mark each row × 3 = 3 marks):**

| Section | Ground | Construction | Key Property |
|---------|--------|---------------|---------------|
| **Circular** | Soft, subaqueous, isotropic | TBM (slurry/EPB) | Pure hoop compression; no bending |
| **D-Shape** | Stratified rock, moderate cover | NATM | Flat invert for track; rock arch in crown |
| **Horse-Shoe** | Hard rock, drill-and-blast | Conventional | Convenient staging; clear invert |

---

#### Part (b) — Hoop Stress and Section Area (7 marks)

**Given:** $D_o = 5.40$ m → $r_o = 2.70$ m; $t = 0.30$ m → $r_i = 2.40$ m; $p = 0.32$ MPa.

**(i) Hoop stress (3 marks):**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot r}{t} = \frac{0.32 \times 2.70}{0.30} = \frac{0.864}{0.30}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = 2.88 \;\text{MPa}}$$

**(ii) Cross-sectional area of lining (2 marks):**

$$A_{lining} = \pi (r_o^2 - r_i^2) = \pi (2.70^2 - 2.40^2)$$

$$A_{lining} = \pi (7.29 - 5.76) = \pi \times 1.53 = 4.806 \;\text{m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A_{lining} \approx 4.81 \;\text{m}^2}$$

**Safety check (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_h = 2.88 \;\text{MPa} \;\text{vs.}\; \sigma_{allow} = 8.00 \;\text{MPa}$$

$$FOS = \frac{8.00}{2.88} \approx 2.78 \geq 1.5 \;\text{(standard for tunnel linings)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Section is SAFE.}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes — KTU 2024 Exam:**
> 1. **Mistake:** Using 1.10 for **width** and 1.15 for **length** of the lock chamber.
>    **Reality:** It is the **opposite** — 1.10 × L (length) and 1.15 × B (beam). Examiners specifically test this swap.
> 2. **Mistake:** Forgetting to convert units in the hoop-stress formula (using $p$ in N/mm² but $r$ and $t$ in mm — this is correct, but mixing mm and m loses full marks).
> 3. **Mistake:** Reporting lock depth equal to ship draft, **without** the 0.6 m–1.0 m keel clearance.
> 4. **Mistake:** Listing a tunnel section shape without stating the **ground condition** it suits — KTU 2024 requires both to be paired in answers.
> 5. **Mistake:** Writing "caisson" generically without specifying **sliding / floating / box**, which costs 1.5 marks.
> 6. **Mistake:** Not stating the **freeboard** (1.5–2.0 m above impounded level) for dock coping — a frequently asked follow-up.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **wet dock** impounds water at a constant level using a **lock or caisson** at the entrance.
- Lock dimensions: $L_c = 1.10 L_s$, $W_c = 1.15 B_s$, $D_c = D_s + 0.6$ to $1.0$ m.
- Dock entrance width: $B_s + 1.5$ to $3.0$ m.
- Three main **caisson types**: **sliding, floating, box (gate)**.
- **Capstans** haul ships; **bollards** moore them; **fenders** absorb berthing impact.
- **Transit sheds** store cargo short-term; **warehouses** long-term.
- Sluice discharge: $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2gH}$; filling time $T = \dfrac{A_c \sqrt{2H}}{C_d A_s \sqrt{g}}$.
- Floor uplift check: $FOS = \dfrac{\gamma_c t_f}{\gamma_w h_s} \geq 1.2$.
- **Tunnel section** = shape + lining geometry selected for ground, depth, method, and gauge.
- **Circular** section → soft ground, TBM, subaqueous; pure hoop compression.
- **D-Shape** → stratified rock, NATM; flat invert for track.
- **Horse-shoe** → hard rock, drill-and-blast.
- **Rectangular** → cut-and-cover shallow or immersed tube.
- Tunnel cross-section parts: **crown, haunches, springing line, side walls, invert, bench, drains, walkway, ventilation duct, clearance envelope**.
- Hoop stress in circular lining: $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p r}{t}$.
- Lining area: $A = \pi (r_o^2 - r_i^2)$.
- Standard KTU numbers to memorise: **1.10** (length factor), **1.15** (beam factor), **0.6 m** (keel clearance), **1.5 m** (coping freeboard), **$C_d = 0.6$** (sluice coefficient).
- For M25 concrete lining, allowable hoop stress ≈ **8 MPa**; safe FOS ≥ **1.5**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tunnel Surveying - Alignment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET502 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tunnel Surveying - Alignment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Tunnel Surveying** is the specialized branch of civil engineering surveying that involves the precise determination, setting out, and control of the geometric alignment (both horizontal and vertical) of an underground passage, ensuring that excavation proceeds along a predetermined route to meet at a designated exit point within allowable engineering tolerances.

**Tunnel Alignment** specifically refers to the **three-dimensional geometric path** (plan + longitudinal profile + cross-section) along which the tunnel bore is driven, connecting the **portal (entrance)** to the **exit face** through the intervening rock/soil mass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes that tunnel alignment is a **multi-stage surveying operation** that bridges *surface geodetic control* with *underground excavation control* through shafts, adits, or inclined drifts.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to **drill a hole through a thick wall from one room to another without being able to see through it**. The drill bit must emerge *exactly* at a pre-marked spot on the opposite wall.

| Surface Phenomenon | Tunnel Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Marking start point on wall | Setting out **portal** coordinates |
| Marking target point on far wall | Establishing **exit face** coordinates |
| Drill angle adjustment | **Setting grade and direction** underground |
| Checking the drill's progress | **Transferring alignment** through shafts |
| Ensuring drill doesn't deviate | **Underground control traverse** |

The challenge is amplified because in tunnels, you cannot "see" your target — you must **mathematically project** the alignment from both ends (or through intermediate shafts) and hope they meet in the middle with sub-millimeter precision over kilometers of rock.

## 1.3 Why Tunnel Alignment is Critical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Tolerance in Modern Tunnels:**
> - Railway tunnels: ±50 mm lateral, ±25 mm vertical
> - Metro tunnels: ±25 mm lateral, ±15 mm vertical
> - Hydro tunnels: ±100 mm typically
> - **HTEM (High-Tolerance Excavation Method)** often demands ±10 mm

A misalignment of even a few centimetres can:
- Cause expensive **rework** or **redrilling**
- Induce **stress concentrations** in lining
- Lead to **flooding** in pressure tunnels
- Generate **drainage problems** in gravity-flow tunnels

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard minimum curve radius** in tunnels: **300 m to 600 m** (rail), **120 m to 200 m** (metro)
> - **Standard gauge clearance** for B.G. railway tunnel: **6.40 m × 4.88 m** (horseshoe shape)
> - **Standard laser wavelength** used in tunnel alignment: **He-Ne 632.8 nm** (visible red)
> - **Plumb line deflection per 100 m depth**: ≈ 1 mm (due to Earth's rotation, depends on latitude)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stages of Tunnel Surveying

The entire alignment process is broken into **five sequential stages**:

### Stage 1: Reconnaissance Survey
- **Goal:** Identify the general corridor
- **Tools:** Topographic maps, aerial photographs, satellite imagery
- **Output:** Preliminary alignment corridors (typically 2–3 alternatives)

### Stage 2: Preliminary Survey
- **Goal:** Compare alternatives on technical + economic merit
- **Tools:** Total station, GPS, geological mapping
- **Key outputs:**
  * Plan length L
  * Gradient G
  * Cross-section type
  * Tunnel cross-sectional area A
  * Volume of muck (excavated material) V
  * Number of working faces

### Stage 3: Detailed Surface Survey
- **Goal:** Establish **surface geodetic control network** above the tunnel
- **Tools:** Precise theodolites, total stations, EDMs, GNSS
- **Output:** Triangulation / traverse network with sub-centimetre accuracy

### Stage 4: Transfer of Alignment Underground
- **Goal:** Project alignment through shafts or adits into the tunnel
- **Methods:** Plumb lines, optical plummets, laser plummets, gyro-theodolites

### Stage 5: Underground Control & Driving
- **Goal:** Continuously guide excavation
- **Tools:** Tunnel lasers, jumbos with laser receivers, real-time GNSS-denied positioning

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Tunnel Alignment Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus lists the following governing factors:

| S.No | Factor | Influence on Alignment |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Geology & Rock type** | Avoid weak/fractured zones; favor stable rock |
| 2 | **Groundwater table** | Higher water table → deeper alignment |
| 3 | **Tunnel length** | Long tunnels → straighter alignment |
| 4 | **Gradient** | Gravity flow vs pressure flow |
| 5 | **Construction method** | TBM-friendly = gentle curves |
| 6 | **Approach grades** | Connecting to existing routes |
| 7 | **Cost** | Shorter alignment vs geological safety trade-off |
| 8 | **Ventilation** | Long tunnels need intermediate access |
| 9 | **Environmental impact** | Avoid disturbing sensitive areas |
| 10 | **Existing utilities** | Avoid interference with other infrastructure |

## 2.3 Shape and Size Considerations

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Tunnel Cross-Sections:**

| Section Type | Use Case | Advantages |
|---|---|---|
| **Circular** | Water/pressure tunnels | Best stress distribution |
| **Horseshoe** | Rail/road tunnels | Max useful area, easy lining |
| **D-shaped** | Hydro tunnels with steel lining | Smooth hydraulic flow |
| **Egg-shaped** | Sewer tunnels | Better flow at low discharge |
| **Rectangular** | Cut-and-cover metro | Easy construction, equipment fit |

**Design dimensions** must accommodate the **kinematic envelope** + **clearances** + **walkways** + **drainage** + **ventilation ducts**.

## 2.4 Surface Control Network for Tunnels

The surface survey establishes a **high-precision traverse/triangulation network** with:
- **Two triangulation stations** (one near each portal) connected by a long baseline
- **Azimuth** determined by **astronomical observations** or **gyro-theodolite**
- **Elevation** transferred by **precise levelling**

**Azimuth Control (Astronomical Method):**

$$\tan A = \frac{\sin t}{\sin\phi \cos t - \cos\phi \tan\delta}$$

Where:
- $A$ = Azimuth of the star at elongation
- $t$ = Hour angle of the star
- $\phi$ = Latitude of the observation station
- $\delta$ = Declination of the star

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **hour angle t** is the angle measured at the celestial pole between the observer's meridian and the star's hour circle, expressed in time (1 hour = 15°).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tan A = \dfrac{\sin t}{\sin\phi \cos t - \cos\phi \tan\delta}$ | Azimuth at elongation | Astronomical azimuth |
| 2 | $V = A \times L$ | Muck volume (uniform section) | Quantity estimation |
| 3 | $V = \dfrac{(A_1 + A_2)}{2} \times L$ | Volume (varying section) | End-area method |
| 4 | $\Delta_{plumb} = \dfrac{\omega^2 R h^2}{2gR_e}\sin(2\phi)$ | Plumb-line deflection | Shaft plumbing |
| 5 | $e = \dfrac{L}{2R}$ | Versine for chord L, radius R | Curve setting |
| 6 | $\theta_{LA} = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{R^2 + R^2 - d^2}{2R^2}\right)$ | Long chord to arc conversion | Setting out curves |
| 7 | $h_{loss} = h_f + h_e$ | Head loss (friction + entry) | Hydraulic tunnels |
| 8 | $L_{tunnel} \propto \dfrac{1}{\sin\theta}$ | Length vs gradient θ | Longitudinal design |
| 9 | $T_R = R\tan(\Delta/2)$ | Tangent length for curve Δ | Curve elements |
| 10 | $M = R\!\left[1 - \cos(\Delta/2)\right]$ | Middle ordinate | Vertical clearance |

**Boundary conditions and units:**
- All lengths in **metres (m)**
- Angles in **radians** for trigonometric functions, **degrees** for field use
- Earth rotation $\omega = 7.292 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s
- Earth's radius $R_e = 6371$ km
- Gravity $g = 9.81$ m/s²

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern tunnel surveying uses **Gyrotheodolites** that measure true north with **±3 arc-second** accuracy underground (where magnetic compasses fail due to steel reinforcement and geomagnetic disturbances). This is critical for **Trans-Himalayan tunnels**, **metro corridors** in cities, and **hydro-electric headrace tunnels** where even a 0.001° azimuth error can translate to **metres** of misalignment over a few kilometres.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Azimuth of a Star at Elongation

### Given
- Latitude of observer: $\phi$
- Declination of star: $\delta$
- Hour angle at elongation: $t_e$

### Astronomical Background
A star is at **greatest elongation** when the vertical circle through the star is tangent to the star's diurnal circle. At this instant, the star's azimuth is stationary — making it the most precise moment to measure azimuth.

### Derivation

From the **astronomical triangle** (Pole P, Zenith Z, Star S), apply the **sine rule**:

$$\frac{\sin A}{\sin(90°-\delta)} = \frac{\sin(90°-h)}{\sin PZS}$$

For elongation, the angle at the star $PZS = 90°$. The altitude $h$ becomes the **co-altitude** of the zenith distance.

The fundamental relationship in the PZS triangle is:

$$\sin\delta = \sin\phi \sin h - \cos\phi \cos h \cos A$$

Differentiating w.r.t. time t (for stationary azimuth at elongation, $\dfrac{dA}{dt} = 0$):

$$0 = \sin\phi \cos h \frac{dh}{dt} + \cos\phi \sin h \cos A \frac{dh}{dt}$$

Dividing by $\dfrac{dh}{dt}$:

$$0 = \sin\phi \cos h + \cos\phi \sin h \cos A$$

Solving for $\cos A$:

$$\cos A = -\frac{\sin\phi \cos h}{\cos\phi \sin h} = -\tan\phi \cot h$$

Now using the **co-function identity** $\cos A = \sin(90°-A)$ and applying to the PZS triangle via the sine rule:

$$\frac{\sin A}{\cos\delta} = \frac{\sin(180°-PZS)}{\cos\phi}$$

With $PZS = 90°$:

$$\sin A = \frac{\cos\delta}{\cos\phi}$$

Substituting into the standard astronomical formula using the **hour angle t**:

$$\tan A = \frac{\sin t}{\sin\phi \cos t - \cos\phi \tan\delta}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\tan A = \frac{\sin t}{\sin\phi \cos t - \cos\phi \tan\delta}}$$

This gives the true azimuth of the star at any observed hour angle $t$, which is the basis for fixing the **true meridian** at surface control stations for the tunnel.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Plumb-Line Deflection in a Vertical Shaft

### Given
- Shaft depth: $h$
- Observer latitude: $\phi$
- Earth's rotation rate: $\omega = 7.292 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s
- Earth's radius: $R_e = 6371$ km

### Physical Setup
A plumb line in a deep shaft is deflected from the true vertical by the **Coriolis effect** (apparent) and the **direction of gravity** (true). The total deflection $\Delta$ must be known to correct shaft plumbing.

### Step 1: Effective gravity
The resultant of gravitational and centrifugal acceleration gives the **effective vertical**. Its deviation from the Earth's axis is:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{\omega^2 R_e \sin\phi \cos\phi}{g - \omega^2 R_e \cos^2\phi}$$

### Step 2: For typical latitudes, $\omega^2 R_e \approx 0.034$ m/s² ≪ g, so:

$$\tan\alpha \approx \frac{\omega^2 R_e \sin(2\phi)}{2g}$$

### Step 3: The plumb line at depth h
At depth $h$ below the surface, the centrifugal component is reduced. A first-order Taylor expansion yields the **differential deflection** at depth $h$ relative to the surface:

$$\Delta_{plumb} = \frac{\omega^2 R_e h^2 \sin(2\phi)}{2gR_e} = \frac{\omega^2 h^2 \sin(2\phi)}{2g}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\Delta_{plumb} = \frac{\omega^2 h^2 \sin(2\phi)}{2g}}$$

### Numerical Example
For a tunnel shaft at $\phi = 30°$ N, depth $h = 500$ m:

**Step 1:** Compute $\omega^2$

$$\omega^2 = (7.292 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 5.317 \times 10^{-9} \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\sin(2\phi)$

$$\sin(60°) = 0.8660$$

**Step 3:** Substitute

$$\Delta = \frac{(5.317 \times 10^{-9})(500)^2(0.8660)}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$\Delta = \frac{(5.317 \times 10^{-9})(250000)(0.8660)}{19.62}$$

$$\Delta = \frac{1.1514 \times 10^{-3}}{19.62}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta \approx 5.87 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad} = 12.1 \text{ arc-seconds}}$$

This ~12″ deflection is **measurable and significant** for high-precision tunnel work.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Volume of Muck from Tunnel Excavation

### Given
- Cross-section area at start: $A_1$
- Cross-section area at end: $A_2$
- Length of tunnel segment: $L$
- Cross-section varies linearly between stations

### Step 1: End-Area Method
For a **truncated prism** (frustum-shaped excavation segment):

$$V = \int_0^L A(x) \, dx$$

With $A(x) = A_1 + \dfrac{(A_2 - A_1)}{L} \cdot x$:

$$V = \int_0^L \left[ A_1 + \frac{(A_2 - A_1)}{L} x \right] dx$$

### Step 2: Integrate

$$V = A_1 L + \frac{(A_2 - A_1)}{L} \cdot \frac{x^2}{2} \bigg|_0^L$$

### Step 3: Evaluate at bounds

$$V = A_1 L + \frac{(A_2 - A_1)}{L} \cdot \frac{L^2}{2}$$

$$V = A_1 L + \frac{(A_2 - A_1)L}{2}$$

$$V = \frac{2A_1 L + (A_2 - A_1)L}{2}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{V = \frac{(A_1 + A_2)}{2} \times L}$$

This is the **average end-area method** — the standard formula for tunnel muck volume estimation in KTU examinations.

### Numerical Example
A 200 m tunnel segment has $A_1 = 60$ m² at the portal and $A_2 = 80$ m² at the exit (widening for clearance). **Step 1:** Sum areas.

$$A_1 + A_2 = 60 + 80 = 140 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Take average.

$$\bar{A} = \frac{140}{2} = 70 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by length.

$$V = 70 \times 200 = 14{,}000 \text{ m}^3$$

For a **bulking factor of 1.5** (broken rock expands), the **loose volume to dispose** = 21,000 m³.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Alignment Transfer Methods

| Method | Accuracy | Best For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plumb line + wire** | ±5 mm/100 m | Shallow shafts (<100 m) | Wire oscillation in air |
| **Optical plummet** | ±1 mm/30 m | Medium-depth shafts | Line of sight obstruction |
| **Laser plummet** | ±0.5 mm/100 m | Deep shafts | Refraction in air columns |
| **Gyro-theodolite** | ±3″ azimuth | Long tunnels | Time-consuming; needs calibration |
| **Total station traverse** | ±2 mm + 2 ppm | Standard metro tunnels | Angular accumulation error |
| **Real-time GPS** | Not applicable underground | Surface control only | Signal blocked by rock |
| **Tunnel laser + auto-target** | ±3 mm/100 m | Active excavation guidance | Initial setup critical |
| **Inertial navigation** | ±0.05% of distance | Emergency / reconnaissance | Drift accumulation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **KTU exam purposes**, the most commonly tested method is **plumb-line plumbing** with correction for plumb-line deflection.

## 3.5 Curve Setting in Tunnels

When the tunnel alignment includes a horizontal curve, the surveyor must set out the curve underground using **chord-and-offset** or **deflection-angle** methods, since tape lengths are constrained by the tunnel width.

### Chord-Offset Method

For a curve of radius $R$ and central angle $\Delta$:

$$T_R = R \tan(\Delta/2) \quad \text{(tangent length)}$$

$$C = 2R\sin(\Delta/2) \quad \text{(chord length)}$$

$$M = R[1 - \cos(\Delta/2)] \quad \text{(middle ordinate)}$$

### Setting at mid-tunnel
The middle ordinate $M$ is **laid off perpendicular** to the chord at its midpoint, **toward the centre of the curve**. This requires only short measurements — ideal in the confined tunnel space.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tunnel Surveying Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Surface Control Network Establishment] --> A1[Establish Triangulation Stations]
    A0 --> A2[Precise Levelling Network]
    A0 --> A3[Azimuth Determination - Astronomical or Gyro]
    A1 --> B0[Underground Alignment Transfer]
    A2 --> B0
    A3 --> B0
    B0 --> B1[Shaft Construction]
    B0 --> B2[Adits or Inclined Drifts]
    B0 --> B3[Direct Portal Drive]
    B1 --> C1[Plumb Line Method]
    B1 --> C2[Optical Plummet Method]
    B1 --> C3[Laser Plummet Method]
    B2 --> C4[Traverse Extension from Adit]
    B3 --> C5[Direct Traverse from Portal]
    C1 --> D0[Underground Control Network]
    C2 --> D0
    C3 --> D0
    C4 --> D0
    C5 --> D0
    D0 --> D1[Centre Line Staking in Tunnel]
    D0 --> D2[Grade Markers on Invert]
    D0 --> D3[Cross-Section Profile Marks]
    D1 --> E0[Active Excavation Guidance]
    D2 --> E0
    D3 --> E0
    E0 --> E1[Tunnel Laser Beam]

    E0 --> E3[Real-Time Profile Check]
    E1 --> F0[Continuous Monitoring and Verification]
    E2 --> F0
    E3 --> F0
    F0 --> G0[Final Lining Survey]
    G0 --> H0[As-Built Documentation]
    H0 --> I0[Project Completion]
```

## 4.2 Alignment Transfer Through Vertical Shaft

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Surface Triangulation Station TS1] -->|Plumb Wires| B[Shaft Top Bracket]
    B -->|Two Plumb Lines| C[Shaft Bottom]

    D --> E[Tunnel Centre Line TC1]
    E --> F[Working Face WF1]
    A2[Surface Station TS2 at Exit Portal] --> G[Direct Sight]
    G --> F
    F -->|Meeting Point| H[Breakthrough Verification]
```

## 4.3 Tunnel Cross-Section Reference Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Standard Railway Tunnel Cross-Section BG] --> A1[Top Heading Excavation]
    A0 --> A2[Bench Excavation]

    A0 --> A5[Waterproof Membrane]
    A0 --> A6[Final Concrete Lining]
    A0 --> A7[Walkway and Drainage Channel]
    A0 --> A8[Ventilation Duct and Cable Tray]
    A0 --> A9[Catenary for Electric Traction]

```

## 4.4 Horizontal Curve in Tunnel — Setting Out Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Tunnel Approach Tangent] --> A1[Point of Curvature PC]
    A1 --> A2[Tangent Length TR]
    A2 --> A3[Point of Tangent PT]
    A3 --> A4[Tunnel Exit Tangent]
    A1 --> B0[Chord 1 of Length C1]
    B0 --> B1[Point 1 on Curve]
    B1 --> B2[Chord 2 of Length C2]
    B2 --> B3[Point 2 on Curve]
    B3 --> B4[Chord n]
    B4 --> B5[Continue to PT]
    A0 --> C0[Middle Ordinate M laid off Perpendicular to Chord at Midpoint]
    C0 --> C1[Offset toward Curve Centre]
```

## 4.5 Error Propagation in Tunnel Alignment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Sources of Error in Tunnel Survey] --> A1[Angular Misclosure]
    A0 --> A2[Linear Measurement Error]

    A0 --> A5[Instrument Centring Error]
    A0 --> A6[Target Sighting Error]

    A2 --> B0
    A3 --> B0
    A4 --> B0
    A5 --> B0
    A6 --> B0
    B0 --> C0[Acceptable if within ±50 mm for Rail Tunnels]
    B0 --> D0[Redrive Required if Exceeds Tolerance]
    B0 --> E0[Statistical Adjustment by Least Squares Network]
```

## 4.6 Modern Laser-Guided Tunnel Boring Machine (TBM) Control

```mermaid
    A1 --> A2[Laser Target on TBM Shield]

    A3 --> A4[Real-Time Computer]
    A4 --> A5[Display to Operator]
    A4 --> A6[Steering Correction to TBM]
    A5 --> A7[Continuous Display of Lateral and Vertical Offset]
    A6 --> A8[Hydraulic Jack Adjustment]
    A8 --> A9[TBM Path Correction]
    A9 --> B0[Continuous Traverse Check from Underground Control]
    B0 --> A4
```
